philosophy: Boros Energy is an explosive aggro-ramp deck that generates energy counters to cast Phlage, Titan of Fire's Fury, leveraging its massive haste and board-wiping ETB effect to close games quickly. It excels at punishing slow, reactive decks and outpacing combo strategies through relentless pressure and high-impact threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one Plains and one Mountain), at least one energy generator, and a turn-2 play. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands with only removal/Phlage and no energy generation.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast an energy generator. Turn 2: Play a second land, generate energy, and cast Ragavan, Ocelot Pride, or attempt Phlage if you have 5+ energy. Prioritize playing lands first, then energy engines, then threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Affinity: +1 Artifact Hate, +1 Red Elemental Blast, -2 Ocelot Pride. vs Ruby Storm: +1 Red Elemental Blast, +1 Surgical Extraction, -2 Arcbound Worker. vs Jeskai Blink: +1 Disenchant, +1 Red Elemental Blast, -2 Ocelot Pride. vs Mirror: +1 Disenchant, +1 Red Elemental Blast, -2 Arcbound Worker.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoarding energy counters until you can cast Phlage or clear a board. Use Ragavan's ability to dig for lands, energy generators, or answers. Avoid spending energy on marginal spells when Phlage is your win condition.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough energy to cast Phlage or protect your board with Ocelot Pride. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal; use Ragavan to dig for answers or stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers (like Wrath of God), and artifact hate. Note when opponents tap out for removal or board wipes, as this is your window to cast Phlage or swing with Ocelot Pride.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Phlage, Titan of Fire's Fury, and Ocelot Pride with combat tricks. Cast Phlage to deal 5 damage and swing for 6, or use Ocelot Pride to close out games with +1/+1 counters and trample/flying.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 5+ energy for Phlage, or when the opponent is at 5 or less life and you can swing with multiple threats. Commit when you've disrupted their plan and they have no sweepers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked combo deck with no interaction, if you're out of resources against a stabilized control deck, or if your hand is entirely dead removal/Phlage with no energy generation.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast Phlage at instant speed when they have no mana, use Ragavan to dig for answers, or rely on haste/flying to close before they can respond.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them with early threats, use spot removal on key ramp pieces, and use Phlage to clear their board or swing past.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early removal to answer key threats, Ocelot Pride for combat tricks, and Phlage to swing past and close the game quickly.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early with Ragavan and Ocelot Pride, use Ragavan to dig past counterspells, and cast Phlage to break their board or close the game.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through superior sequencing, better resource management, and sideboard hate. Prioritize value engines and use Ragavan to out-draw your opponent.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your game plan to the matchup. Against artifact-heavy decks, bring in artifact hate and more removal. Against combo, add direct damage and graveyard hate. Against control, increase your threat density and protection. Always keep your core energy generators and Phlage, but adjust your removal and sideboard cards to target the specific threats in your opponent's deck.
notes: Energy management is critical; never spend energy on marginal spells when Phlage is your win condition. Ragavan's ability can be a double-edged sword, so use it to dig for answers or more Phlage, not just for damage. Ocelot Pride requires protection or combat tricks to be effective, so sequence it carefully. Watch for sweepers and have a plan to cast Phlage before or after they resolve.

philosophy: U/R Affinity is a fast, artifact-centric combo-aggro deck that leverages Mox Opal for explosive mana acceleration to cast Engineered Explosives for instant-speed board control and Weapons Manufacturing to generate overwhelming token armies or finish with high-power threats like Arcbound Ravager. It aims to outpace midrange and control strategies by applying relentless early pressure and resetting the board on favorable terms.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with Mox Opal plus two artifacts, or three artifacts with a playable land. Prioritize hands with Arcbound Ravager, Signal Pest, or turn-one artifact plays. Mulligan hands with three or more lands, no artifacts, or only single artifacts without a land. Aim for 4-5 artifact-producing/fixing lands (Shimmer Glen, Arcane Sanctum, Myr Meadow, Treetop Village) in your opening hand.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Arcbound Ravager or Signal Pest. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Mox Opal, or cast a second artifact. If you have Opal and an artifact, tap Opal for mana to cast another threat or cast Engineered Explosives. Sequence Opal as early as possible to enable explosive turns. If opening with Opal, play it on turn 1 or 2 and use it to accelerate into your game plan.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Amulet Titan (aggro/ramp): Bring in Spell Pierce, Flusterstorm, Surgical Extraction, and Rest in Peace. Against Ruby Storm/Jeskai Blink (control/combo): Bring in Engineered Explosives, Flusterstorm, Surgical Extraction, and Leyline of Punishment (if format-legal). Against Affinity mirror: Bring in Flusterstorm, Surgical Extraction, additional Engineered Explosives, and artifact hate. Remove basic lands, extra artifacts, or redundant interaction based on the matchup.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Mox Opal as your primary mana engine; tap it for one mana at a time to enable multiple spells per turn. Avoid overcommitting artifacts if you lack protection or follow-up. Use Engineered Explosives to clear boards when ahead or to stabilize when behind. Manage your graveyard carefully if facing discard or graveyard hate, and prioritize keeping key artifacts in play.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have Opal online to protect them or when you can follow up with removal. Hold back if the opponent has unanswered sweepers or hard artifact hate. Use Engineered Explosives to reset the board favorably, targeting the opponent's board when you're ahead or clearing blockers when you're behind. Never cast EE without considering the mana value target and your board state.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Flusterstorm, Spell Pierce, Mana Leak), board wipes (Swords to Plowshares, Wrath of God, Engineered Explosives), and artifact hate (Rest in Peace, Null Rod, Surgical Extraction, Leyline of Punishment). Track the opponent's hand size and graveyard to anticipate combo turns or sweepers. Note when they tap out for interaction, as this is your window to attack or cast key spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Arcbound Ravager combined with Mox Opal and Engineered Explosives for a lethal burst, or Weapons Manufacturing to generate a massive token army that overwhelms the board. If the format allows, Glimpse the Unthinkable with an artifact in hand can close the game. Otherwise, grind out value with artifact synergies and clear the board with EE to secure lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Mox Opal online, the opponent has no interaction in hand, and you have a clear path to lethal. Commit when you can protect your key artifacts or when Engineered Explosives can clear the board without hurting your own board. Also commit when the opponent is on a combo turn you can interrupt with your interaction suite.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you face a hard lock with no artifacts in play, no way to bypass Null Rod/Rest in Peace, or if the opponent is on a combo turn you cannot interrupt. Concede if you are down to one life with no artifacts, no interaction, and the opponent has a clear win condition. Also concede if you are consistently drawing dead hands against a resilient control or combo deck.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast spells in response to counterspells, use Mox Opal to protect key artifacts, and rely on Engineered Explosives to clear the board if your threats are countered. Use cheap artifacts to pressure through interaction, and consider casting spells when the opponent has no mana available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to prevent them from stabilizing. Use Engineered Explosives to clear ramp threats before they can cast their win conditions. Do not let them get ahead in mana; trade artifacts efficiently and use your interaction suite to disrupt their key spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Spell Pierce and Flusterstorm to interrupt key spells. Use Engineered Explosives to clear the board when threatened. Weapons Manufacturing can stabilize against aggressive decks by generating tokens. Do not overcommit to the board; hold back interaction for critical turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early with cheap artifacts and Opal acceleration. Use Engineered Explosives to clear blockers and stabilize. Force the control player to interact early, then capitalize on their mana shortage. Use your interaction suite to protect your key spells and close the game before they can set up their win conditions.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win on card advantage and better sequencing. Use Flusterstorm to protect your key spells and disrupt theirs. Use Engineered Explosives to clear boards favorably. Trade artifacts efficiently, and prioritize keeping Mox Opal online. Do not overcommit to the board; wait for the opponent to make a mistake or run out of resources.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your land count to match the matchup (e.g., reduce basics against control, increase artifact-producing lands against aggro). Prioritize bringing in artifact hate and interaction against control/combo, while focusing on disruption and board wipes against aggro. In the mirror, bring in more Flusterstorm and Surgical Extraction to win key interactions. Always keep Engineered Explosives and Mox Opal in your main deck, and adjust your artifact count based on how many spells you need to cast.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting artifacts without Opal protection, forgetting to tap Opal for mana, and mismanaging Engineered Explosives targets. Always track the opponent's hand for counterspells and sweepers. Unique interactions include using Opal to cast Engineered Explosives on the same turn you play it, and using Weapons Manufacturing to generate tokens that can be sacrificed for Arcbound Ravager's trample. Avoid casting EE when you have fewer artifacts than the opponent, as it can backfire. Practice sequencing Opal and artifacts to maximize your explosive turns.

philosophy: A three-color combo deck that accelerates mana and chains spells to cast a high number of spells in a single turn, winning via storm damage or storm-triggered win conditions. It aims to out-speed midrange and control archetypes while struggling against fast aggro and hard-countermagic strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably with fetch/land drop synergy), Ruby Medallion, Pyretic Ritual, and at least one cantrip or win condition. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no mana acceleration, or only win conditions without pieces to assemble the combo.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or ritual, and drop Ruby Medallion if possible. Turn 2: Chain rituals and cantrips to draw into your win condition or additional pieces. Prioritize fetching lands that enter untapped or using mana rocks to maintain tempo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro: Bring in counterspells, fast removal, and artifact hate; reduce combo pieces. Vs Control: Add more counters, protection spells, and additional cantrips. Vs Combo/Mirror: Include graveyard hate, additional win conditions, and hand disruption. Vs Amulet Titan/Affinity: Bring in artifact destruction and faster clock pieces.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a resource for storm count, but avoid unnecessary discards. Sequence rituals to maximize mana while preserving cards for the storm turn. Keep cantrips ready to dig through your deck if the combo is disrupted.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Avoid committing to the board unless you have a backup win condition or can stabilize. Storm decks are fragile; prioritize drawing into the combo over trading creatures. If forced to interact, use cheap counters or removal to protect your combo pieces.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (especially in control/mirror), removal targeting your win condition or mana rocks, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents hold up mana for multiple turns, as they likely have a counter or sweep. Track their graveyard size if they play storm or graveyard-based strategies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cast 10+ spells in a single turn to trigger storm damage or win conditions like Ral, Monsoon Mage. Sequence rituals first, then cantrips, then the finisher. Ensure you have enough mana to cast the finisher for lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana sources and rituals to cast 10+ spells, or when you can guarantee the finisher resolves. If you have a backup win condition or can protect the storm turn, commit aggressively.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are behind on board with no combo pieces, have been countered multiple times without a backup plan, or face a hard-lock strategy (e.g., mass counters, graveyard hate) with no sideboard answers. Also concede if you draw dead to the combo after multiple mulligans.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig through counters, cast spells in response to their counters, and sequence your storm turn to out-value their hand. Bring in additional counters and protection in the sideboard.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them with fast mana and early storm attempts. Use counterspells and artifact hate to disrupt their mana base. Don't let them stabilize; pressure them with early threats or combo out before they reach their big spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Sideboard in counterspells, fast removal, and life gain if available. Play defensively early, using cantrips to dig for answers. If you can't stabilize, aim for a faster combo turn or concede if the board is too far gone.
threat_responses.against_control: Dig deeper with cantrips, use counters to protect your combo, and pressure them with early threats. If they lock you out, use sideboard hate to break their resources. Aim to combo out before they can stabilize or counter your entire sequence.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Out-value with better sequencing and sideboard hate. Prioritize graveyard protection, additional win conditions, and hand disruption. Track their storm count and counters, and aim to resolve your finisher first. Bring in more counters and protection to protect your combo.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and resource count based on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, increase counters and protection. Against aggro/ramp, add faster interaction and artifact hate. In mirrors, prioritize graveyard hate and additional win conditions. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't dilute your combo consistency; keep at least 4 copies of key pieces.
notes: Ruby Medallion is crucial for reducing red spell costs, so sequence it early. Pyretic Ritual provides burst mana but can be a target; protect it with cantrips or counters. Be mindful of the storm mechanic: your graveyard count matters, so avoid unnecessary discards. Common mistake: over-committing to the board instead of drawing into the combo. Always count your spells carefully before the storm turn to ensure lethal damage.

philosophy: Amulet Titan is a fast combo-ramp deck that uses Amulet of Vigor to double untaps, generating massive mana on turn 1 or 2 to cast Primeval Titan and Scapeshift for a lethal 10+ life drain or massive land search. It aims to win before opponents can stabilize, excelling against slower control and combo decks while racing aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with Amulet of Vigor + 1-2 lands, or 2+ lands + 1+ mana dork (Elvish Mystic, Llanowar Elves, Birds of Paradise). Keep hands with Scapeshift + 2+ lands if supported by ramp. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only Primeval Titan and no ramp/Amulet, or hands with multiple dorks but no lands.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast a mana dork. Turn 2: Play second land, cast Amulet of Vigor (or second dork). Tap dorks, untap with Amulet, play Primeval Titan or Scapeshift. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast combo pieces; prioritize dual lands if you have fetches.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control/counters (Jeskai Blink, Boros Energy): Bring in Thoughtseize, Abrupt Decay, Red Elemental Blast, and Spell Pierce. Against aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in Lightning Helix, Go for the Throat, and additional removal. Against combo (Ruby Storm): Bring in Stifle, Red Elemental Blast, and graveyard hate. Against mirror: Bring in Thoughtseize, Abrupt Decay, and counterspells.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; always prioritize untapping lands with Amulet. Don't overcommit to one Titan if you can Scapeshift for more. Keep protection cards in hand until you're ready to combo. Graveyard is generally not a primary resource, but monitor opponents' graveyard strategies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back Primeval Titan if you have Scapeshift in hand and can combo off next turn. Don't attack with Titan unless you're going for lethal or need to pressure. Use Scapeshift to fetch 10+ lands for lethal or to fetch a specific land if needed.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Force of Will, Ponder/Preordain into countermagic), removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and graveyard hate. Note when opponents play discard or hand disruption.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Scapeshift for 10+ lands (lethal), or Primeval Titan + Scapeshift for massive land search + lethal. Sometimes just casting Primeval Titan and attacking with it if the board is open.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have Amulet + 2+ mana dorks + Primeval Titan/Scapeshift in hand, or when you can reliably cast Scapeshift for 10+ lands. Also commit when you have protection (counterspell, discard, or instant-speed protection) and the opponent has no removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're on turn 3+ with no Amulet, no dorks, and the opponent has a board of removal/counterspells. If you're facing a hard lock (Stax, empty board with counters) and have no way to break it. If you're down to 1 life and have no combo pieces.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard (Thoughtseize) to remove counterspells. Use cheap, uncounterable threats if possible. Use Ponder/Preordain to dig for protection. Sometimes just casting Scapeshift in response to a counter if you have a backup plan, but generally, hate out or discard is key.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use Stifle on their combo pieces. Use Red Elemental Blast or Abrupt Decay on their mana rocks. Attack early with Titan to race them. Don't let them get ahead in mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Lightning Helix/Go for the Throat to remove early threats. Use Primeval Titan to block or trade. Sometimes you can combo off turn 2 or 3 to win before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats. Use discard to remove key control cards. Combo off before they can stabilize. Use Scapeshift to go wide and pressure.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race. Use Thoughtseize to remove their Amulet or Scapeshift. Use discard to remove their protection. Attack with Titan to force trades. Sometimes you just combo off first.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize hate cards against the top meta decks. Against control/counters, bring in more discard and cheap protection. Against aggro, bring in more removal and life gain. Against mirror, bring in more hand disruption and counters. Adjust land counts if needed (e.g., more dual lands for consistency).
notes: Amulet of Vigor is the engine; never mulligan it if you have lands. Scapeshift is your win condition; protect it. Primeval Titan is both a threat and a win condition. Watch for Stax effects (Wasteland, Field of Ruin) which can shut you down. Don't overextend with Titans if you can Scapeshift for more. Common mistake: casting Primeval Titan without a way to protect it or without enough mana to attack.

philosophy: Jeskai Blink generates disproportionate value by repeatedly exiling and returning key creatures to trigger enter-the-battlefield effects, leveraging card advantage, incremental damage, and evasive threats to out-resource opponents. It excels against combo and control decks that struggle to handle recursive value engines, while remaining vulnerable to fast aggro and mass board wipes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetch/shock lands for WU/R), at least one cheap flicker effect (e.g., Flickerwisp, Ghostly Flicker, or Cloudshift), and a core ETB trigger (Quantum Riddler, Solitude, or Phlage). Mulligan if you have 4+ lands with no early plays, lack any flicker mechanism, or hold only high-cost threats with no way to trigger them.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, using fetch/shock to setup dual-colored mana. Turn 2: Cast a low-cost ETB threat or Solitude, then flicker it with a cheap effect to trigger its ability. If opening with Solitude, cast it, then flicker it with a cheap effect to copy its ETB. Prioritize establishing a board presence with evasive or protective creatures while preserving mana for reactive flicker effects.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in 2-3 early removal spells (Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Helix) and 1-2 life gain spells; drop 2-3 heavy blink engines. Against control/combo: Bring in 2-3 cantrips/card draw (Brainstorm, Ponder) and 1-2 protection spells; drop 2-2 direct damage. Against mirror: Bring in 2-3 targeted removal and 1-2 additional flicker effects; drop 2-2 generic threats to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve flicker effects for high-impact ETBs (Solitude, Phlage, Quantum Riddler). Avoid over-committing mana to flicker low-value creatures. Use blue mana for card draw to maintain hand size, as Blink decks are highly vulnerable to hand disruption. Track graveyard hate if present, as some blink engines rely on recursion or sacrifice synergies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have flicker protection or removal ready to answer targeted hate. Hold back if the opponent has board wipes or sweepers in play. Use Solitude's ETB copying to generate incremental value without overextending. Never flicker a creature into a board wipe unless you can recoup value immediately.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Bolt), and counterspells. Note if the opponent is playing discard or hand disruption, as Blink relies on hand resources. Track if they're playing sacrifice hate, exile hate, or graveyard hate, and adjust flicker timing accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Phlage, Titan of Fire's Fury (damage on ETB and when it leaves the battlefield), repeated Quantum Riddler/Solitude triggers, or a chained flicker sequence. Close out games by flickering Phlage for damage, then attacking with evasive threats or finishing with residual ETB damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to flicker multiple times, the opponent has no board wipes, or you can close the game in 1-2 turns. Also commit when you've established card advantage and can out-value their removal, or when Phlage's damage triggers can bypass blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards with no flicker effects, the opponent has a board wipe ready, or you're facing a combo deck that can win before you can stabilize. Also concede if you're locked out of mana, have no answers to their win condition, or are facing a mirror where you've lost the resource war.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig for flicker effects and threats. Cast threats in response to counterspells when possible. Use instant-speed flicker effects to bypass stack interaction. Spread value across multiple triggers rather than over-committing to single high-cost spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply pressure early before they can cast their big spells. Use removal for their mana dorks and acceleration spells. Flicker threats to out-value their ramp and force them to spend resources on multiple triggers instead of developing their board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and protection. Use Solitude/Quantum Riddler to generate value quickly without over-flickering. Focus on surviving turns 3-4, then close out the game with Phlage's damage or evasive threats. Avoid trading creatures; prioritize life total and card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and incremental damage. Use Phlage to pressure the face and force them to use removal on threats. Flicker threats to bypass targeted removal and force them to spend multiple resources on a single trigger. Apply consistent pressure to prevent them from setting up their win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing flicker effects in hand and using targeted removal to break their board. Out-value them by triggering more ETBs per mana spent. Track their hand size and mana, and avoid over-committing when they have sweepers or removal ready. Use Solitude's copying to generate value faster than they can answer.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends: prioritize card draw and protection against control-heavy fields, bring in early removal and life gain against aggro, and focus on resource denial and additional flicker effects in mirrors. Always keep at least one versatile removal and one card draw spell, and consider bringing in format-specific hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, etc.) if the metagame demands it.
notes: Common mistake: Over-flickering low-value creatures or flickering into board wipes. Always calculate ETB value before committing mana. Solitude's self-exile mechanic requires careful timing—flicker it before it dies to natural causes or sacrifice effects. Phlage's damage triggers can be used to finish games or clear blockers, but avoid flickering it into targeted removal unless you have an answer ready. Watch for format-specific hate cards and adjust your flicker targets accordingly.

philosophy: Goryo's Vengeance is a control-combo deck that leverages graveyard recursion and instant-speed interaction to disrupt opponents while assembling a card advantage engine. It aims to out-value midrange and control strategies, and combo out against slower decks, while using precise disruption to survive against aggro and fast combo.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one cantrip or early interaction (Thoughtseize, Inquisition, Fatal Push), and preferably Goryo's Vengeance paired with a land or draw spell. Mulligan to 5+ lands, all removal with no threats, or hands lacking early interaction against fast archetypes.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Cast Thoughtseize or Inquisition into a land or cantrip. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a cantrip (Opt, Preordain) or early removal (Fatal Push). Turn 3: Deploy Goryo's Vengeance if mana allows, or continue cantripping to find answers. Prioritize fetching shock lands and playing basics to avoid slow starts.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro/Combo (Boros Energy, Affinity): +3 Bloodlinecrusher, +2 Drown in the Loch, +2 Fatal Push, +1 Solitude. vs Control/Midrange (Jeskai Blink, Amulet Titan): +2 Kethis, the Hidden Hand, +2 Atraxa, Grand Unifier, +2 Solitude, +1 Thoughtseize. vs Mirror: +2 Thoughtseize, +2 Solitude, +1 Drown in the Loch.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Goryo's Vengeance as a one-time resource; only cast it when you can immediately resolve a high-impact instant (Solitude, Drown in the Loch, or Thoughtseize). Preserve graveyard resources by avoiding unnecessary discard spells if you plan to cast them from the graveyard later. Manage hand size by cantripping through dead draws.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats like Atraxa only when you have a clear path to resolution or a bounce/removal package to protect it. Hold back Solitude for key permanents (Amulet, Energy accelerants, opposing Atraxa) rather than using it on low-impact creatures. Use Thoughtseize to strip combo pieces or win conditions before they stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana rocks and accelerants (Amulet, Lotus Bloom, Energy counters) that signal combo turns. Track opponent's graveyard for recursion threats. Note when they hold up mana for counterspells or bounce effects, as this dictates whether you can safely cast Atraxa or need to disrupt first.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Atraxa, Grand Unifier is the primary win condition; once it resolves and untaps, it draws cards and can be replayed or bounced for extra value. Secondary finishers include Kethis, the Hidden Hand for card advantage, or out-grinding with Solitude bounce loops.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a resolved Atraxa with protection, or when the opponent has no interaction left to stop your combo. Commit to casting Atraxa when you can follow up with Solitude or discard to ensure it resolves and generates immediate value.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold against fast combo decks (Ruby Storm, Amulet Titan) if you lack early interaction and they have a guaranteed win condition in hand. Concede if you're completely out of resources, have no way to interact, and the opponent has a locked board or uncounterable win.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Goryo's Vengeance to cast instant spells from the graveyard, which still go on the stack but can surprise opponents. Rely on Thoughtseize to remove counterspells from hand, and sequence Solitude/Atraxa when you know they are out of interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Thoughtseize to grab accelerants (Amulet, Lotus Bloom), Fatal Push for early threats, and Solitude to bounce key mana rocks. Use Drown in the Loch from the graveyard via Goryo's to deal with problematic permanents.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early Thoughtseize for threats, Fatal Push for 1-drops, and Solitude to bounce key attackers. Prioritize survival over combo; stabilize with removal and cantrips before attempting to assemble Atraxa.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with card draw and disruption. Use Solitude to bounce opposing permanents and draw cards, then cast Atraxa to generate card advantage. Pressure with Thoughtseize to strip their answers and force them to play around interaction.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by sequencing better and holding more interaction. Thoughtseize to disrupt their combo pieces, and use Solitude to bounce their Atraxa or key permanents. Prioritize graveyard resources and be ready to cast Goryo's Vengeance at the wrong time for them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against faster decks, prioritize early interaction and hate bears. Against slower decks, increase card draw and late-game finishers. Always ensure your sideboard plan aligns with your win condition; don't bring in too much removal if you're trying to combo off.
notes: Goryo's Vengeance is a one-shot, so never cast it without a clear target. Solitude can be countered, so sequence it carefully. The deck is vulnerable to fast combo; don't overcommit to Atraxa if you can't protect it. Mana base can be tricky, so consider playing extra basics if you're consistently mana-screwed.

philosophy: Domain Zoo is a five-color aggro-midrange hybrid that leverages graveyard diversity and cheap, efficient spells to trigger Domain effects for massive value, removal, and pressure. It aims to outpace traditional aggro with flexible interaction while outlasting control through relentless, multi-colored threats and graveyard synergy.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (fetches/shocks preferred for consistency), at least one Domain enabler (Leyline of the Guildpact, cheap creatures, or graveyard-filling spells), and a mix of early threats (1-2 mana) and interaction. Mulligan on all-lands, all-removal, or hands missing both early pressure and Domain triggers. Specifically, keep hands with Tribal Flames + a cheap creature, or Archon of Cruelty + two cheap spells. Prioritize hands that can cast a spell on turn 1 to start building graveyard diversity.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-mana spell (creature or instant) to begin accumulating card types in the graveyard. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a 2-mana threat or use Tribal Flames if your graveyard has 3+ different card types. Sequence cheap spells to maximize Domain triggers without overcommitting mana. Prioritize lands that enter untapped, but never skip casting a crucial spell just to untap a land if you can cast on the current turn.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy and Affinity: Bring in additional Tribal Flames, targeted removal, and artifact hate if needed. Against Ruby Storm and Amulet Titan: Bring in graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), hard removal, and clock upgrades. Against Jeskai Blink: Bring in exile effects, discard, and threat upgrades. Against the mirror: Bring in extra Tribal Flames, cheap threats, and graveyard recursion to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Track graveyard card types meticulously; prioritize casting spells that add new types (lands, creatures, instants, sorceries, enchantments) to maximize Tribal Flames and other Domain effects. Avoid overcommitting mana on low-impact spells when a higher-impact Domain trigger is available. Manage hand size by using cheap spells to dig or discard dead cards, and preserve interaction for critical turns.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction or a clear path to the face. Hold back if the opponent has a board wipe or hard removal pending. Use Tribal Flames to clear key blockers or swing through. Archon of Cruelty is a late-game finisher; avoid casting it too early unless you can pump it or it's needed to stabilize against aggressive pressure.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (especially against control), hard removal (pathfinders, swords), and combo pieces (Storm counters, Amulet targets). Pay attention to when opponents tap out for interaction versus when they hold up mana. Note if they're playing lands that fetch or search, indicating ramp or control strategies. Track their graveyard for recursion or Domain synergy.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Archon of Cruelty with multiple +1/+1 counters, Tribal Flames for direct damage or clearing a final blocker, and a swarm of Domain-boosted creatures. The goal is to pressure while keeping interaction ready for the last turn, often using Tribal Flames to swing for lethal or remove a key defender.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to the face, the opponent has no hard removal or board wipes in hand, or when you can win before their combo/control piece resolves. If you have 3+ Domain types in your graveyard and a Tribal Flames, it's often worth swinging. Commit when your opponent is tapped out or has no interaction left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards with no Domain enablers, the opponent has established a lock (Rest in Peace, hard counters), or you're facing a combo deck that has already assembled its win condition. Also concede if you're out of resources against a resilient control deck with no clock, or if your hand is completely dead against their current board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Tribal Flames as a free spell (instant speed, flexible targeting) to pressure through counters. Wait for end step or use cheap spells to force counters. Play around counters by having multiple threats in hand and using fetch lands to dig for answers. Don't overcommit to a single spell; spread your threats across turns.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to beat them out. Use Tribal Flames to remove their ramp targets or finish them. Don't trade inefficiently; focus on the face. Bring in more direct damage and clock. If they stabilize, use Archon of Cruelty to outvalue them in the late game.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal (Tribal Flames, pathfinders) and early threats. Don't overextend; use interaction to clear key blockers. Lean into your speed; Domain Zoo is often faster than traditional aggro. Use your five-color mana base to answer their threats while applying your own pressure.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure relentlessly with cheap threats. Use Tribal Flames to clear blockers or swing for lethal. Don't give them time to draw; play around their counterspells by having multiple threats. If they have a wipe, hold up interaction or swing through it. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race and a resource war. Prioritize graveyard diversity to maximize Tribal Flames. Track their hand size and graveyard. Use discard or cheap interaction to disrupt their setup. Archon of Cruelty wins the long game; pump it with their creatures or your own. Win the graveyard war by casting more spells and keeping your types diverse.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggression to a more balanced midrange-control hybrid. Against combo and ramp, prioritize graveyard hate and hard removal to disrupt their game plan. Against control, bring in more direct damage and threat upgrades to close games faster. Always keep at least one Leyline of the Guildpact if you're not opening with it, and consider bringing in additional Domain enablers or graveyard recursion. Adjust land counts if needed (fewer basics, more fetches/shocks for consistency) and tailor your interaction to the specific metagame threats.
notes: Common mistake: Overlooking graveyard card types when casting spells. Always count before casting Tribal Flames to ensure maximum value. Another: Casting Archon of Cruelty too early without a way to pump it or protect it. Unique interaction: Leyline of the Guildpact counts as an enchantment for Domain, so having it in play or graveyard helps. Domain triggers check graveyard, library, hand, and battlefield simultaneously. Remember that fetch lands can be sacrificed for Domain if needed, but usually you want them in library. Practice tracking your graveyard types to make split-second decisions in fast games.

philosophy: Belcher is a fast combo deck that aims to assemble a specific two-card combination to deal 20 damage in a single turn, excelling at racing slower aggro, out-comboing midrange, and exploiting control decks that overcommit to disruption.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, 2+ draw/cantrip spells (Brainstorm, Ponder, Seething Song, Fire/Ignite), and at least one combo piece or way to find it (Sea Gate Restoration, Disrupting Shoal, or direct damage). Mulligan hands with 3+ lands, no draw spells, or only one land and no cantrips.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast a cantrip or draw spell. Turn 2: Play land, cast another draw spell, sequence to find combo pieces. Prioritize finding Goblin Piledriver, Seething Song, Fire/Ignite, or Restoration. Never attack with creatures unless you are at lethal or need to stabilize.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control and combo: Bring in additional Disrupting Shoal, Thoughtseize, and Lightning Bolt/Searing Spear to protect your combo and add clock. Against aggro and ramp: Prioritize Shoal and discard to disrupt their game plan while maintaining your race. Against the mirror: Focus on disruption and protection to win the combo race.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; always keep 2 mana open for Shoal and Restoration until you are ready to combo. Do not overdraw if you have enough pieces, and use Restoration to fetch from the graveyard when needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Avoid trading creatures. Belcher wins by combo, not combat. Hold back to protect your combo pieces and maintain mana flexibility for protection spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Force of Will, Counterspell), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), and spot removal. Track the opponent's life total carefully for Charbelcher's damage calculation.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Untapped Goblin Charbelcher plus 2 mana (or equivalent damage sources). Ensure Charbelcher is untapped and you have exactly the right damage to hit 20.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have the combo pieces, can protect them via Shoal or discard, and are ahead in life or can successfully race.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are dead to the board, have no way to assemble the combo, and the opponent is ahead. Also concede if you are out of resources and the opponent has established a lock.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Disrupting Shoal to counter counterspells. Use Thoughtseize to remove them early. Chain cantrips to find answers and pressure through disruption.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them aggressively. Use Shoal to protect your combo. Discard their ramp spells and use damage spells to stabilize your life total.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Shoal to protect your combo from instant-speed threats. Discard their key creatures and use damage spells to stabilize while you assemble your win condition.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cantrips to find your combo before they can lock you out. Use Shoal to protect your combo from counterspells and out-grind them with card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Race to combo first. Use Shoal to protect your combo and counter theirs. Use Thoughtseize to disrupt their hand and win the disruption race.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, increase protection (Shoal, discard) and clock (Bolt, Spear) against control and combo. Against aggro, prioritize Shoal and discard to disrupt their game plan while maintaining your clock. In the mirror, focus on disruption and protection to win the race.
notes: Common mistakes include forgetting to check Charbelcher's untapped status, miscalculating damage, or overcommitting to the board. Always track the opponent's life total. Shoal can be cast at instant speed to protect your combo or counter threats. Restoration can target your own graveyard. Do not attack with creatures unless absolutely necessary.

philosophy: Neobrand is a high-variance 5-color combo-control deck that uses Griselbrand as a game-winning engine, fueled by sacrifice outlets and Pact-based search. It aims to stabilize early, dig for its win condition, and overwhelm opponents with massive card advantage and late-game threats, excelling against control and combo decks while struggling against fast aggro and artifact strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize duals/fixing), a Summoner's Pact or sacrifice outlet, and a way to protect or find Griselbrand. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only single-colored fixing with no fetches, or a hand full of late-game cards with no early interaction. Prioritize hands that can play a turn-1 land and have a turn-2 threat or Pact.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap cantrip or protection spell if available. Turn 2: Cast Allosaurus Rider or a Pact, or play a second land. If you have Pact, search for a creature (like a sacrifice outlet or early threat), sacrifice it to draw into your combo pieces. Sequence lands carefully to ensure you can cast B spells on time for Pact/Griselbrand.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): +1-2 Dispel/Counterspell, +1-2 Lightning Axe/Red removal, -2 Pact, -1 Griselbrand. vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): +2 Thoughtseist/Inquisition, +1 Rest in Peace/Graveyard hate, -2 Allosaurus Rider, -1 Pact. vs Amulet Titan/Ramp: +1 Engineered Plague, +2 Red/Blue spot removal, -2 Pact, -1 Griselbrand. vs Mirror: +1-2 additional protection/counters, -2 sacrifice outlets.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is tight; always keep 1-2 mana open for Pact or protection. Don't overcommit to board if you can't protect Griselbrand. Graveyard is a resource for Pact but also a vulnerability; be mindful of graveyard hate. Hand size should be maintained via cantrips; never dump hand without a clear path to Griselbrand.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or a clear win condition. If opponent has sweepers or counters, hold back and stabilize. Use Allosaurus Rider to cycle into answers. Prioritize board presence that can't be easily removed or that provides value when sacrificed.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold Pact/Griselbrand until safe), sweepers (have a backup plan or protection), graveyard hate (switch to midrange/aggro plan if Pact is dead), and fast combo turns (have a clock or disruption ready). Key tells: Opponent tapping for 2+ mana on turn 2/3, discarding your hand, or targeting your graveyard.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Griselbrand is the primary finisher. Once you draw 7, you typically win via card advantage, a secondary threat, or overwhelming board state. Secondary finishers include large creatures or combo pieces found via Pact.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection (counters, hexproof, or opponent out of answers), a clear path to Griselbrand, and enough resources to survive the 7 life cost. If you can draw into Griselbrand and have a way to protect it or win immediately, commit.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can win before turn 5, if you're dead to graveyard hate and have no Pact/sacrifice outlets, or if you're down to 1 life with no way to stabilize against sweepers/counters. Also concede if you're in a mirror and opponent has more disruption/protection.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Pact on your turn to avoid targeting, or cast Griselbrand when opponent has no mana open. Use cheap cantrips to pressure through counters. If possible, use protection spells or sacrifice outlets to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Allosaurus Rider and cantrips. Use spot removal to kill ramp targets. Don't overextend; stabilize and find Griselbrand before they can deploy their win condition.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction (counterspells, spot removal, discard). Use Allosaurus Rider to cycle into answers. Don't commit to Griselbrand until you have a clock or protection. Stabilize with cheap threats and discard.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and cantrips. Force them to use resources on your early game so you can find Griselbrand when they're tapped out. Use Pact to dig for answers or win conditions. Don't let them stabilize; keep the pressure on.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a game of resource management and disruption. Prioritize discard to empty their hand, use Pact to find key pieces before they do, and protect your Griselbrand with counters or hexproof. Win by out-drawing them and finding your win condition first.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against fast decks, prioritize early interaction and clock. Against control/combo, bring in disruption and graveyard hate. In mirrors, focus on protection and hand disruption. Always keep at least one Pact and one sacrifice outlet, but reduce them if the format is too fast. Adjust land counts if needed for consistency.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to Griselbrand without protection or a win condition. Always have a backup plan. Another: Ignoring the 7 life cost; track your life total carefully. Unique interaction: Allosaurus Rider can be sacrificed to Pact to find Griselbrand, then sacrificed to Rider to draw into it, creating a powerful loop if you have the mana. Also, Pact can target creatures in your graveyard, so keep a few cheap creatures there as fuel.

philosophy: The deck aims to disrupt the opponent's hand with efficient discard spells, then cast Living End to resurrect a board of powerful creatures from the graveyard. It wins by overwhelming opponents with sudden, unblockable threats or value engines that outvalue disruption and control.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetch/shock if applicable), at least one discard spell (Thoughtseize/Duress), and a clear path to cast Living End by turn 3-4. Mulligan on pure land, no discard, or multiple low-impact creatures without mana support. Prioritize hands that can execute the discard -> Living End sequence.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize/Duress on key threats or combo pieces. Turn 2: Develop mana, potentially cast a utility creature or discard again. If the opponent is cooperative or you have enough mana, cast Living End on turn 3-4. Sequence lands carefully to ensure you can cast both discard and Living End without flooding.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and graveyard hate. Against control/combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in additional discard, protection spells, and graveyard recursion denial. Against mirror: Bring in extra graveyard hate and discard to disrupt their setup.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between casting discard, developing board, and saving for Living End. Don't overcommit to one spell if you can't follow up. Manage graveyard by knowing what you want to resurrect vs. what you want to protect from hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on casting Living End if the opponent has significant removal or graveyard hate in play. Commit when you can resolve it or when you have enough value creatures to pressure through disruption.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction), counterspells, and board wipes. Note when opponents play fetch/shock or mana dorks, as they may be setting up a combo or ramp. Track discard targets to anticipate their next move.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Resurrecting Cryptbreaker for infinite mana/bleed, Scavenging Ooze for graveyard recursion, or Tarmogoyf for board presence. Win by overwhelming the board with these engines or using them to out-value the opponent.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you can resolve Living End, have sufficient mana to cast multiple creatures, or when the opponent has no graveyard hate or removal. Also commit when you can pressure through their hand disruption.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Leyline of the Void + Rest in Peace), have no way to interact with their graveyard, or are consistently one-shot by aggro without a clear path to stabilize. Also concede if you're dead to the deck after sideboard.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Force of Negation to protect Living End or key discard. Sequence spells to force counters, then resolve the next one. Use cheap threats or discard to disrupt their hand before casting Living End.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and cheap creatures. Don't let them stabilize; use graveyard hate if needed. Force them to interact with your threats rather than developing their mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize discard to remove early threats. Use spot removal and board wipes to stabilize. Cast Living End when you can resurrect a board of value creatures to outpace their aggression.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with discard to disrupt their hand. Cast Living End when they have no removal or graveyard hate. Use Force of Negation to protect key spells. Out-value them with graveyard engines.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize graveyard hate to disrupt their Living End. Use Force of Negation to protect your own spells. Track their graveyard to know what they want to resurrect.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your mana base if needed, but focus on swapping in matchup-specific hate. Against control/combo, prioritize additional discard and protection. Against aggro, bring in removal and board wipes. In the mirror, bring in graveyard hate and discard. Always keep a balance between disruption and your own game plan.
notes: Common mistake: casting Living End too early without a plan for what to resurrect, or overcommitting to discard and leaving yourself vulnerable to aggro. Always track the opponent's graveyard and hand. Force of Negation is crucial for protecting your combo pieces. Subtlety should be used to maximize graveyard value. Don't forget to fetch lands efficiently and manage your graveyard for both offense and defense.

philosophy: Cast as many cheap instants and sorceries as possible to trigger Prowess, chipping away with 1 damage per spell while developing a board of resilient threats. Wins by out-grinding opponents through spell volume, leveraging card advantage engines, and closing out games with combat damage or Prowess triggers before control/combo decks can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (duals/fixers), plus at least one Prowess threat (Slickshot Show-Off, Cori-Steel Cutter) or a cheap cantrip/removal. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only expensive spells, or hands lacking early interaction against aggro. Prioritize hands that can cast 2+ spells on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mishra's Bauble (sacrifice for {2} damage or draw), or cast a cheap cantrip/removal. If opening with a Prowess threat, play land, cast threat, attack to trigger Prowess + spell trigger. Turn 2: Develop board with another cheap spell or threat, continue casting instants/sorceries to maximize Prowess damage. Sequence lands to ensure double-blue or red sources for Bauble/fixing.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in early interaction (Shock, Lightning Strike, Spell Pierce) and discard. vs Control/Combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm, Amulet Titan): Bring in more Prowess threats, card draw (Opt, Brainstorm), and direct damage to race. vs Mirror: Bring in more removal and Prowess enablers to out-value.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize casting cheap instants/sorceries over holding mana for Prowess triggers. Use Bauble for card advantage when ahead, damage when behind. Don't overcommit to board if opponent has sweepers; keep hand full of spells to trigger Prowess off threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you can protect them or when Prowess can close the game. Hold back if opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use Prowess to trade efficiently (e.g., cast Shock on their blocker, then attack with Prowess creature).
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), counterspells, and removal. Note when they tap out for big spells. Track graveyard if they rely on flashback or dredge. Watch for combo pieces (Amulet, Storm count, Blink targets).

endgame_tactics.finishers: Prowess triggers from Slickshot/Cori-Steel, direct damage spells, and combat damage. Win by chipping to lethal or attacking with multiple Prowess threats while casting spells in response.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ spells in hand, a Prowess threat on board, and opponent has no sweepers or has tapped out. Also commit when you can race combo decks to lethal before their turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1 life, opponent has a sweeper/removal in hand, you have no spells to trigger Prowess, and they have a win condition on the stack or ready to combo. Also concede against Amulet Titan if you can't race and they have protection.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Prowess to cast spells in response to counterspells (e.g., cast Shock, let them counter, cast another spell to trigger Prowess again). Use Bauble for card advantage. Don't overcommit expensive spells; rely on cheap volume.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them. Prowess is fast. Use early pressure and direct damage to win before they play big threats. If they stabilize, use Prowess to trade efficiently.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early interaction (Shock, Lightning Strike) to remove key threats. Use Prowess to trade damage efficiently. Don't overextend; keep spells to answer their board.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early before they can stabilize. Use Prowess to bypass board wipes by casting spells in response to their sweepers. Win the resource war by casting more spells than they can answer.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Out-value them with better sequencing. Prioritize casting spells to trigger Prowess before attacking. Use Bauble for card advantage. Win by having more spells in hand and better threat resilience.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Against control/combo, increase Prowess threats and card draw to race. Against aggro, add early interaction and discard. Always keep Bauble and core Prowess enablers. Consider bringing in specific hate cards (e.g., Red Elemental Blast for counters, Spell Pierce for combo). Maintain a balance between pressure and interaction.
notes: Common mistake: Holding too many lands and not casting enough spells to trigger Prowess. Always prioritize casting instants/sorceries over holding mana. Remember that Prowess triggers even if the spell is countered (as long as it was cast). Use Bauble for damage when behind, card draw when ahead. Watch out for sweepers and plan spell sequencing around them. Cori-Steel Cutter and Slickshot Show-Off are your primary win conditions; protect them or use them to cast spells in response to threats.

philosophy: R/G Eldrazi Ramp accelerates mana through lands, artifacts, and spells to cast massive, game-ending Eldrazi threats that overwhelm opponents via evasion, board wipes, and card advantage. It dominates slower, resource-heavy matchups but must carefully manage its curve to survive fast aggro and combo strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands plus at least one ramp spell (Rampant Growth, Cultivate, Farseek, Kodama's Reach) or a turn-1 artifact (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet). Keep Kozilek's Command if paired with 2+ lands. Mulligan to 5+ lands, pure ramp without lands, or hands lacking early interaction when facing Boros Energy or Affinity.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, or cast a ramp spell. Turn 2: Cast a second ramp spell or Eldrazi Temple; aim for 3-4 mana by turn 3. Turn 3: Cast your first Eldrazi threat (Reality Smasher, Kozilek, Butcher of Truth) or Kozilek's Command. Prioritize untapping lands for ramp over casting threats if you're behind on board or facing aggro.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity: +2 Artifact Hate, +2 Discard, -2 Ramp. Against Ruby Storm: +2 Discard, +1 Fast Threat, -2 Ramp. Against Amulet Titan/Jeskai Blink: +2 Extra Value Engines, +1 Board Wipe, -2 Discard. Against Mirror: +2 Discard, +1 Extra Threat, -2 Ramp.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat mana as a deployment tool, not just a casting cost. Hold priority to cast Kozilek's Command in response to opponent's removal or sweepers. Avoid over-committing to hand if you lack answers; prioritize card advantage via Kozilek's Command draw mode or Eldrazi draw engines to maintain pressure.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough mana to answer a board wipe or when the opponent is low on interaction. Hold back if you suspect a sweeper or if you're facing aggro and need to stabilize. Use Kozilek's Command to clear key blockers or sweepers before committing your own threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (e.g., Wrath of God, Supreme Verdict), targeted removal, and counterspells. Note when opponents tap out for sweepers or discard effects. Track graveyard hate if they're trying to disrupt your ramp or value engines.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emrakul, the Promised End is the primary win condition, providing evasion, extra turns, and card advantage. Kozilek, Butcher of Truth and Ulamog, the Ceaseless Hunger provide secondary win paths through card draw and board wipes.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to cast two threats or when the opponent has exhausted their interaction. Commit fully if you can answer a potential board wipe or if you're ahead on board and can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Stasis, Winter Orb, or a combo that wins on turn 3), if you have no answers to a cleared board, or if you're drawing dead lands while the opponent is at lethal. Also concede if you're behind in a mirror and the opponent has more value engines.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Kozilek's Command (discard) to remove counterspells from hand. Cast threats in response to counterspells to force them to spend resources. Deploy multiple threats to overwhelm counter suites.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-value them by prioritizing Kozilek's Command to disrupt their ramp spells or key permanents. Race if they're ahead, or use discard to slow their game plan.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Answer early threats with Kozilek's Command (destroy) or hold back a Reality Smasher to block. Prioritize survival over ramp; mulligan aggressively for interaction. Use discard to remove key aggro threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats to force interaction. Use Kozilek's Command to draw cards or remove key permanents. Emrakul wins against control by generating extra turns and card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value is king. Use Kozilek's Command (discard) to remove their threats or ramp. Track their mana development and commit threats when they're tapped out. Prioritize card advantage engines to out-value them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against faster decks, reduce ramp by 2-3 and add discard, artifact hate, or early interaction. Against slower decks, add more finishers and card advantage. Always keep Kozilek's Command flexible; its modes make it a universal tool. Avoid over-indexing on hate cards if your game plan is to race or out-value.
notes: Common mistake: Over-ramping into a board wipe without answers. Always hold priority to cast Kozilek's Command in response to sweepers. Eldrazi are colorless, so they bypass color-specific hate but are vulnerable to artifact/enchantment hate. Sowing Mycospawn is a niche mana source; prioritize it only if you're severely mana-flooded. Watch for opponent's discard effects; hold Kozilek's Command to protect your hand.

philosophy: Eldrazi Tron is a combo-ramp archetype that wins by assembling three specific lands to generate massive amounts of colorless mana, then deploying game-ending Eldrazi threats that overwhelm opponents through raw power, unblockable damage, and permanent exile. It excels against slow, reactive, and combo decks that lack immediate board wipes or hard locks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with two or more Tron lands (Ancient Tron, Eye of Ugin, Urza's Tower), or one Tron land plus two mana-producing lands that can fetch basics. Keep Thought-Knot Seer if paired with at least two lands. Mulligan on three or more lands with zero Tron pieces, or hands with only one land and no acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thought-Knot Seer if possible, or play a Tron land. Turn 2: Play another land, cast Seer or a second Tron piece. Sequence lands to ensure you can fetch basics for Tron activation. Prioritize getting the trio online over casting threats prematurely.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in Dispel, Kozilek's Command, and Rest in Peace. Against control/combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm): Bring in Thought-Knot Seer, Kozilek's Command, and Dispel. Against Amulet Titan/mirror: Bring in Dispel, Kozilek's Command, and additional Tron pieces or hate.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; hoard it until you can cast a threat with a backup removal spell. Use Thought-Knot Seer to disrupt opponent hands and dig for answers. Manage the graveyard by using Kozilek's Command to exile key recursive threats or combo pieces.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a Kozilek's Command or Dispel ready to answer removal. Don't drop Emrakul into a spot where it can be countered or removed without a backup. Use Ulamog to exile key permanents and Kozilek to disrupt opponent's mana or permanents.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Day of Judgment, Boros Energy finishers), counterspells, and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or when they start tapping lands for Tron-like acceleration.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Emrakul, the Aeons Torn (unblockable, draws cards, exiles permanents), Ulamog, the Ceaseless Hunger (exiles permanents, destroys lands), Kozilek, the Great Distortion (taps/untaps, destroys artifacts/enchantments), and Ugin, the Spirit Dragon (exiles permanents, deals damage).
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have the Tron lands online, enough mana to cast a threat and have a removal spell ready, or when the opponent is at low life and you can go wide with unblockable threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're missing key Tron pieces, the opponent has a hard lock (massive board wipe + counterspells), or you're facing a combo that can go off before you can stabilize.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Kozilek's Command to exile the counter spell, or cast threats in response to their casting triggers. Use Thought-Knot Seer to dig for answers. Split your mana to cast threats when they're tapped out.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use Kozilek's Command to exile their ramp sources or finishers. Thought-Knot Seer to disrupt their hand. Don't overcommit if they can out-value you.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early Thought-Knot Seer to disrupt their curve. Kozilek's Command for sweepers. Hold back a threat to answer their key attacker. Stabilize with Ugin or Kozilek.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with Seer and early threats. Don't let them stabilize. Use Kozilek's Command to remove their key permanents. Go wide with multiple threats if possible.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race and a resource war. Prioritize hand disruption. Use Kozilek's Command to exile their Tron pieces or finishers. Win the mana race by optimizing land drops. Dispel is key.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw combo/ramp to a more interactive control/ramp hybrid. Increase hand disruption and permanent removal. Adjust land counts if needed. Bring in hate cards against specific archetypes (Rest in Peace vs. graveyard, Dispel vs. counters, Kozilek's Command for versatility).
notes: Common mistakes include overextending into sweepers, forgetting to fetch basics for Tron, mismanaging Kozilek's Command (exiling the wrong thing), and not using Thought-Knot Seer early enough. Tron lands produce colorless mana that can be used for other spells. Emrakul's draw step can be used to dig for answers. Ugin's -3 can be used to exile key cards.

philosophy: Jeskai Control aims to stabilize the early game with efficient interaction and disruption, control the midgame with Narset and Teferi, Time Raveler, and close out the late game with resilient threats and card advantage. It wins by out-grinding opponents in the late game while dictating the pace and neutralizing key threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 4+ mana sources (lands, fetches, shock lands) and at least one land. Prioritize hands with 2+ answers (Lightning Strike, Path to Exile, Cryptic Command, Orim's Chant) or a Narset/Teferi, Time Raveler. Mulligan pure land draws, hands missing a color of mana, or hands with multiple low-impact cards like Orim's Chant without answers.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, hold up 1-2 mana for interaction. Turn 2: Play a shock land or fetch, develop mana, and cast a cheap answer if threatened. If safe, drop Narset or Teferi, Time Raveler. Prioritize untapping lands for shock targets to enable tempo or dig for specific colors.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in Lightning Strike, Abrupt Decay, Surgical Extraction. vs Combo (Ruby Storm): Bring in Narset, Teferi, Time Raveler, Surgical Extraction, Dispel. vs Control/Blink: Bring in Teferi, Hero of Dominaria, Cryptic Command, Negate, Disenchant. vs Mirror: Bring in Negate, Dispel, Teferi, Hero of Dominaria, Surgical Extraction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve counterspells and removal for high-value targets. Use fetches/shocks strategically to enable shock lands for tempo or dig for colors. Manage the graveyard with Narset, Teferi, Time Raveler, Abrupt Decay, or Surgical Extraction. Avoid overcommitting to the board without answers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing threats until you have at least one answer or a Teferi, Time Raveler out. Use Orim's Chant to protect your board or disrupt opponent combos/aggro. Prioritize efficient 1-for-1 trades: removal for threats, counterspells for game-ending spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent mana bases to predict key spells, graveyard activity for Storm/Ruby, and board state for sweepers or lock pieces. Note when they hold up mana past turn 3-4, as this often signals a counterspell or removal ready for your threat.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Teferi, Hero of Dominaria (draws cards, protects board, generates value), Teferi, Time Raveler (locks opponent, protects your board), or a well-timed Lightning Strike/Path to Exile sequence to clear the way for a single threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a Teferi out, or when the opponent has no answers to your threat. Commit when you can stabilize and they're at low life, or when you have a clear path to victory with minimal risk.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you're at low life, have no answers to a committed board, and the opponent has multiple win conditions or a lock piece (e.g., multiple Narsets, Teferi, Time Raveler, or a Storm combo ready). Also concede if you're dead to the topdeck and have no comeback.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Orim's Chant to protect your spells, cast Narset/Teferi at instant speed when they're tapped out, or use fetch/shock to dig for answers. Don't overcommit to one threat; trade efficiently and respect their mana.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction (Lightning Strike, Path, Orim's Chant) to stop key ramp spells. Use Teferi, Time Raveler to lock them out. Don't let them get to 5+ mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Lightning Strike, Path to Exile, Cryptic Command, and Orim's Chant. Don't overextend. Use Teferi, Time Raveler to slow them down. Win with Teferi, Hero or efficient trades.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind with card draw (Teferi, Hero, Narset). Use Orim's Chant to protect your threats. Trade efficiently and don't let them sweep your board. Win by out-resourcing and closing with a resilient threat.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing and better sequencing. Prioritize Teferi, Time Raveler and Narset. Use Orim's Chant to protect your board. Trade efficiently and don't overcommit. Surgical Extraction and Negate are key.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your mana base if needed (fewer fetches/shocks if going mono-colored, more basics). Bring in hate cards based on the metagame. Prioritize keeping your core game plan intact while adding specific answers. Don't over-sideboard; keep the deck functional and ensure your win conditions remain viable.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without answers, misusing Orim's Chant on your own spells unnecessarily, forgetting to track opponent mana and graveyard, and failing to use Teferi, Time Raveler's tap ability effectively. Unique interactions: Orim's Chant can protect your Narset/Teferi from removal, and Teferi, Time Raveler's -1 can be used to bounce your own creatures to recast them or protect them from sweepers.

philosophy: Grixis Reanimator aims to disrupt the opponent’s early game with targeted discard, then leverage the graveyard to reanimate game-winning threats or generate overwhelming card advantage. It wins by out-resourcing combo and control decks while stabilizing against aggro with unblockable or evasive reanimated threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one discard spell (Thoughtseize/Duress), and a reanimation spell or early threat. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only lands, or only threats with no discard/mana. Prioritize hands that can play turn 1 Thoughtseize into a land or early threat, ensuring you can sequence mana for disruption before committing to the graveyard.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize targeting the opponent’s key early piece (ramp spell, combo piece, or early threat). Turn 2: Play a land, cast a second discard or an early threat/reanimation spell. If you have a reanimation target in hand or graveyard, sequence mana to cast it. Land sequencing: Prioritize basics or duals that match your first two spells; avoid overextending into removal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and additional discard. Against control/combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm): Bring in graveyard hate, extra discard, and resilient reanimation targets. Against Amulet Titan/ramp: Bring in artifact hate, early threats, and targeted removal. Against mirror: Bring in additional discard, graveyard hate, and sideboard reanimation targets to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for reanimation versus disruption; avoid overcommitting to the graveyard without protection. Keep cards in hand for interaction, and use Abhorrent Oculus to dig for reanimation spells or threats. Track your graveyard carefully to ensure you have enough resources to follow up on reanimation plays.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection (discard, counters, or opponent tapped out) or when you can follow up with additional value. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal in play. Use discard to clear the path for your reanimation, and avoid swinging into unfavorable combat.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Fatal Push), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), and combo win conditions. Note when the opponent taps out for interaction, as this is your window to reanimate threats or dig deeper.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Reanimated Archon of Cruelty or Abhorrent Oculus. Oculus generates card advantage and mana to swing for lethal, while Archon crashes through with trample/haste and clears the board on entry. Secure these threats in the graveyard, protect them from hate, and swing when the path is clear.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection, the opponent is tapped out, the graveyard is safe from hate, or you can swing for lethal in one or two turns. Also commit when you’ve disrupted the opponent’s hand and they have no interaction left.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re down to one card, the opponent has a hard board wipe, you have no reanimation spells, and they are ahead on board. Also concede against locked-out control or combo decks that win before you can stabilize, or if you’re consistently dead to graveyard hate.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells before casting reanimation spells. Cast threats in response to their interaction, and use cheap spells to pressure. If counters are pervasive, pivot to graveyard value or alternative win conditions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and early threats. Don’t let them stabilize or find their big threats. Use graveyard hate if they rely on it, and reanimate threats to race their ramp spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep discard in hand for turn 1-2 to disrupt early plays. Use spot removal for key creatures, and reanimate a blocker or threat to stabilize. Don’t overcommit to the graveyard if you can’t protect it.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind them with graveyard value and discard. Use Thoughtseize to clear the path, and reanimate threats when they tap out for sweepers. Pressure them to force mistakes and avoid letting them stabilize.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war by prioritizing graveyard hate and tracking their reanimation spells. Reanimate threats when the opponent is tapped out, and use Abhorrent Oculus to out-value them in card advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Generally, increase graveyard hate against combo/control, add more spot removal against aggro, and bring in additional discard against control/mirror. Keep reanimation targets flexible and ensure your mana base can support the adjusted game plan.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the graveyard without protection, forgetting to track the opponent’s hand for counters/removal, and missequencing mana for reanimation. Abhorrent Oculus can be reanimated to draw cards and generate mana, enabling further plays, while Archon of Cruelty’s enter-the-battlefield effect can clear the board. Always keep a Thoughtseize for turn 1 to disrupt the early game, and be prepared to pivot if your primary game plan is shut down.
