philosophy: Disrupt the opponent's early game with Thoughtseize, establish a resilient board presence using Agatha's Soul Cauldron and efficient midrange threats, and out-value opponents through card advantage engines like Abhorrent Oculus. Wins by out-resourcing control, stabilizing against aggro, and disrupting combo before it resolves.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep 2-3 lands (including Agatha's if possible) plus 1-2 spells. Prioritize hands with Thoughtseize + a land, or a 2-drop threat + lands. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only Agatha's and no spells, or hands with multiple Thoughtseize but no early plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if possible. Turn 2: Play Agatha's or a midrange threat. Tap Agatha's for mana/Zombies as needed. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for Thoughtseize and threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros/Affinity): +1 Bloodtithe Harvester, +1 Fatal Push/Go Blank, -2 Abhorrent Oculus, -1 The World Tree. vs Combo (Ruby/Amulet): +1 Surgical Extraction, +1 Thoughtseize, -2 Agatha's Soul Cauldron, -1 Abhorrent Oculus. vs Control/Blink: +1 The Wandering Emperor, +1 Fatal Push, -2 Thoughtseize, -1 Agatha's.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoards mana for Thoughtseize early, then commits to board. Uses Agatha's to fuel spells and generate incremental value. Avoids overextending into spot removal; keeps a Thoughtseize or removal for key threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commits threats when opponent has no spot removal or counters. Holds back if they have sweepers or spot removal. Uses Agatha's tokens to chump or trade into removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watches for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods), spot removal (Fatal Push, Go Blank), and combo pieces (Amulet, Ruby, Energy counters). Notes when they tap out for threats vs holding up removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: The Wandering Emperor, The World Tree, or a fully fueled Abhorrent Oculus combo with Agatha's. Wins by trading up in cards and closing with unblockable threats or combat damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When opponent is tapped out, has no removal, or you've established a board they can't clear. Goes all-in with threats and Agatha's pumps.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If down to one life, opponent has a board wipe ready, you have no removal, and your hand is empty. Also against locked combo decks with no hate.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Uses Thoughtseize to remove counterspells. Casts threats in response to their counterspells. Uses Agatha's to generate mana for multiple threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Thoughtseizes their ramp pieces. Plays early threats to pressure before they can cast big spells. Uses spot removal on key ramp creatures.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Thoughtseizes early threats. Plays spot removal and chumps with Agatha's tokens. Stabilizes and transitions to midrange value.
threat_responses.against_control: Applies pressure with midrange threats. Uses Thoughtseize to remove key answers. Out-values them with card draw and incremental board presence.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Thoughtseizes for their Thoughtseize or key threats. Trades efficiently. Wins by having better card advantage and better late-game finishers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from disruption to value against control, and from value to disruption/spot removal against aggro/combo. Prioritize hate cards against combo, and board wipes/spot removal against aggro. Adjust land count if needed.
notes: Agatha's Soul Cauldron is a land, so it doesn't count as a spell for counterspells. Abhorrent Oculus synergizes with Agatha's tokens and sacrifice outlets. Don't overcommit to Agatha's if you can't protect it. Watch for artifact hate vs Affinity.

philosophy: Esper Blink generates incremental value and board presence by repeatedly exiling and returning key creatures to trigger their enter-the-battlefield effects, trading efficiently while controlling the board with versatile interaction. It wins by out-valuing slower strategies, leveraging protection and tempo to handle aggression, and closing out games with resilient threats or combo finishes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fetch/shock-compatible), a turn-1 or turn-2 play (cheap threat, cantrip, or early blink enabler), and at least one interaction (removal, protection, or cantrip). Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only high-cost spells, no way to interact with early aggression, or a completely dead hand of removal without threats to blink.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap cantrip or early threat if mana allows. Turn 2: Cast a blink enabler or early value creature, then blink it if possible to trigger ETB effects. Turn 3: Ensure WUB mana is online for key spells; sequence fetch/shock into basics if you need specific colors for interaction or protection. Prioritize establishing board presence without overcommitting to a single threat.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in cheap removal, board wipes, artifact hate, and protection. Remove expensive blink targets and late-game finishers. vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Amulet Titan): Bring in graveyard hate, additional targeted removal, card draw, and late-game win conditions. Remove early interactive spells and low-impact threats. vs Mirror (Jeskai Blink): Bring in targeted removal, extra protection, and cards that punish ETB triggers. Keep the core game plan intact.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction and blink triggers; avoid tapping out for non-essential spells. Use cantrips to dig for key pieces and maintain a hand of 3-4 cards. Manage your graveyard carefully—avoid unnecessary exile effects if the opponent has recursion, but leverage them to your advantage. Track opponent's mana and hand size to time your blinks.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or removal ready to handle sweepers or removal. Hold back if the opponent has untapped mana for board wipes or heavy interaction. Use blink effects to trade efficiently (e.g., blink a low-cost creature into a higher-value one) and avoid overextending into known answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., board wipes, artifact destruction), counterspells, and recursion engines. Note when the opponent taps out for a key spell or has no interaction left. Track graveyard and exile contents for combo pieces or recursion threats. Pay attention to mana development to predict when they can cast their win conditions.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large blink targets with powerful ETB effects, or combo finishes using repeated blink triggers to generate infinite value or lethal damage. Secure the board with removal and protection, then attack with resilient threats or chain triggers to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, protection against sweepers, and the opponent is low on resources or has no immediate answer. Commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns or when you've forced the opponent to use their interaction on non-critical threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to a single vulnerable creature against a known sweeper, have no interaction, and the opponent is stabilizing or comboing out. Also concede if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no win conditions, or if your hand is completely dead against the current board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips to dig past counters, cast spells at instant speed when safe, use protection, or commit multiple threats to overwhelm. Avoid blinking into known counters; instead, trade value or wait for a window when they've used their interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early interaction with cheap removal and artifact hate. Don't overcommit to the board; value trade efficiently and use protection on key threats. Force them to spend resources on removal rather than accelerating their game plan.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap removal and board wipes. Use protection on key threats and avoid blinking into the board if it will be wiped. Don't overextend; hold for value and stabilize before committing to a win condition.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with resilient threats and use card draw to out-value them. Blink to bypass removal or trigger value, and force them to use interaction on non-critical threats. Don't let them stabilize; keep pressure on while managing your resources.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades matter most. Use targeted removal to kill their blink targets before they blink. Protect your own threats and track ETB triggers. Win by out-resourcing them in card advantage and making better decisions about when to commit to the board.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction density based on the matchup. Against aggro, reduce blink targets and increase removal/wipes to stabilize early. Against control/combo, add graveyard hate, additional card draw, and late-game finishers to out-value them. In mirrors, prioritize targeted removal and protection to win value trades. Always keep your core win condition intact and avoid over-indexing on one matchup.
notes: Common mistake: Blinking into a known sweeper. Always ask 'what happens if they have a board wipe?' before committing. Unique interactions: Some blink effects can chain with specific ETB triggers for infinite value; learn your deck's specific combos and practice them in mirrors. Mana base: Ensure you can reliably cast WUB spells by turn 3; consider basic lands or duals that enter untapped if needed. Track opponent's graveyard and exile zones, as blink decks often interact with these zones. Practice timing your protection spells to maximize value trades.

philosophy: Sultai Midrange aims to out-value opponents through resilient threats, efficient interaction, and late-game lock pieces, leveraging card advantage to outlast aggressive and combo decks while grinding down control strategies. It wins by controlling the early game, establishing a board presence with Abhorrent Oculus, and closing out games with Subtlety-protected threats or Sink into Stupor.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one 1-2 mana interaction (discard or spot removal), and a 2-3 drop threat or card advantage. Mulligan on 1-lands, 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands completely lacking interaction against aggro/combo.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a 1-mana interaction or pass. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a 2-drop (removal, discard, or early threat). Turn 3: Deploy Abhorrent Oculus if mana is open, or use interaction to protect it. Prioritize untapped lands for flexibility and to enable Subtlety or Sink into Stupor later.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): +2 Surgical Extraction, +2 Rest in Peace, +2 Spell Pierce, -2 Sink into Stupor, -1 Abhorrent Oculus. vs Control/Combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm, Amulet Titan): +2 Spell Pierce, +2 Rest in Peace, +2 Surgical Extraction, +1 Abhorrent Oculus, -2 early interaction. vs Mirror: +2 Spell Pierce, +2 Surgical Extraction, -2 aggro hate.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until threats are committed. Use Abhorrent Oculus to stabilize hand size and board. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection; Subtlety should be saved for key threats or to enable a surprise attack. Manage graveyard by using Surgical Extraction or Rest in Peace when needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have card advantage or removal ready. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or spot removal. Use Subtlety on your most resilient threat or to protect a Sink into Stupor cast.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Boros Charm, Swords to Plowshares), spot removal, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for spells vs. threats. Track graveyard hate availability.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sink into Stupor is the primary finisher, locking the opponent out of their deck or ending the game outright. Subtlety-protected threats (like Abhorrent Oculus or other midrange creatures) can also close out games.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no sweepers, and you can protect your threats with Subtlety or removal. Also commit when you can safely cast Sink into Stupor and the opponent lacks graveyard hate or disruption.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that out-races your stabilization, if you're dead to the board (e.g., no interaction against a locked board), or if you have a completely dead hand with no comeback potential after 3+ turns.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Subtlety to protect key spells, cast threats in response to counterspells, or use multiple threats to out-value counters. Sink into Stupor is often uncounterable or worth the risk due to its game-ending potential.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with interaction, use Surgical Extraction to disrupt mana dorks, and stabilize with Abhorrent Oculus. Avoid trading inefficiently; out-value them with card advantage.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and discard to slow the pace. Use Abhorrent Oculus to stabilize and draw into answers. Subtlety can protect a key threat to close out the game quickly.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage (Oculus, discard). Use Subtlety to protect threats from spot removal. Sink into Stupor can lock them out of their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Card advantage wins. Use Subtlety on your most resilient threat or to protect a Sink into Stupor. Prioritize graveyard hate and Spell Pierce to disrupt their resources. Win by out-drawing and out-resolving.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve by removing late-game lock pieces against fast decks and adding early interaction. Against control/combo, lean into hate cards and card advantage. In the mirror, prioritize disruption and graveyard hate. Always ensure your mana base can support the new card distribution.
notes: Abhorrent Oculus is fragile to sweepers but excels at generating card advantage and board presence. Subtlety is a versatile tool—use it to protect threats, enable surprise attacks, or protect Sink into Stupor. Sink into Stupor is a game-ending lock but vulnerable to graveyard hate; have backup plans. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection, and always track opponent's mana and hand size to time your plays optimally.

philosophy: Yawgmoth is a recursive combo-control deck that converts creatures from your library into card advantage and life via Agatha’s Soul Cauldron and Yawgmoth, Thran Physician, ultimately aiming to cast Yawgmoth from the graveyard to win the game. It excels at out-graveyarding control and combo decks while stabilizing against aggro and ramp through efficient life gain and value generation.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with Agatha’s Soul Cauldron, Yawgmoth, Thran Physician, or Badgermole Cub paired with at least two lands. Also keep hands with two lands plus two key pieces, or three+ lands with early interaction. Mulligan hands with three+ lands and no key pieces, or hands with one land and no early plays. Prioritize hands that can start the engine or stabilize against early aggression.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. Turn 2: Play a second land, then cast Agatha’s Soul Cauldron if possible. If you have a creature in hand and the Cauldron is in play, mill it. Turn 3: Cast Yawgmoth, Thran Physician if you have two creatures in the graveyard, sacrifice them for life and draw, then mill more. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit Cauldron on turn 2 or 3 without overextending into early aggression. If facing aggro, prioritize lands and early interaction over the Cauldron.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity/Amulet Titan (aggro/ramp): Bring in 4x Thoughtseize/Discard, 3x Fatal Push/Sheoldred’s Edict, 2x Rest in Peace/Leyline of the Void, and 2x more life-gain or spot removal. Against Ruby Storm/Jeskai Blink (control/combo): Bring in 3x Thoughtseize, 3x Rest in Peace, 2x Surgical Extraction, and 2x additional interaction or graveyard recursion. Against mirror: Bring in 3x Leyline of the Void, 2x Surgical Extraction, and 2x additional value creatures or discard.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between casting the Cauldron, Physician, and early interaction. Avoid overcommitting to milling when below 15 life. Use graveyard creatures as incremental value rather than all-in threats. Keep cards in hand to answer sweepers or graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have at least two creatures in the graveyard to recoup via Physician. Hold back against sweepers, board wipes, or heavy removal. Use the Cauldron to mill defensively when pressured, but don’t sacrifice key creatures if it leaves you vulnerable to a counter-attack.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Leyline of the Void, Soul-Guide Lantern, Rest in Peace) and sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods). Note when opponents tap out for combo pieces or counterspells. Track which creatures are in the graveyard to calculate Physician value and avoid over-sacrificing.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cast Yawgmoth from the graveyard or library. Once in play, sacrifice remaining graveyard creatures for massive life and card draw, then attack or cast additional Yawgmoths. Use recursion or mill effects to keep the engine running if Yawgmoth is removed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have three or more creatures in the graveyard, sufficient life to survive a turn, and no immediate sweepers or graveyard hate on the board. Also commit when the opponent has no interaction left or is locked into a slow game plan.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re dead to a board wipe with no recovery, have no way to rebuild your graveyard, or are facing a locked-in combo with no interaction. Also concede if you’re consistently drawing lands with no key pieces or interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast key pieces at instant speed when possible, use multiple low-cost threats to force counter decisions, and rely on graveyard recursion to bypass hand disruption. Don’t overcommit to a single win condition.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use spot removal on key ramp creatures, stabilize with Physician life gain, and out-value them with card draw. Don’t race; focus on disrupting their acceleration and closing out with Yawgmoth once they overextend.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and discard to slow them down. Stabilize with Cauldron/Physician life gain and incremental threats. Once stabilized, close out the game quickly before they can recover.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and use graveyard recursion to bypass hand disruption. Force them to use answers on the Cauldron or Physician, then win with Yawgmoth when they run out of resources. Don’t overcommit to milling if they have sweepers ready.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange is key. Track which Yawgmoths are in play and prioritize surviving to out-value. Use graveyard hate to disrupt their engine, and win by having more creatures in the graveyard or better sequencing. Don’t over-sacrifice if it leaves you vulnerable to their Physician.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your land count to 24-25 based on metagame speed. Lean into graveyard hate and discard against control/combo, and prioritize interaction and life gain against aggro/ramp. Ensure your post-sideboard deck has a clear plan to disrupt opponent strategies while maintaining your own engine. Always track which hate cards are still in the game.
notes: Never mill blindly; calculate Physician value before sacrificing. Watch for Leyline of the Void and Soul-Guide Lantern, which can completely shut down your game plan. Sequence Agatha’s Soul Cauldron carefully to avoid dying to early aggression. Remember that Yawgmoth is a legendary creature, so you can only have one in play at a time. Practice graveyard tracking to maximize value.

philosophy: Temur Prowess aims to cast a high volume of cheap instants, sorceries, and cantrips to rapidly trigger Prowess, inflating the power and toughness of your creatures to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize. It wins by racing combo and control decks with explosive early turns, while leveraging card advantage and tempo to out-value aggressive and mirror matches.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fetch/shock-compatible), at least one 1-mana spell (Mishra's Bauble, Opt, Lightning Strike), and a Prowess threat (Slickshot Show-Off or Cori-Steel Cutter). Mulligan on 2+ lands with no 1-drops, 3+ lands with no threats, or hands that cannot cast two spells on turn 1. Prioritize hands that enable a turn-1 Bauble or cantrip into a turn-2 threat.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Mishra's Bauble or a 1-drop cantrip/inst. Turn 2: Play land, cast another spell, then drop Slickshot Show-Off or Cori-Steel Cutter. Sequence Bauble -> Opt -> Slickshot to maximize Prowess triggers before combat. If opening with Cori-Steel Cutter, ensure you control a creature to cast it for 2, or sequence a 1-drop first to enable the discount.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): +1 Blood Moon, +1 Red Elemental Blast, +1 Abrupt Decay, -2 Mishra's Bauble, -1 land. vs Control/Combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm, Amulet Titan): +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Abrupt Decay, +1 Lightning Axe, -2 Mishra's Bauble, -1 land. vs Mirror: +1 Lightning Axe, +1 Red Elemental Blast, -2 Mishra's Bauble, -1 land. Adjust land count based on matchup speed and Blood Moon presence.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize card advantage over raw board presence. Never cast a Prowess spell if it leaves you with zero cards and no threats unless necessary to stabilize. Use Mishra's Bauble to dig for answers or threats, but avoid overextending into spot removal. Keep mana open for interaction (Lightning Strike, Abrupt Decay) while sequencing spells to maximize Prowess triggers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have card advantage or interaction to protect them. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or spot removal ready. Use Prowess to trade efficiently: cast a 1-drop, attack with your threat, block with another threat, then cast another spell to pump the blocker. Avoid attacking into known removal or sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Boros Charm), spot removal (Lightning Axe, Abrupt Decay, Path to Exile), and combo win conditions. Note if the opponent is playing tapped lands or artifact acceleration (Affinity). Track graveyard hate if you rely on Thought Scour, and monitor their hand size to gauge interaction availability.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Slickshot Show-Off with 5+ Prowess triggers (7/7 flyer) or Cori-Steel Cutter with massive stat inflation. Direct damage spells like Lightning Strike or Lightning Axe to close out games, trade up, or remove key blockers. Use Prowess to stabilize into a lethal attack or force unfavorable trades.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, the opponent has no interaction, or you can trade threats efficiently. Commit when you can cast 2+ spells this turn to guarantee a lethal-sized threat or force a favorable combat math scenario. Don't hold back if you can pressure through their remaining interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1 card, the opponent has a board wipe ready, or you're facing a combo deck that has already assembled its win condition with no disruption or race available. Also concede if you're consistently drawing lands with no 1-drops or threats, or if you're out-valued in a mirror with no sideboard answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast cheap spells to trigger Prowess before casting threats. Drop threats at end step to avoid counters. Use Bauble to dig for answers or additional spells to trade up. Never overcommit to a single threat; use Prowess to force multiple blocks or trades.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them aggressively. Use cantrips to find interaction and disrupt their mana base. Don't let them stabilize; pressure early and often. Use Bauble to accelerate your own game plan and outpace their ramp spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use spot removal (Lightning Strike, Abrupt Decay) on key threats. Trade efficiently and avoid overextending into combat tricks. Use Prowess to stabilize and take over the board. Don't let them establish a wide board; answer early and pressure back.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure and cantrip into threats. Don't let them stabilize with card draw or sweepers. Use interaction to protect your board and force unfavorable trades. Win by out-valuing them and racing to lethal before they can assemble their game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value card advantage and efficient trades. Use Lightning Axe to remove their threats and disrupt their Prowess triggers. Don't overcommit to Prowess if they have removal. Win by having more resources, better sequencing, and superior sideboard positioning.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure tempo/race plan to a more interactive midrange game plan. Bring in hatebears, spot removal, and sweepers against combo and control decks to disrupt their game plans and stabilize. Bring in additional interaction and land disruption against aggro to slow their pace. Adjust your land base based on matchup speed and Blood Moon presence, and prioritize keeping hands with interaction and threats over pure cantrips.
notes: Common mistakes include overcasting Prowess spells without threats on board, ignoring opponent interaction, and mismanaging Mishra's Bauble (sacrificing it too early or late). Cori-Steel Cutter can be cast for 2 if you control a creature, making it a powerful early drop. Slickshot's flying makes it difficult to block, so use it to pressure or trade efficiently. Always sequence spells to maximize Prowess triggers before combat, and track graveyard hate if you rely on Thought Scour.

philosophy: Mardu Blink is a disruptive tempo-control hybrid that wins by repeatedly exiling and returning threats to trigger enter-the-battlefield effects, generate incremental value, and neutralize key opponent pieces. It leverages hand disruption and instant-speed protection to outmaneuver combo and control decks, while using recursive threats and token generation to close out aggro matchups efficiently.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one hand disruption spell (Thoughtseize/Duress), and a blink enabler or early threat (Blinking Spirit, Phantom Monster, or a mana rock). Mulligan to 4-5 lands if you lack disruption or enablers, as you need to find your interaction early to protect your game plan. Keep hands with Solitude or Overlord if paired with a cantrip or cheap enabler to ensure you can cast and blink them.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if possible, or deploy a mana rock/cheap enabler. Turn 2: Cast a cheap blink creature or mid-cost threat. Sequence mana rocks into enablers, then blink your own creatures or opponent's threats. Prioritize finding lands if you're light, and use Thoughtseize to remove combo pieces or early aggro threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in more removal (Fatal Push, Lightning Bolt), protection (Lightning Greaves, Swiftfoot Boots), and discard. Against control/combo (Ruby Storm, Amulet Titan): Bring in additional disruption, graveyard hate if needed, and extra blink threats. Against Jeskai Blink mirror: Bring in more interaction, targeted removal, and cards that disrupt the mirror's key pieces (like Solitude for Overlord).

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Keep mana flexible for instant-speed blinking and disruption. Avoid overcommitting to blink without protection or follow-up. Manage your graveyard carefully if the deck uses recursion, but prioritize exile as the primary engine. Maintain hand size with cantrips; use Thoughtseize on game-ending threats or combo pieces.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or protection ready. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or hard interaction. Blink opponent's threats to temporarily remove them, but be wary of triggering beneficial ETB effects for them. Use your own blink creatures to clear the board or protect key threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), hard counters, and combo win conditions. Note when the opponent taps out for a key spell—this is your window to attack or blink. Track graveyard contents if they rely on recursion, and monitor their hand for disruption to plan your turns accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Solitude and Overlord of the Balemurk are primary finishers. Blink them repeatedly to deal damage, generate Zombie tokens (via Overlord's death trigger), and remove remaining threats. Use Lightning Greaves to protect them from removal and ensure they survive long enough to trigger their effects.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have multiple blink enablers, protection, and the opponent has no sweepers or hard interaction. Commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns via combat damage from blinked creatures or when you can overwhelm the opponent with token generation and threat recursion.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be disrupted, if you have no interaction against a locked board, or if you're consistently mulliganing to dead hands. Concede if the opponent has multiple sweepers and you're out of resources, or if your game plan is completely neutralized by sideboard cards.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap enablers and flash threats to bypass counters. Blink in response to counterspells if possible. Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells from hand. Sequence around them by committing multiple threats or using instant-speed blink effects to pressure through interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and cheap threats. Don't let them stabilize. Use removal to kill their ramp sources. Blink their key threats to remove them temporarily and disrupt their mana development.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Thoughtseize to disrupt early plays. Play cheap blockers or blink creatures to trade efficiently. Use Lightning Greaves to protect your threats. Stabilize with removal and then close the game with blink threats before they can overwhelm you.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with discard and early threats. Don't let them draw into sweepers. Use blink to bypass removal (exile and return). Go wide with tokens if Overlord is on board, and force them to use interaction on low-value threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the disruption war. Use Thoughtseize to find their blink enablers or threats. Prioritize killing their key threats with Solitude/Overlord. Manage mana for instant-speed responses. Win by out-resourcing their blink engine or disrupting their hand before they can stabilize.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against combo-heavy formats, prioritize disruption and graveyard hate. Against aggro, bring in more removal and protection. In mirrors, sideboard in cards that target the mirror's key pieces. Always keep a balance of interaction and threats, and be prepared to shift your game plan from control to aggro or vice versa based on what the opponent leaves in.
notes: Common mistake: Blinking your own creatures without protection, leading to removal. Always weigh the risk of ETB triggers. Don't overcommit to blinking if you can't follow up. Use Thoughtseize aggressively early to disrupt combo and control. Remember that Solitude and Overlord exile and return the same card, so plan your turns accordingly. Overlord's death trigger is a key resource—use it to generate tokens when clearing the board or when opponent's removal hits your threats.

philosophy: Izzet Steel-Cutter is an artifact-accelerated midrange deck that leverages Mox Opal for early mana, Tamiyo for card advantage and interaction, and Cori-Steel Cutter as a scalable threat. It aims to out-value and out-resource control and combo decks while using artifact synergy to close out games quickly against slower strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any 7-card hand with 2+ lands (including Mox Opal) and at least one of Tamiyo or Cori-Steel Cutter. Keep hands with Mox Opal plus two artifacts in play or in hand if you have a turn-2 play. Mulligan hands with three or more lands and no early threats, or hands with Mox Opal but no artifacts to enable it.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then Mox Opal if possible. If you have a two-drop artifact or creature, play it to enable Opal. Turn 2: Cast Tamiyo or Cori-Steel Cutter. If you have Opal plus two artifacts, you can cast a three-mana spell on turn two. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit your mana steps without flooding; prioritize duals or fetches that support your artifact count.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy and Affinity, bring in artifact hate, spot removal, and early threats. Against Ruby Storm and Jeskai Blink, bring in counterspells, discard, and interaction. Against Jeskai Blink and control, prioritize card advantage and sweepers. Against the mirror, bring in targeted removal and extra card draw.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn three or four. Use Tamiyo's abilities to cycle cards and maintain hand size. Do not overcommit artifacts if the board is unstable; Opal enables spells, but do not sacrifice your hand for tempo. The graveyard is generally neutral, so avoid self-mill unless explicitly synergistic.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Cori-Steel Cutter when you have protection or can trade favorably. Hold back Tamiyo if facing sweepers. Use your artifact count to dictate board state; if you have three or more artifacts, you can afford to be aggressive. Protect your key pieces with counters or cheap artifacts.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers like Cyclonic Rift or Wrath of God, counterspells like Spell Pierce or Mana Leak, and targeted removal like Shock or Path. Track opponent artifact count if they are also artifact-based. Note when they tap out for a single big spell or when they are low on resources for interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cori-Steel Cutter with protection or haste, backed by Tamiyo's card advantage and Opal's mana acceleration. Overwhelming board presence with artifact synergies and direct damage spells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have three or more mana available, Tamiyo on the board, and the opponent has no sweepers or counterspells in hand. Also commit when you can trade threats favorably or when the opponent is at low life with no blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you are down to two life with no artifacts, no hand, and the opponent has a board of three or more creatures. If you are facing a locked combo that resolves on their next turn with no interaction left. Also concede if you are out of resources against a control deck that has stabilized.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split spells, cheap artifacts to enable Opal, and Tamiyo's card draw to out-value counters. Do not overcommit to one spell; threaten with multiple small plays and force them to use multiple counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Cori-Steel Cutter. Use artifact hate and spot removal to slow their mana development. Tamiyo's interaction is key to disrupting their acceleration steps.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with spot removal and Tamiyo's counters or interaction. Do not go wide with artifacts if you cannot block; focus on removing key threats and drawing into answers. Use Opal to cast cheap removal efficiently.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and Opal acceleration. Tamiyo's card advantage out-grinds their removal. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their win condition. Play around sweepers by threatening with multiple small artifacts.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war. Tamiyo's abilities are crucial for cycling and drawing into answers. Prioritize keeping interaction in hand. Trade artifacts favorably and use Cori-Steel Cutter to close when they run out of answers. Value each spell carefully.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure acceleration to targeted interaction. Add more spot removal and counterspells against control and combo, and more early threats or artifact armor against aggro. Adjust your land base to include more basics if facing heavy removal or sweepers, and consider bringing in graveyard hate if Ruby Storm or Amulet Titan becomes prevalent.
notes: Mox Opal requires two artifacts to activate, so do not play it on turn one unless you have a backup plan. Tamiyo's abilities likely scale with artifacts or provide card draw; use them to maintain tempo. Cori-Steel Cutter's name suggests cutting or combat damage; protect it with counters or cheap artifacts. Avoid overextending into sweepers. Common mistake: playing Opal without enabling it, or holding too many lands. Always track opponent interaction before committing your board.

philosophy: Jeskai Energy is a value-control archetype that generates massive amounts of energy counters to cast high-impact spells and fuel card-draw engines. It aims to out-resource opponents through superior mana acceleration, using Phlage as a pivotal win condition and Nulldrifter/Doorkeeper Thrull to control the board while accelerating toward game-ending threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing energy-producing lands or fetch/shocks if format-legal), at least one energy generator (Energy Converter, Harvester, or Refractor), and a turn-2 play. Keep hands with 3+ energy generators even with 2 lands. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, zero energy generators, or only expensive spells without acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then an energy generator if available. Turn 2: Ramp into a second generator or cast Doorkeeper Thrull. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your turn-2 generator. Use energy counters to cast instants or protect threats. Prioritize playing lands that tap for energy or mana, and avoid overcommitting to cheap spells if you lack board presence.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Boros Energy/Affinity: Bring in Nulldrifter, additional removal, and artifact hate. vs Ruby Storm/Amulet Titan: Bring in countermagic, graveyard hate, and early interaction. vs Jeskai Blink/Control: Bring in additional card draw, Phlage, and board wipes. General: +2 Nulldrifter, +2 Doorkeeper Thrull, -2 lands, -2 cheap instants.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard energy counters for Phlage or Nulldrifter. Don't overspend on cheap instants if you're behind on board. Manage your graveyard carefully, as Nulldrifter triggers for all players. Keep hand size high by using energy to cast draw spells or sacrifice Phlage at optimal times.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction or energy to protect them. Nulldrifter is a board wipe that also stops graveyard recursion; play it when opponents have multiple threats or when you're ahead. Doorkeeper Thrull protects your energy generators; keep it on the board to sustain your engine.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's graveyard size (Nulldrifter trigger), their mana base (can they answer Phlage?), and their interaction count. Key tells: Opponent holding up mana for counterspells/removal means you should commit threats gradually or use energy to cast instants in response.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Phlage, Titan of Fire's Fury is the primary finisher. Cast it, draw two cards, then sacrifice it for X cards to find more threats or win conditions. Alternatively, use accumulated energy to cast multiple high-impact spells in a single turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 5+ energy, a clear path to combat, or when the opponent has no interaction left. Commit Phlage when you can follow up with card draw or have protection ready.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life, have no energy generators, and the opponent has a board wipe or lock piece. Also concede if you're facing a combo deck that has already assembled their win condition and you have no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use energy to cast instants in response to counterspells. Play threats in multiples or use protection spells. Bait counterspells with cheap energy spells before committing Phlage.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Nulldrifter shuts down their graveyard recursion. Use early interaction to disrupt their mana development. Phlage's card draw helps you out-value their ramp.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize Doorkeeper Thrull and early energy generators to stabilize. Use Nulldrifter to clear the board. Hold up interaction for their removal, then commit threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with energy generators and Doorkeeper Thrull. Use Phlage to out-value their card draw. Force them to use interaction on your threats, then win with accumulated value.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize energy generation and card draw. Nulldrifter is key to breaking their engine. Use Phlage to out-value them in card draw. Sideboard in additional interaction and graveyard hate.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve by removing lands and cheap instants in control/mirror matchups, and adding more interaction and removal in aggro/combo matchups. Always prioritize keeping Nulldrifter and Doorkeeper Thrull, as they are format-warping. Adjust your mulligan strategy to be more aggressive in control matchups and more conservative in aggro matchups.
notes: Energy counters are a unique resource; track them carefully and don't waste them on low-impact spells. Phlage's sacrifice ability is a double-edged sword; only sacrifice it when you can find immediate value or win the game. Nulldrifter's enter-the-battlefield ability triggers for all players, so be mindful of your own graveyard. Common mistake: Overcommitting to energy generation without threats to spend it on, or sacrificing Phlage too early without follow-up. Unique interaction: Energy Refractor can generate energy from tapping, synergizing with Doorkeeper Thrull's protection.

philosophy: Grixis midrange aims to control the board with efficient removal and counterspells while out-value opponents with resilient threats and card advantage engines. It wins by stabilizing early, neutralizing combo and ramp threats, and closing out games with high-impact planeswalkers or evasive creatures.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or fetches for fixing), a turn-1 or turn-2 interaction like Counterspell or removal, and a low-cost threat or card draw. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, all high-cost spells, or no interaction in the first two turns.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and cast a cheap interaction or set up mana if possible. Turn 2: Develop your mana base, cast a 2-drop threat or value engine, and prioritize untapping for color fixing. If facing aggro or ramp, hold interaction for turn 2-3 threats rather than overcommitting early.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in discard, cheap removal, and board wipes. Vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Add counterspells, graveyard hate, and protection. Vs Amulet Titan: Include artifact destruction and additional removal. Vs Mirror: Prioritize card draw and targeted removal over generic sweepers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until threats appear, and use card draw to maintain hand size without overextending. Manage your graveyard by using bounce or exile effects when recursion becomes a threat, and avoid tapping out for non-essential spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to resolution or sufficient answers to protect them, holding back high-value permanents if the board is unstable. Prioritize trading efficiently and never overcommit to a board state you cannot recover from.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo pieces like Storm counts, Titan triggers, or Blink targets, and note when opponents tap out for big spells. Track graveyard accumulation for recursion threats and monitor when key interaction is on the stack to time your plays.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact planeswalkers like Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student, and evasive threats win the game by establishing an unanswerable board state. Close out games by leveraging card advantage to out-value opponents and forcing them to spend resources on multiple threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, board control, and the opponent is low on resources or locked out of their game plan. Commit when you can safely resolve a win condition or planeswalker without fearing a sweep.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are consistently one-shotted, have no interaction against a locked combo, or face a dominant board wipe with no recovery tools. Concede when your win conditions are consistently answered and you lack the resources to rebuild.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats, split your spells across turns, or use protection and enablers to bypass counters. Overload counters with multiple low-cost spells or time your key plays to resolve after they've spent their interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction, artifact hate, and board wipes to prevent opponents from establishing a massive mana base. Answer key ramp pieces immediately and avoid trading into their early threats without clearing the board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play efficient removal and counterspells early to stabilize by turn 3-4, then transition to value engines and card advantage. Don't overextend into combat; focus on surviving the early game and out-grinding them midgame.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats and use card advantage to out-value them, forcing them to spend resources on multiple targets. Protect your key pieces with counterspells or protection, and capitalize on their mana denial by playing around their answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing card draw, targeted removal, and protection over generic sweepers. Out-value them by resolving more threats, answering their key pieces first, and leveraging Tamiyo's card draw to maintain hand superiority.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the specific meta by increasing graveyard hate against recursion and storm, adding artifact destruction against Amulet and Affinity, and including more discard or board wipes against aggro. Maintain a flexible balance of removal and counterspells to stay adaptable to shifting threats.
notes: A common mistake is overcommitting to the board without answers, so always keep at least one interaction in hand for turn 3-4. Protect Tamiyo at all costs, as her loyalty abilities provide essential card advantage and board control. Be mindful of Commander tax and use it to your advantage by casting spells in response to triggers or when opponents are tapped out.

philosophy: Mono-Black Midrange aims to dominate the early game through targeted discard and efficient spot removal, then close out matches with resilient, high-impact threats that demand multiple answers. It wins by out-valuing opponents, punishing greedy mana bases, and exploiting mismatched interactions across aggro, combo, and control archetypes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a Thoughtseize or Thought-Knot Seer, and at least one early threat or removal spell. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ lands with no plays, or multiple Thoughtseizes without threats/removal. Prioritize hands that can execute a turn 1 Thoughtseize or turn 2 Thought-Knot Seer to disrupt the opponent's plan immediately.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on the opponent's most disruptive or combo piece. If they lack key threats, take a land or low-impact card to preserve your hand. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Thought-Knot Seer or a midgame threat. Against combo, prioritize Thought-Knot Seer to dig for and discard their win condition. Sequence lands carefully to ensure you can cast disruption on turn 1 and threats/removal on turn 2 without flooding.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Boros Energy/Affinity (Aggro): Bring in Fatal Push, Lightning Helix, and additional Thoughtseize for early pressure. vs Ruby Storm/Amulet Titan (Combo): Bring in Thought-Knot Seer, Surgical Extraction, and Rest in Peace to disrupt and lock their graveyard. vs Jeskai Blink (Control): Bring in Liliana, Dreadhorde General, Sheoldred, and threat upgrades to out-value their interaction. vs Mirror: Bring in extra Thoughtseize, Surgical Extraction, and discard protection to win the hand war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve hand disruption for critical turns; avoid Thoughtseizing into empty hands. Use spot removal efficiently against unblockable or high-impact threats. Manage your graveyard by avoiding unnecessary self-mill unless synergistic, and consider Rest in Peace if combo is prevalent to deny recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or discard to answer their interaction. Hold back if you're out of answers and they have sweepers or counters. Prioritize trading removal for their most dangerous permanent, not just the biggest, to maintain board presence and hand advantage.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo win conditions (e.g., Storm count, Amulet activation, Blink targets) and track their removal and discard to know when you can safely commit a threat. Note if they're playing lands that suggest a specific strategy (e.g., multiple Islands for Blink, Mountains for Energy) to adjust your disruption priorities.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact threats like Tarmogoyf, Liliana, Dreadhorde General, or Sheoldred, supported by graveyard recursion (Bloodghast) or card advantage. Win by applying pressure while holding up interaction, or by out-valuing through discard and efficient removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage in hand, they have no sweepers/counters, or you can safely attack with a threat that demands multiple answers. Also commit when you've disrupted their combo or control plan and they lack the interaction to stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards with no threats, they have a guaranteed combo turn, or you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Rest in Peace + no recursion) with no way to break it. Also concede if you're consistently drawing lands and they have multiple sweepers or counters.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Kozilek's Command to destroy a land or bounce a permanent, bypassing counters. Cast threats in response to their counterspells if you have protection, or use discard to remove their counterspells before they can cast them. Force them to spend resources on land destruction rather than counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Thought-Knot Seer to dig for and discard their ramp spells or win conditions. Use spot removal on their mana sources or early threats. Kozilek's Command to destroy a land or remove a key permanent. Out-value them by trading efficiently and punishing their greedy mana development.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize Thought-Knot Seer to dig for removal. Use spot removal on their biggest threats. Hold back on committing too many threats early; stabilize with discard and removal, then close out with resilient threats. Don't overcommit to the board if they have sweepers or pump spells.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient threats and discard their counterspells/sweepers. Use Kozilek's Command to break their board or remove a key permanent. Out-value them through card advantage and graveyard recursion. Force them to spend resources on interaction rather than developing their board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize Thought-Knot Seer to dig for removal or threats. Use Kozilek's Command to destroy their lands or remove a key threat. Track their hand to know when to commit, and prioritize taking their disruption over their threats to maintain hand advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the matchup. Against combo, prioritize early disruption and graveyard hate. Against control, bring in more threats and card advantage. Against aggro, focus on spot removal and early pressure. Always keep at least one discard spell and one land destruction option in your sideboard for flexibility, and consider bringing in Rest in Peace or Surgical Extraction against decks that rely heavily on the graveyard.
notes: Common mistake: Overthinking Thoughtseize targets. Take the most disruptive card, not necessarily the biggest threat. Another mistake: Committing too many threats early without answers, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers. Unique interaction: Kozilek's Command can be used to destroy your own land for mana fixing or to bounce a permanent to bypass hexproof/shroud. Always track opponent's graveyard if playing against recursion or combo decks, and consider sideboarding in graveyard hate early if the metagame shifts toward combo-heavy strategies.

philosophy: Outrace and out-mill your opponent by rapidly emptying your library while disrupting their game plan, leveraging efficient creatures and instant-speed graveyard synergies to win before control or combo decks can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one early mill engine (Hedron Crab or Tasha's Hideous Laughter), and a discard spell (Thoughtseize/Duress) if you have a land drop. Keep hands with Fractured Sanity if you can reach 5+ cards in your graveyard quickly. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple copies of the same low-impact spell, or hands lacking early mill engines or disruption.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Hedron Crab or Tasha's Hideous Laughter. If you have acceleration (Lotus Petal, Chrome Mox, Arcane Signet), use it to cast a mill creature or discard on turn 1. Turn 2: Cast a second mill engine or use discard to remove a key threat. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your first two mill pieces by turn 2. If you have Fractured Sanity, hold it for when your graveyard has 5+ cards.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in Fatal Blow, Fatal Push, Dispel, Spell Pierce, or Surgical Extraction. vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in Thoughtseize, Duress, Negate, Spell Pierce, Cryptbreaker, or Rest in Peace. vs Mirror: Bring in additional mill engines (Tasha's, Hedron Crab), discard, and graveyard hate if needed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve your graveyard for Fractured Sanity and recursion. Don't overcommit creatures if you can mill from your hand. Use discard to protect your mill engines or remove key threats. Manage mana to ensure you can cast Fractured Sanity at instant speed when your graveyard is large.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit mill creatures early to apply pressure and protect them with bounce or protection if available. Hold back if the opponent has a clear answer (e.g., Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction) and you can't protect your graveyard. Prioritize milling over attacking with creatures unless you're close to winning.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, Leyline of the Void), sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), and combo win conditions. Note if the opponent is playing discard or counterspells to protect their library or disrupt yours.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Fractured Sanity cast from hand or graveyard when your graveyard has 10+ cards. Tasha's Hideous Laughter sacrificed repeatedly. Hedron Crab sacrificed with acceleration. Aim to reduce opponent's library to 0 while keeping yours above 0.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 2+ mill engines on board, your graveyard is large enough for a lethal Fractured Sanity, or the opponent is below 5 cards in library. Don't hold back if you can win this turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast aggro deck and your hand is empty of mill engines or protection, if the opponent has established a hard lock (Rest in Peace + sweepers), or if you're down to 0 cards in library and have no way to mill.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells. Cast mill creatures in response to their counterspells. Use Fractured Sanity from the graveyard to bypass counters. Consider Spell Pierce or Negate to protect your own spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use discard to remove key ramp pieces. Mill them out before they can cast their big spells. If they have a fast combo finisher, prioritize mill over removal.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early discard to remove threats. Use Fatal Blow/Fatal Push to clear board. Mill quickly to race. Don't overextend creatures; prioritize graveyard size for Fractured Sanity.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with mill creatures. Use discard to remove key answers. Mill through their library while they try to stabilize. Fractured Sanity can win instantly if their library is low.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the graveyard war. Use discard to remove their mill engines. Prioritize casting Fractured Sanity when your graveyard is larger. Use Cryptbreaker to recur Tasha's or Hedron Crab. Control the board with discard and removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Against fast decks, prioritize removal and discard to stabilize. Against control/combo, bring in more disruption and graveyard protection. In the mirror, focus on graveyard size and recursion. Always carry at least one copy of Surgical Extraction or Rest in Peace if the meta is heavy on graveyard strategies.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to attacking with creatures instead of milling. Remember, mill wins the game, not combat. Another mistake: Not tracking the opponent's library size. Use Fractured Sanity when you can win. Watch out for Leyline of the Void or Rest in Peace; have a plan B (like direct damage or mill creatures that don't rely on the graveyard). Tasha's Hideous Laughter is a 2-for-1 if it dies; use it wisely. Fractured Sanity can be cast from the graveyard, so don't be afraid to mill yourself to set it up.

philosophy: This deck aims to assemble a sacrifice/death-trigger loop centered around Blade of the Bloodchief and Devourer of Destiny to generate incremental damage, card advantage, and board control, ultimately closing out games with Kozilek's Command or direct lethal triggers. It excels against slower, control-oriented, and combo-heavy archetypes that overextend, while struggling against fast, disruptive aggro.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Kozilek's Command, and at least one early enabler (Blade of the Bloodchief or combo starter). Mulligan on 1-land hands, hands with only removal and no threats, or hands with multiple copies of the same low-impact card. Prioritize hands that can develop mana and establish a board by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and hold for Kozilek's Command if facing disruption, or cast a cheap enabler. Turn 2: Develop mana, deploy Blade of the Bloodchief or combo starter, and look to establish a board presence while preserving interaction for turn 3-4. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Kozilek's Command on time if needed.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity: Bring in artifact/enchantment hate, additional Kozilek's Command, and lifegain. Against Ruby Storm/Jeskai Blink: Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal, and card advantage. Against Amulet Titan: Bring in artifact destruction and early interaction. Against mirror: Bring in extra combo pieces and protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize mana development for Kozilek's Command's versatility. Use the graveyard strategically for value, but avoid overcommitting if graveyard hate is present. Manage hand resources by holding interaction for key threats rather than burning it on minor plays.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a clear answer to the opponent's removal or when you can close the game. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy disruption. Use Blade of the Bloodchief's lifelink to stabilize against aggressive pushes.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and graveyard hate. Note when opponents tap out for key spells. Track life totals carefully due to Blade of the Bloodchief's death trigger and opponent lifegain. Pay attention to artifact/enchantment counts for Kozilek's Command targeting.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Assemble the combo loop to trigger Blade of the Bloodchief repeatedly for lethal damage, or use Kozilek's Command to clear the board and draw into a win condition. Direct damage from death triggers or card draw into a finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a guaranteed combo sequence, when the opponent is at lethal range, or when you can protect your combo pieces from removal. Commit when you can out-value their interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast aggro deck with no stabilization tools, if you're dead to graveyard hate with no alternative win cons, or if you're out of resources and the opponent has a clear path to victory. Concede when your combo pieces are consistently answered without a backup plan.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Kozilek's Command's instant speed modes, split your combo into multiple steps, or rely on mana dorks/land drops to out-value counters. Cast threats in response to counterspells when possible.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize artifact/enchantment destruction, use lifegain to survive their early damage, and race with your own acceleration. Disrupt their mana base with targeted removal.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Mulligan for early interaction, use Kozilek's Command to remove key threats, stabilize with lifegain, and transition to a control/combo game plan. Don't overcommit to the combo if you're behind on the board.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure, use card advantage to out-value their removal, and close the game before they can stabilize. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war, prioritize protecting your combo pieces, and use Kozilek's Command to disrupt their setup. Trade efficiently and look for the first misstep in their combo sequence.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the specific metagame threats. Increase artifact/enchantment hate against Amulet Titan and Affinity, add graveyard hate against Ruby Storm and Jeskai Blink, and bring in additional lifegain and early interaction against Boros Energy. Adjust your curve to be more resilient against the specific strategies you face, and consider cutting low-impact cards that don't align with the post-sideboard game plan.
notes: Be mindful of Blade of the Bloodchief's death trigger timing; it can be a double-edged sword if you accidentally sacrifice it to your own effects. Kozilek's Command is your most versatile tool—use it to answer problems rather than just as removal. Avoid overcommitting to the combo if the board state is unfavorable, and always keep a backup plan in case your primary win condition is disrupted.

philosophy: Green Eldrazi is a hybrid ramp and disruption deck that uses early thought-horror to slow opponents while accelerating mana to deploy game-winning Eldrazi threats. It excels against artifact-heavy and combo decks that rely on permanents or specific spell sequences, leveraging its built-in hate and uncounterable threats to close out games quickly.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably Forests or duals), plus at least one early disruption (Thought-Knot Seer) or ramp spell. Keep hands with 3+ mana sources and a Thought-Knot Seer or Kozilek's Command. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple copies of the same low-cost spell without lands, or hands lacking early interaction against artifact/combo decks.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Forest, cast Thought-Knot Seer if possible, or hold for ramp. Turn 2: Cast ramp spells (Cultivate/Farseek) or drop a second land, then cast Thought-Knot Seer if mana allows. Prioritize playing lands first, then casting disruption/ramp. If you have a hand with Kozilek's Command, hold for turn 3-4 when you can cast it for its full value.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Affinity/Amulet Titan/Boros Energy: Bring in Kozilek's Command (artifact/enchantment modes), Ancient Grudge, or other artifact hate. Against Ruby Storm/Jeskai Blink: Bring in discard (Thought-Knot Seer copies), graveyard hate, or additional removal. Against control/midrange: Keep disruption, bring in more threats (Emrakul, World Breaker) and sweepers if available.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for ramp vs. casting threats. Don't overextend into sweepers. Use Thought-Knot Seer's scry/discard to dig for answers or disrupt opponent's combo pieces. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary scry if opponent runs graveyard hate, but generally, Eldrazi benefits from card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal/hate ready for their interaction. Hold back against sweepers or board wipes until you have a way to protect or recast. Use Kozilek's Command to clear key permanents (artifacts/enchantments) before committing your own.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for artifact acceleration (Arcbound Ravager, Mycosynth Lattice), combo win conditions (Storm count, Blink triggers), and sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment). Note when opponents tap out for spells vs. holding mana for protection.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Glaring Fleshraker (trample, haste, artifact/enchantment destruction), Emrakul, the Aeons Torn, or World Breaker. Get there by ramping to 4-5 mana, using disruption to clear the path, and deploying threats when opponents are tapped out or out of answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a lethal threat on board, opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal, or you can follow up with a second threat. Also commit when you've disrupted their combo pieces early.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that outpaces your disruption, if you're dead to a board wipe with no recovery, or if you're consistently mulliganing into dead hands against artifact decks that out-value you.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Eldrazi threats are typically uncounterable or cast via ramp that bypasses counters. Use Thought-Knot Seer to discard counterspells. Cast Kozilek's Command on their permanents before they can respond.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-ramp them with your own acceleration. Use Thought-Knot Seer to disrupt their mana rocks. Kozilek's Command can remove key ramp pieces.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Thought-Knot Seer is your best friend—discard their threats. Kozilek's Command removes early attackers. Hold back on committing big threats until you stabilize, then close with trample.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early disruption and mid-game threats. Use Kozilek's Command to remove their answers. Don't overcommit; play around sweepers and control spells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-drawing and out-resolving. Thought-Knot Seer is key to disrupting their hand. Prioritize clearing their threats with Kozilek's Command. Win the resource war by ramping faster and deploying uncounterable threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame shifts. Against artifact-heavy formats, maximize artifact/enchantment hate (Kozilek's Command, Ancient Grudge, Relic of Progenitus). Against combo, add discard and graveyard hate. Against control, bring in more threats and protection. Always keep a balance of disruption and finishers.
notes: Common mistakes: Overcommitting to the board without answering their interaction, misusing Kozilek's Command (always pick the artifact/enchantment mode against this metagame), and forgetting to scry with Thought-Knot Seer to dig for answers. Unique interaction: Glaring Fleshraker's ETB can clear key artifacts/enchantments, making it a perfect mid-game swing against this specific metagame.

philosophy: Esper Midrange seeks to dictate the pace of the game through precise hand disruption and targeted interaction, transitioning into a resilient midgame where Psychic Frog applies pressure while out-resourcing opponents. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on specific pieces or slow game plans, but requires careful resource management to close out against aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a Thoughtseize or Counterspell, and a turn-2 play. Mulligan pure land hands, single-color mana hands, or hands with multiple Thoughtseizes but no early threats or interaction. Keep hands that can play a land, cast Thoughtseize on turn 1, and follow up with a land or interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on the opponent's most threatening card (prioritize combo pieces, ramp spells, or early aggro threats). Turn 2: Play a land, cast Psychic Frog or a cheap interaction/finisher. If facing combo, prioritize disruption over development. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/playing duals/shocks if available, otherwise ensure non-basic lands enter untapped or use fetches to find basics for shock targets.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy/Affinity): In 4x Surgical Extraction/Rest in Peace, 2x Damping Sphere, 2x Spell Pierce/Counterspell. Out 2x Psychic Frog, 2x late-game finishers. vs Combo (Ruby Storm/Amulet Titan): In 4x Counterspell, 2x Thoughtseize, 2x Damping Sphere, 2x Engineered Plague or specific hate. Out 2x Psychic Frog, 2x extra threats. vs Control/Jeskai Blink: In 2x Damping Sphere, 2x extra threats, 2x discard. Out 2x Counterspell, 2x hate. vs Mirror: In 2x extra interaction, 2x threat upgrades. Out 2x basic lands or redundant effects.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn 3-4. Don't overextend Psychic Frog into sweepers or removal. Use Thoughtseize early to deny key pieces, then rely on card advantage from threats. Manage graveyard by using Surgical Extraction or Rest in Peace if needed, but prioritize hand disruption.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Psychic Frog when you have interaction to protect it or when the opponent has no sweepers. Hold back if they have potential board wipes or removal. Use Counterspell to protect your own threats or disrupt their win conditions.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo decks setting up (e.g., Amulet Titan charging, Ruby Storm drawing cards). Note when control decks have their main interaction up. Track if aggro decks are holding up mana for removal. Key tell: Opponent tapping out on turn 2-3 usually means they're setting up a combo or have a specific win condition.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Psychic Frog applying pressure, supported by Counterspell and discard. If the board is clear, commit to attacking with all threats. Use Thoughtseize to remove the last piece of their combo or a key blocker.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage, they have no sweepers, and you can protect your threats. If they're at 5 or less life and you have a clear path, go all-in.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing a fast combo that has already assembled their win condition and you have no interaction left. If you're down to 1-2 cards and they have a board wipe or sweepers ready. If the matchup is dead (e.g., no way to stop a specific combo).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells early. Cast threats at end step or when they have no mana. Use multiple threats to force multiple counters. If they have heavy counter, pivot to discard and pressure.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early disruption is key. Thoughtseize their ramp spells. Use Counterspell on their big threats. Apply pressure with Psychic Frog to force them to interact instead of ramping.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction (Counterspell, discard) to slow them down. Play Psychic Frog to trade efficiently. Don't overcommit to long games; close quickly with threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with Psychic Frog. Use Thoughtseize to remove their key interaction or win conditions. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than setting up their own game.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing and better sequencing. Use Thoughtseize to remove their key threats or interaction. Protect your Psychic Frog with counters or discard. Win the attrition war by having better late-game threats and interaction.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame shifts. Generally, increase interaction vs combo, increase threats vs control, and add hate vs specific strategies. Keep the core game plan intact but tailor disruption to the top decks.
notes: Common mistake: Overplaying Psychic Frog into removal without protection. Always track opponent's mana and hand. Psychic Frog's discard effect is a form of interaction, so don't waste it on empty hands. Use fetch lands to optimize mana base. Remember that Counterspell is versatile; save it for key moments.

philosophy: Lantern is a midrange-control hybrid that aims to lock the opponent out of the game using Ensnaring Bridge and targeted discard, while generating incremental card advantage with Lantern of Insight and Whir of Invention. It wins by out-grinding opponents through resource denial, board locks, and attrition until they concede or are overwhelmed by small threats and value engines.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/shocklands if available, otherwise basics), plus at least one of Ensnaring Bridge, Thoughtseize, or Whir of Invention. Keep hands with discard spells if paired with a land or a protection/removal spell. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple non-basic lands without fetch sources, or hands heavy on late-game threats with no early interaction or lock pieces.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize/Duress if mana allows, or play a basic land. If opening with Bridge, play land then Bridge, then discard. Turn 2: Play second land, cast Whir of Invention if Lantern is in play, or play another discard/bridge. Sequence lands to ensure you can hit your 2-drop and 3-drop turns consistently. Prioritize getting Bridge online by turn 2, as it immediately pressures and enables Whir recursion.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in Go for the Throat, Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, and additional discard. Against control/combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction, and extra Thoughtseize/Duress; remove creature-heavy cards. Against Amulet Titan: Bring in Rest in Peace, artifact hate, and discard to disrupt their combo pieces. In the mirror: Bring in additional Whir of Invention, discard spells, and small threats to win the attrition war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana for interaction and Whir of Invention triggers. Lantern of Insight is your primary card advantage engine; protect it and use it to dig through your deck. Graveyard hate (Rest in Peace/Surgical) is crucial against combo decks; deploy it early. Avoid overcommitting with small creatures if the Bridge is still vulnerable or if you need mana for recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when the Bridge is safe or when you have enough removal/discard to handle the opponent's response. Hold back if you suspect a sweepers or if you need to preserve mana for Whir recursion. The Bridge is your primary board control tool; use it to dictate the pace and force unfavorable trades.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), artifact destruction (Abrade, Shatter), and graveyard recursion (Reanimate, Flashback). Note when opponents tap out for big spells; this is your window to deploy Bridge or Whir. Track their hand size after Thoughtseize to know what they're holding and predict their next play.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ensnaring Bridge lock combined with small creatures (Knight of the Reliquary, Thalia's Lieutenant, or discard-induced card advantage). Whir of Invention to recur Lantern for infinite card draw if you have enough mana/lands. Attrition through discard and graveyard hate until the opponent runs out of resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the Bridge is established, you have removal for their threats, and you can pressure with creatures or discard to empty their hand. Also commit when you can chain Whir of Invention to generate lethal or win the game via card advantage. Push for the win when the opponent has no sweepers or artifact hate in hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can win before turn 4, if you're locked out of lands, or if the opponent has a hard lock (like a permanent Ensnaring Bridge or a sweepers you can't answer). Also concede if you're down to one card and the opponent has a win condition you can't interact with, or if you're in a dead mirror with no discard or recursion left.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Cast spells at unexpected times (end step, after they tap out). Whir of Invention can be activated in response to counterspells if you have enough mana. Use cheap spells and value engines that are hard to counter or that provide immediate board impact.
