threat_responses.against_ramp: Thoughtseize/Duress to remove ramp spells. Rest in Peace to stop graveyard recursion. Ensnaring Bridge to neutralize their large creatures. Surgical Extraction to remove key cards from the graveyard. Force them to play around your lock by holding up interaction.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early discard to remove threats. Ensnaring Bridge to lock them out. Go for the Throat/Rest in Peace to handle small creatures. Don't overcommit to card draw; stabilize first and use the Bridge to stall until you can close out the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Bridge and small creatures. Use discard to remove key control spells. Whir of Invention to generate card advantage and out-grind them. Force them to use removal on your lock pieces, then recur with Whir. Play around sweepers by holding up interaction or using discard to remove them from hand.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping Thoughtseize/Duress. Use Whir of Invention to recur Lantern for card advantage. Ensnaring Bridge is key; try to deploy it first. Trade removal efficiently and avoid overcommitting to creatures. Control the board state by forcing them to answer your lock pieces while you dig for more value.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your deck to the specific metagame threats. Prioritize Rest in Peace and Surgical Extraction against combo and control decks to neutralize graveyard strategies. Bring in targeted removal and early interaction against aggro decks to stabilize. In the mirror, sideboard in more discard and Whir of Invention to win the attrition war. Always keep your land count optimized for consistency, and consider adding basic lands if you're facing heavy artifact or enchantment hate.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to Whir of Invention without enough mana to protect it or answer sweepers. Another: Forgetting to use Thoughtseize on key combo pieces or removal on critical threats. Unique interaction: Whir of Invention can target Lantern of Insight in your graveyard to recur it, enabling infinite card draw if you have enough lands/mana. Also, Ensnaring Bridge can be targeted by Whir of Invention to recur it if it gets destroyed, but be careful of sacrifice outlets. Always track the opponent's graveyard and hand size to make informed discard and recursion decisions.

philosophy: Dimir Murktide is a tempo-control deck that uses discard and counterspells to disrupt the opponent while building a graveyard advantage to cast Murktide Necromancer and Flow State for explosive, game-winning turns. It excels against combo and midrange decks by out-valueing them with cheap threats and graveyard recursion.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches), at least one early interaction (Thoughtseize, Inquisition of the Deep, Daze, Force of Negation), and a Flow State or cheap threat (Psychic Frog, Orcish Bowmasters, Thought Scour). Mulligan pure land, three-plus lands without interaction, or hands lacking a turn-1 play.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Cast Thoughtseize/Inquisition on key threats (Amulet, Energy, Storm combo pieces, Affinity artifacts). Turn 2: Play a shock land, fetch a basic, cast Daze/Force of Negation/Thought Scour, or drop Psychic Frog/Orcish Bowmasters. Prioritize fetching duals for shock lands to preserve life total against aggro.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in additional counters (Daze, Force of Negation), discard (Thoughtseize), and life-gain/shock lands. Against control/combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm): Bring in graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction), more threats (Psychic Frog, Orcish Bowmasters), and card advantage. Against mirror: Bring in additional counters, discard, and graveyard hate to disrupt recursion.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Thought Scour to fuel Murktide and Flow State while digging for answers. Preserve counterspells for critical threats; don't waste them on non-essential spells. Keep graveyard size in check against hatebear decks, but maintain it for Murktide recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when you can swing with Flow State. Hold back on casting expensive spells if you lack counters/discard to protect them. Use counters to protect your Flow State or key threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo win conditions (Amulet Titan, Ruby Storm, Energy) and artifact acceleration (Affinity). Note when opponents tap out for big threats, as you can counter or discard them. Track graveyard size to anticipate Murktide or opponent recursion.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Flow State casting multiple cheap threats (Orcish Bowmasters, Psychic Frog, Thought Scour, counters) followed by Murktide Necromancer to recur them. Alternatively, Murktide + graveyard shenanigans to overwhelm the board.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ spells/threats in hand/graveyard, enough mana to cast Flow State, and a clear path to victory (opponent low on life, no immediate removal). Don't commit if you're facing graveyard hate or have no way to protect your combo.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold against fast combo decks that can win before turn 4, or against control decks that have established a board you can't break through. Also concede if you're dead to graveyard hate with no way to rebuild, or if you're down to a single land with no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells early. Cast Flow State or threats in response to counterspells to break the stack. Use Daze/Force of Negation to protect your own spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-value them with cheap threats and counters. Thoughtseize to remove key ramp pieces. Flow State to swing before they can cast their big threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early counters and discard. Use Psychic Frog/Orcish Bowmasters to trade efficiently. Flow State to swing for lethal or to clear the board. Don't overextend; play around removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap threats and discard. Use Flow State to out-value their removal. Dig for answers with Thought Scour/Brainstorm. Force them to use resources on your threats while you set up Murktide.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Thoughtseize/Inquisition to remove their counters and threats. Use counters to protect your Flow State. Graveyard hate (Rest in Peace) can shut down their recursion. Out-value them with better sequencing and card advantage.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction ratio based on the metagame. Against combo, prioritize graveyard hate and additional counters. Against aggro, bring in more early interaction and life-gain. In the mirror, focus on resource denial and protecting your own combo. Always keep at least one copy of Thoughtseize and Flow State in the main deck for consistency.
notes: Flow State is your win condition, not just a tempo play. Sequence it carefully: cast cheap threats first, then counters/discard, then Flow State to cast them all for free. Don't cast Flow State on turn 2 unless you have a clear path to lethal. Murktide requires a graveyard, so be mindful of Rest in Peace or Surgical Extraction. Thought Scour is your best friend for fueling both Murktide and Flow State.

philosophy: Rakdos Delirium aims to rapidly assemble four card types in the graveyard to activate delirium, then leverage scalable threats and graveyard synergies to out-value or out-scale opponents. It excels against slower, resource-heavy decks that overcommit, while struggling against hyper-aggressive or fast combo strategies that can race or disrupt the graveyard before delirium stabilizes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing non-foils or duals if available), at least one delirium enabler (discard spell, graveyard filler, or Nethergoyf/Moonshadow), and a threat or interaction. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or 2+ lands with only threats and no enablers/interaction. Prioritize hands that can hit turn 1 delirium or establish board presence by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a discard spell or low-cost delirium enabler to start filling the graveyard. If you have Nethergoyf or Moonshadow, play it on turn 1 or 2 once delirium is active. Turn 2: Drop a second land, cast another enabler or threat. If delirium is active, prioritize deploying scalable creatures. Land sequencing: Play non-basic lands first if they enter untapped, then basics to avoid summoning sickness on key turns.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): +1 Artifact Hate, +1 Discard/Counterspell, -2 Graveyard fillers. vs Control/Combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm): +1 Graveyard Hate, +1 Board Sweep/Removal, -2 Early enablers. vs Amulet Titan: +1 Artifact Hate, +1 Early Pressure, -2 Late-game finishers. vs Mirror: +1 Targeted removal, +1 Hand disruption, -2 redundant delirium enablers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for delirium triggers and threat deployment. Don't overextend into sweepers. Use graveyard fillers efficiently—don't dump everything if you're behind on board. Prioritize keeping hand size above 3 to maintain disruption.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have delirium active and opponent has no immediate removal. Hold back if they have sweepers or spot removal. Use discard to answer key threats before committing.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for artifact acceleration (Affinity/Amulet), combo pieces (Ruby Storm), or blink/evade threats (Jeskai). Note when they tap out for sweepers or counterspells. Track graveyard contents if they have hate.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Nethergoyf (massively scaled), Moonshadow, and Cori-Steel Cutter once delirium is stable. Win by trading efficiently and deploying a delirium-powered threat they can't answer.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When delirium is active, you have board presence, and opponent has no sweepers or spot removal. Go all-in if you can close the game in 1-2 turns.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1-2 cards, opponent has a board wipe ready, delirium is broken by graveyard hate, or you're facing a fast combo that's already at lethal.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Cast threats in response to their spells. Use multiple low-cost threats to force counter trade-offs.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure. Use discard to hit their ramp spells. Don't let them stabilize; trade efficiently and win before they cast their finisher.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and discard. Hold up removal for their key threats. Stabilize with delirium threats that out-scale their board.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure consistently. Use delirium to generate value from the graveyard. Don't overcommit; wait for them to use removal, then deploy threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize targeting their enablers and threats. Use targeted removal efficiently. Control the graveyard by dumping key types when advantageous.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure delirium assembly to matchup-specific disruption. Against fast decks, prioritize early interaction and artifact hate. Against control/combo, add graveyard hate and sweepers. In the mirror, emphasize hand disruption and targeted removal. Always ensure your delirium threshold remains achievable with the new card distribution.
notes: Delirium is fragile; avoid overfilling the graveyard with useless cards if you're facing graveyard hate. Track opponent's removal and sweepers carefully—overextending with delirium threats is the most common mistake. Moonshadow and Nethergoyf scale differently, so prioritize which one to commit based on board state. Cori-Steel Cutter likely synergizes with delirium triggers, so time its activation for maximum value.

philosophy: Grixis Murktide is a discard-driven control-combo deck that uses spell disruption to dig through its deck, find Agatha's Soul Cauldron to generate mana and card advantage, and transition into game-ending threats like Abhorrent Oculus or Murktide Predator. It wins by out-resourcing opponents through discard efficiency and explosive mana acceleration, targeting control, combo, and midrange decks that rely on board presence or specific game plans.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one basic for shock/bloodstained utility), at least one discard spell (Thoughtseize/Inquisition/Duress), and ideally a turn-2 play. Keep hands with Agatha's Soul Cauldron if supported by 2+ lands and a discard. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only non-basic lands and no basics, or hands with multiple discard spells but no lands.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on the opponent's key piece (e.g., Energy token generator, Artifact, Combo piece, or Amulet). Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another discard or dig spell. If you have Agatha's, sequence land, sacrifice a creature (like Murktide Predator or a cheap discard target) to it, draw a card, and ramp. Prioritize shock/bloodstained sequencing to ensure U/B/R access.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Boros Energy/Affinity: +1 Surgical Extraction, +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Duress, -2 Agatha's, -1 Abhorrent Oculus. vs Ruby Storm/Jeskai Blink: +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Liliana's Caress, +1 Surgical Extraction, -2 Agatha's, -1 Murktide Predator. vs Amulet Titan: +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Surgical Extraction, +1 Duress, -2 Agatha's, -1 Abhorrent Oculus. vs Mirror: +1 Duress, +1 Liliana's Caress, -2 Agatha's, -1 Murktide Predator.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping discard in hand for protection and disruption. Use Agatha's to dig for threats or answers, but avoid over-sacrificing if you're low on life. Manage mana to ensure you can cast threats when the board is clear or when you can pay the free-cast condition. Never sacrifice your only removal or threat to Agatha's unless you have a clear path to closing.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection (discard, removal, or board sweepers) or when you can close the game. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or can stabilize. Use discard to remove key permanents before committing. If the opponent is going wide, prioritize Agatha's value and discard over committing a single large threat.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger, etc.), artifact hate, combo win conditions, and blink engines. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or when they're low on resources. Track graveyard contents for Surgical Extraction or Rest in Peace targets. Pay attention to whether the opponent is playing around your discard or saving it for your threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Abhorrent Oculus (untaps for each opponent permanent, attacks for 2 each) or Murktide Predator (enters with X counters, attacks for X each). Win by casting them when the opponent has 5+ permanents (free cast) or by using Agatha's to generate enough mana to cast them and follow up with discard/removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to combat damage, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can protect your threat with discard. Also commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns, or when the opponent is tapped out and you can resolve a threat safely.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked combo that can't be disrupted, if you're down to 1 life with no threats/discard, or if the matchup is mathematically lost (e.g., no way to stop a storm count or infinite combo, or you're completely out of resources against a resilient control deck).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells or key pieces. Cast threats in response to their spells, or use Agatha's to generate mana for multiple threats. Consider cheap threats that can be cast in response to counters, or hold up mana to protect your own spells with discard or removal.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and Agatha's value. Use Rest in Peace or Surgical Extraction to disrupt mana dorks. Out-value them with card advantage and don't let them stabilize; pressure their life total while they're digging for lands.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early discard to disrupt their curve. Use Agatha's to stabilize and draw into removal or threats. Don't overcommit to Agatha's if you're dying to combat; keep life total in check and prioritize survival over value generation.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with discard to disrupt their hand. Use Agatha's to dig through your deck faster than they can control. Cast threats when they're tapped out or when you can protect them. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping Inquisition/Duress/Thoughtseize. Use Agatha's to out-draw. Target their discard spells and key threats. Sideboard Duress and Liliana's Caress heavily. The first player to resolve a threat often wins, so prioritize turn 1-2 interaction and protect your own Agatha's.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a value/ramp focus to a disruption/control focus. Bring in more discard, artifact hate, and graveyard hate against combo/ramp. Remove Agatha's and some threats against fast decks. Adjust land count if needed (usually keep 24-25). Prioritize hands with discard and early interaction. Remember that sideboard cards like Rest in Peace and Surgical Extraction are often win-more in control matchups but essential against artifact/combo.
notes: Agatha's Soul Cauldron is a double-edged sword; sacrificing too early can leave you vulnerable to combat or sweepers. Abhorrent Oculus untaps for each nonland permanent an opponent controls, not just creatures (includes artifacts, enchantments, and even your own permanents if they control them). Murktide Predator's free cast condition triggers when you cast it if an opponent has five or more permanents. Don't forget to use discard to protect your own Agatha's or threats. In the mirror, the first player to resolve a threat often wins, so prioritize turn 1-2 interaction and protect your own Agatha's.

philosophy: Out-grind opponents with efficient interaction and graveyard-fueled recursion, stabilizing the board before closing games with Archon of Cruelty once your graveyard is stocked and threats are answered.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep 4+ lands, at least one early threat (Street Wraith) or interaction (Fatal Push, Dismember, Go for the Throat). Keep Stitcher's Supplier only if paired with a land or early play. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no early plays, or multiple removal spells with no threats/lands.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Street Wraith or Birds of Paradise if available. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Street Wraith if not played, or hold interaction for opponent's threats. Sequence fetches/shocks to ensure black mana on turn 1-2 for Fatal Push, then green for threats. Do not overextend; prioritize board control and graveyard setup.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity: Bring in additional early removal (Dismember, Go for the Throat) and discard (Thoughtseize) to disrupt their mana and early threats. Against Ruby Storm/Amulet Titan: Bring in Blood Moon, additional discard, and graveyard hate if survivable. Against Jeskai Blink: Bring in more interaction, discard, and consider Rest in Peace to disrupt their graveyard recursion. Against Mirror: Bring in Thoughtseize, additional removal, and Blood Moon to disrupt their mana.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve interaction for key threats; do not burn removal on expendable tokens. Manage your graveyard carefully—Stitcher's Supplier and Archon of Cruelty both require it, but avoid filling it with unplayable cards. Use discard to disrupt opponent's hand while preserving your own resources.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal for their answer or when you can trade favorably. Hold back Archon of Cruelty until you have a clear path to cast it or can use it as a sac outlet. Prioritize trading Street Wraith for opponent's early threats to stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, Surgical Extraction) and discard spells. Track opponent's hand for counters or removal. Note when they start casting their win conditions; adjust interaction accordingly. Pay attention to their graveyard size for Stitcher's value.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Archon of Cruelty is the primary win condition. Get it to the graveyard via discard, sacrifice, or death triggers, then cast it. Follow up with Stitcher's Supplier recursion or additional threats to close out the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have answered their key threats, have a clear path to cast Archon, and can protect it or use it as a sac outlet. Commit when you can trade favorably and have interaction left for their response.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are out of interaction, opponent has an unanswerable win condition, or you are dead to the board with no graveyard resources. Concede if you cannot stabilize against aggro/combo or are consistently out-valued in the mirror.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counters, cast Archon at end step or when they lack mana, or use it as a sacrifice outlet for Stitcher's. Avoid casting it into known counters; instead, pressure with early threats and removal.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Street Wraith and hold interaction for their mana dorks or finishers. Do not let them get ahead; use Fatal Push and Dismember to disrupt their acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and Street Wraith trades. Hold interaction for key threats, not just damage. Once stabilized, transition to graveyard setup and Archon of Cruelty to close out the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Grind them out with efficient removal and Stitcher's Supplier value. Force them to answer multiple threats, then close with Archon of Cruelty. Avoid overcommitting; value out their removal and wait for a mistake.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the value war by using discard to disrupt their graveyard and hand. Trade efficiently, prioritize Stitcher's Supplier recursion, and use Blood Moon if available. Out-value them by managing your graveyard better and closing with Archon when they run out of answers.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction curve based on the metagame. Against faster decks, prioritize early removal and discard. Against slower decks, bring in more graveyard recursion and value engines. Always consider bringing in Blood Moon or discard to disrupt opponent's mana and hand. Avoid bringing in cards that hurt your own graveyard unless absolutely necessary.
notes: Archon of Cruelty is a sorcery, so cast it when you can protect it or use it as a sac outlet. Stitcher's Supplier is fragile; protect it or use it as a sacrifice target. Watch for graveyard hate and adjust your game plan accordingly. Do not overextend with Street Wraith; it is a tool, not a win condition. Practice graveyard management to maximize Stitcher's and Archon's synergy.

philosophy: Gruul Basking Broodscale Combo is a fast, disruptive combo deck that aims to resolve Glaring Fleshraker and Malevolent Rumble repeatedly or in tandem to force opponent sacrifices, leveraging Kozilek’s Command for flexible interaction and Basking Broodscale as the combo engine to multiply threats. It excels against midrange and combo decks that rely on board presence or specific win conditions, while struggling against hyper-aggressive decks that can race it before the combo locks in.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands plus at least one combo piece (Basking Broodscale, Glaring Fleshraker, or Malevolent Rumble) and a way to protect or enable it (Kozilek’s Command, cheap acceleration, or removal). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no threats, 2+ lands with only removal and no combo pieces, or hands with 4+ lands. Prioritize keeping hands that can play a land, cast a threat, and have a plan for turn 2-3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, ideally a dual or shock land if the format supports it, or a basic that taps for R or G. If you have a cheap combo piece or acceleration, play it. Turn 2: Play a second land, then cast your first Glaring Fleshraker or Malevolent Rumble. If you have Basking Broodscale, aim to cast it on turn 2 or 3 to trigger or copy the sacrifice effects. Use Kozilek’s Command defensively (bounce a blocker or destroy a problematic permanent) or offensively (deal 5 damage to close out games against low-HP decks).
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity: Bring in artifact hate and additional removal. Against Ruby Storm/Jeskai Blink: Bring in graveyard hate, additional bounce, and direct damage. Against Amulet Titan: Bring in sweepers or targeted removal for artifacts, plus additional protection. Against aggro: Bring in cheap removal, additional bounce, and life gain if available. Against control: Bring in additional threats, card draw, and Kozilek’s Command for removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana should be prioritized for casting threats and holding up Kozilek’s Command for interaction. Don’t overextend into sweepers; keep a threat in hand as insurance. Manage your graveyard carefully—avoid unnecessary sacrifice triggers if you’re trying to combo off, and use Kozilek’s Command to bounce your own threats if needed to protect them from targeted removal.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no interaction or when you can follow up with a second Fleshraker/Rumble. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, targeted removal, or a clear win condition on the next turn. Use Kozilek’s Command to bounce a key blocker or remove a problematic permanent before committing fully.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), targeted removal (e.g., Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and combo win conditions. Pay attention to when the opponent taps out for a spell—they’re likely setting up a counter or a big play. Track graveyard hate if they’re playing against Storm or Blink decks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Glaring Fleshraker and Malevolent Rumble are the primary finishers. Once you can cast them repeatedly or in response to threats, the game is often over. Basking Broodscale enables this by copying triggers or creating additional threats. Kozilek’s Command can finish low-health decks with its 5 damage option.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to resolving a Fleshraker/Rumble, the opponent has no interaction, or you can follow up with a second threat. If you’re at 10+ life and the opponent is at 5 or less, use Kozilek’s Command to finish them.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing a hyper-aggressive deck with no way to stabilize, if you’re locked out of your combo by graveyard hate or mass removal, or if you’re down to 1 card in hand against a deck with a guaranteed win condition on the next turn.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Kozilek’s Command to bounce your own threats or remove counterspells. Cast threats in response to their spells, or use cheap acceleration to outpace their mana. If possible, sideboard in additional protection or alternative win conditions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to disrupt their mana base. Use Kozilek’s Command to bounce or destroy key ramp pieces. If they get ahead, commit to your combo quickly before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Kozilek’s Command bounce or removal. Mulligan aggressively for early interaction. Once stabilized, transition to your combo win condition. Don’t overcommit to trading; focus on surviving to turn 3-4.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and use Kozilek’s Command to remove their key answers. If they have sweepers, wait for them to use it, then commit. Use card advantage to outlast them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize keeping lands and early threats. Use Kozilek’s Command to bounce their combo pieces or remove their threats. Track their hand size and play around their interaction.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Against combo decks, bring in additional disruption and graveyard hate. Against aggressive decks, prioritize removal and life gain. Against control, bring in more threats and card draw. Always keep Kozilek’s Command as a flexible tool, and adjust your land count if needed to ensure consistent mana.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without follow-up, misusing Kozilek’s Command’s damage option when you need bounce, and failing to track opponent interaction. Remember that Glaring Fleshraker and Malevolent Rumble are identical in function, so prioritize casting them in response to threats to maximize value. Basking Broodscale’s combo interaction is key—practice trigger ordering to avoid unintended sacrifices.

philosophy: A four-color combo-control hybrid that uses Birthing Ritual and Cauldron Familiar to dig through its deck for a game-winning threat or combo piece, supported by Chord of Calling for flexible creature retrieval. It aims to outvalue aggressive and midrange opponents while racing or outlasting control and combo decks by assembling its win condition before the opponent can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably basics or duals that fetch basics), Birthing Ritual, Cauldron Familiar, and Chord of Calling. Keep any hand with Birthing Ritual + Cauldron Familiar + 2 lands. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple copies of Birthing Ritual/Cauldron Familiar without lands, or hands lacking early interaction/draw spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Birthing Ritual for 1 to fetch Cauldron Familiar. Turn 2: Cast Cauldron Familiar, let it die to fetch a second creature (often a mana dork, artifact, or wincon piece), or cast Chord of Calling for 1 to fetch a specific threat. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Birthing Ritual on turn 1 and follow up with Cauldron Familiar on turn 2.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity (aggro/ramp): Bring in artifact hate, board wipes, and early interaction. Against Ruby Storm/Jeskai Blink (control/combo): Bring in graveyard hate, counterspell alternatives, and direct damage. Against mirror: Bring in additional Chord of Calling, discard, and targeted removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping Birthing Ritual and Cauldron Familiar in play as long as possible to maximize value. Use Chord of Calling sparingly for key pieces. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary sacrifice outlets unless necessary for the combo. Keep hand size high by using draw spells or ritual effects.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a clear path to the win condition or can protect them with removal. Hold back Birthing Ritual/Cauldron Familiar if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Use Chord of Calling to answer threats or fetch protection.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), targeted removal (e.g., Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and combo pieces that can end the game quickly. Note when opponents tap out for counterspells or protection spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Birthing Ritual + Cauldron Familiar + Chord of Calling to fetch a win condition like Karn, the Great Creator, Thrun, Breaker of Silence, Goblin Welder, or a massive creature. Once assembled, untap lands and cast Birthing Ritual repeatedly to generate infinite value or win directly.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear win condition in hand or can assemble it in one turn, and the opponent has no immediate removal or sweepers. Also commit when you're ahead on board and can close out the game before the opponent stabilizes.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Stasis, Winter Orb equivalents), have no way to break it, and the opponent has a clear path to victory. Also concede if you're down to a dead hand with no lands or combo pieces, and the opponent has a board that can't be cleared.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Birthing Ritual and Cauldron Familiar as instant-speed threats or value engines. Cast Chord of Calling in response to counterspells if possible, or use protection spells. Sequence spells to beat countermagic (e.g., cast Birthing Ritual, then Cauldron Familiar, then Chord of Calling in separate turns).
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use early interaction to slow down their mana development. Prioritize removing key ramp creatures or artifacts. Use Chord of Calling to fetch removal or protection.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early interaction and board wipes. Use Birthing Ritual/Cauldron Familiar to dig for removal or a large blocker. Stabilize by trading efficiently and then assemble your combo.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and value engines. Use Chord of Calling to fetch protection or direct damage. Outvalue them by generating card advantage with Birthing Ritual/Cauldron Familiar.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by going first, having better sequencing, or having more interaction. Use Chord of Calling to fetch removal or protection. Prioritize breaking their combo pieces before they can assemble theirs.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to be more interactive against control/combo and more aggressive against aggro. Bring in additional protection, hate cards, and win conditions as needed. Ensure your mana base can support the new cards.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting Birthing Ritual/Cauldron Familiar without a win condition, miscounting mana for Chord of Calling, and failing to track opponent's removal. Unique interactions include using Birthing Ritual to fetch basic lands for shock lands or fetch lands, and using Cauldron Familiar's death trigger to fetch creatures with mana value 1 for additional value.

philosophy: Hammer Time is a fast, explosive combo-aggro deck that aims to cast Colossus Hammer on turn one or two using mana acceleration and card draw. It wins by overwhelming slower, interaction-heavy, or combo-based strategies before they can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands plus at least one mana accelerator (Mox Opal, Chrome Mox, Arcane Signet, or Sol Ring) and one cantrip/draw spell. Keep hands with Colossus Hammer paired with two sources of acceleration or draw. Mulligan any hand with three or more lands, no acceleration/draw synergy, or only Hammer with no way to cast it.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mox Opal or Chrome Mox, then cast a cantrip (Opt, Brainstorm, Ponder, or Gitaxian Rampant) to dig for acceleration or Hammer. Turn 2: Cast a second mana rock, ensure Opal is active (land + Opal on battlefield), and cast Colossus Hammer if you have the mana. Sequence mana rocks to enable Opal’s one-mana discount as early as possible.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in Lightning Axe, Spell Pierce, Counterspell, and Swords to Plowshares to stabilize and remove early threats. Against control/combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in Rest in Peace, Blood Moon, Red Elemental Blast, and Dispel to disrupt their engines and protect your combo. Against mirror: Bring in Spell Pierce, Lightning Axe, Swords to Plowshares, and additional counters to win the interaction war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping Mox Opal active to reduce spell costs. Use cantrips to filter into acceleration or Hammer, but avoid over-drawing if you’re low on lands. Manage your graveyard carefully if facing Rest in Peace or Blood Moon, as your draw engine relies on it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or the opponent is tapped out. Hold back Hammer if you suspect hard interaction (Red Elemental Blast, Counterspell) and you lack a backup plan. Use removal/counter to clear the board against aggro before going for the kill.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, discard, and board wipes. Note when opponents tap out for interaction or hold up mana on turns 1-2. Track graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Blood Moon) and adjust your draw strategy accordingly. Be alert for combo win conditions that can outpace your Hammer.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Colossus Hammer is the primary finisher, often cast on turn one or two. Secondary finishers include a combination of cheap artifacts, cantrips, and Lightning Axe/Swords to clear the board and go lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana and draw spells to guarantee casting Hammer, or when the opponent has no interaction left. Commit to threats if you can protect them or if going below lethal forces a mistake.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing hard hate (Rest in Peace, Blood Moon) with no way to work around it, if you have a dead hand with no acceleration/draw, or if the matchup is inherently unfavorable (e.g., fast aggro with no stabilization tools). Don’t overextend into known interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Spell Pierce or cheap cantrips to dig around counters. Cast Hammer when the opponent is tapped out or after they’ve used interaction on threats. Sometimes use cheap artifacts as bait to force counters before casting Hammer.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them aggressively. Use removal and counters to disrupt their mana sources. Don’t let them stabilize; pressure early and finish before their late-game threats come online.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Lightning Axe, Spell Pierce, Counterspell, or Swords to Plowshares. Don’t overcommit to Hammer if you can’t clear the board. Use cantrips to find answers and stabilize before going for the kill.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply early pressure to force interaction. Use cantrips to dig for answers or Hammer. Don’t let them stabilize; if they do, use Lightning Axe or Swords to clear threats and go for lethal. Sometimes you must go below lethal with threats to force a mistake.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the interaction war. Use Spell Pierce and Counterspell to protect your Hammer and disrupt theirs. Prioritize removal on their threats and dig deeper for Hammer. Track their hand for counters and play around them with cheap artifacts or timing.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the metagame. Against control/combo-heavy fields, prioritize graveyard hate and hard counters. Against aggro, bring in more removal and spot interaction. In mirror matches, focus on hand disruption and protection. Always keep at least one copy of Colossus Hammer and your core acceleration in the main deck.
notes: Mox Opal requires a land on the battlefield to activate; never cast it without one. Watch for Blood Moon, Rest in Peace, and Red Elemental Blast, as they can completely shut down the deck. Don’t overcommit to threats if you can’t protect them, and always keep a backup plan if Hammer is countered. The deck’s high variance means practice and metagame prediction are key to consistent results.

philosophy: Death's Shadow is a tempo-combo deck that manipulates life total to cast a 7-mana threat for 2 mana, aiming to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize or execute their game plan. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on answering threats or going off, while struggling against fast, resilient aggro and artifact-based strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with a land and a discount spell (Thoughtseize, Inquisition, or similar) or a direct way to cast Death's Shadow (Flow State, Dark Ritual, Cabal Ritual). Keep hands with 2+ lands if they include discard or ritual acceleration. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only high-cost threats, or hands with multiple rituals but no way to cast Shadow.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize on a key answer or win condition. If holding rituals, save them for turn 2. Turn 2: Cast a ritual (Dark/Cabal/Flow State), then cast Death's Shadow. If the opponent has early threats, use Thoughtseize to remove removal or keep hand clear. Land sequencing: Start with a dual land (Underground Sea, Underground River, etc.) if possible, or a basic Swamp. Ensure you can cast the Shadow on turn 2 or 3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in Surgical Extraction, Thoughtseize, or discard spells; remove some rituals if you have enough discard. vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Null Rod), additional discard, and counterspells if the list runs them. vs Amulet Titan: Bring in artifact hate, discard, and early pressure. vs Mirror: Bring in extra Thoughtseize, discard, and artifact hate if needed.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is king; always keep open mana for counterspells or protection if running them, but prioritize casting Shadow. Manage hand by using Thoughtseize to strip answers to Shadow or combo pieces. Graveyard is a resource for rituals; avoid self-mill unless necessary.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Shadow immediately if you can cast it for 2 mana and the board is clear or you have removal. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or hard removal and you lack answers. Use discard to disrupt their development rather than overcommitting to a single threat.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Go for the Throat), hard removal (Path, Murder), and combo win conditions. Note when they tap out for a threat vs. when they hold up mana for interaction. Track their life total carefully, as it dictates Shadow's casting cost.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Death's Shadow attacking for lethal, often supported by discard to clear blockers or rituals to cast it multiple times. Flow State can tutor for Shadow or other threats to close out games.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no sweepers in hand, or you've established a board advantage that they cannot easily reset.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast aggro deck with no early interaction, if you're locked out of casting Shadow due to life total manipulation you can't counter, or if you're out of resources against a control deck with multiple sweepers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells. Cast Shadow at end step or when they're tapped out. Use Flow State to tutor around counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Aggressive discard to hit their ramp spells. Use Thoughtseize to strip key mana rocks. Pressure them before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early Thoughtseize to remove key threats. Use discard to clear their hand. If running protection, use it on Shadow. Stabilize and close out quickly.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with Shadow. Use discard to strip answers. Force them to use resources on threats rather than going off. Flow State can tutor for additional threats or combo pieces.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Track their life total to know when they can cast Shadow. Use Thoughtseize to strip their rituals or Shadow. Prioritize keeping lands and discard spells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure tempo/combo focus to a more interactive game plan. Increase discard and graveyard hate against combo/control, add artifact hate against Affinity, and ensure you have enough lands to cast Shadow consistently. Adjust your sideboard based on metagame shifts, prioritizing cards that disrupt their primary strategy while maintaining your own win condition.
notes: Common mistake: overpaying for Shadow or failing to track opponent's life total. Unique interaction: Flow State can tutor for Shadow or other threats, but be careful of graveyard hate. Always calculate your life total carefully; a single damage spell can make Shadow uncastable. In Unknown format, adapt to specific rulesets, but the core life-total math remains critical.

philosophy: A midrange-combo hybrid that leverages Wrenn and Six for mana acceleration and card advantage, while using Archon of Cruelty to disrupt opponent graveyards and apply early pressure. The deck aims to stabilize the board, disrupt key combo pieces, and close out games by casting Indomitable Creativity to resolve a high-impact spell from the graveyard while generating a Saproling army.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably including fetchable duals or a Wrenn and Six), plus at least one of Indomitable Creativity, Archon of Cruelty, or a hand disruption spell (Thoughtseize/Inquisition). Keep hands featuring Wrenn and Six paired with a land. Mulligan to hands with four or more lands, multiple copies of low-impact spells, or no viable turn 1-2 plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, hold for Thoughtseize/Inquisition, or cast Wrenn and Six if mana allows. Turn 2: Activate Wrenn and Six for colored mana or a 2/2 creature, or deploy Archon of Cruelty if ahead. Sequence lands to ensure red/green mana for Indomitable Creativity by turn 3-4. Prioritize fetching lands to enable Wrenn and Six's abilities and maintain flexibility.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in Thoughtseize, Inquisition of Kozilek, Fatal Push, Lightning Strike, and artifact hate like Shatterstorm or Null Rod. Against Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace, additional disruption, and versatile removal. Against Mirror: Bring in extra discard, artifact hate, and graveyard disruption to out-resolve and disrupt their setup.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Wrenn and Six to fix mana and draw cards, but avoid overcommitting to a single ability. Guard your graveyard carefully; Indomitable Creativity requires a valuable target, so avoid filling it with low-impact cards unless you are committed to the token win condition. Archon of Cruelty should be used to control the board while protecting your combo pieces.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction or a clear path to victory. Hold Indomitable Creativity until you have a game-winning spell in the graveyard and sufficient mana to cast it safely. Use Archon of Cruelty to remove key threats, block aggressive attackers, or pressure the opponent while you set up your combo.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Monitor combo decks (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink) for graveyard setup and hand resources. Against ramp (Amulet Titan), track early mana rocks and large threats to decide when to stabilize. Against control, track removal and counterspells to time your combo resolution. Always count the opponent's available interaction to avoid dead-angling your Creativity.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Indomitable Creativity casting a high-impact spell (such as a large creature, board wipe, or combo piece) from the graveyard, combined with the Saproling tokens, to overwhelm the opponent. Archon of Cruelty can also close games if the board is open and the opponent lacks removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to victory: Indomitable Creativity in hand, a valuable spell in the graveyard, enough mana to cast it, and the opponent lacks interaction. Commit when you can safely resolve your combo or when the board state favors your token army.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a fast combo (Ruby Storm) with no disruption, if you are against a control deck with no way to break through their board, or if you are dead to the board with no comeback potential. Also concede if you are out of resources and the opponent has a clear, unanswerable win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Wrenn and Six to generate mana and draw around counterspells. Cast Indomitable Creativity when the opponent is tapped out or in response to their counterspells if you have priority. Sideboard cards that protect your spells or remove countersources can also help.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Archon of Cruelty and discard to disrupt their mana base. Use spot removal to kill their early threats. Do not let them stabilize; keep the board clear and race them to their late-game finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Wrenn and Six's creature ability and spot removal. Use Archon of Cruelty to block and pressure. Do not overcommit to your combo early; focus on surviving until you can stabilize and transition to your game plan.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Archon of Cruelty and discard to disrupt their hand. Use Wrenn and Six to draw through their removal. Cast Indomitable Creativity when they have no interaction left or when you can safely resolve it past their counterspells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the game of attrition by out-drawing and out-resolving. Use discard to disrupt their combo setup. Prioritize graveyard hate and artifact removal. Win by resolving Indomitable Creativity first or by out-valuing with Archon of Cruelty and Wrenn and Six.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your game plan based on the metagame. Against faster decks, prioritize disruption and removal to stabilize and race. Against slower decks, bring in more combo pieces and graveyard hate to close out games efficiently. Always maintain a balance between interaction, removal, and win conditions. Adjust land counts if needed to ensure consistent mana for your spells, and consider bringing in additional fetch lands if the format demands faster mana acceleration.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Wrenn and Six's abilities too early, filling the graveyard with useless cards before casting Indomitable Creativity, and mismanaging colored mana for the combo. Always track the opponent's graveyard and hand to time your spells correctly. Archon of Cruelty is both a threat and a utility card; use it to control the board while protecting your combo pieces. Remember that Indomitable Creativity's token generation scales with graveyard size, so curate your graveyard deliberately rather than treating it as a free win button.

philosophy: Esper Reanimator aims to cheat high-impact threats into play using graveyard-fueled spells, leveraging discard and card draw to assemble combos quickly. It wins by overwhelming opponents with unblockable or game-swinging reanimated threats before they can stabilize or answer them.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep 7-card hands with 2-3 lands and at least one Thoughtseize or reanimation spell. Keep 6-card hands with 2 lands, a discard, and a reanimation target/spell. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ untapped non-fetch lands, or hands with multiple threats but no discard/reanimation/mana.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if possible to strip counters, removal, or combo pieces. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast a cantrip or discard, or begin mana development. Prioritize fetching for color fixing. Sequence to reach 5-7 mana by turn 3/4 for reanimation.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +1 Ghostly Prison, +1 Anguished Unmaking, +1 Dismember; -2 Reanimation spells, -1 threat. vs Control/Combo: +1 Negate, +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Surgical Extraction; -2 basic lands, -1 threat. vs Mirror: +1 Dismember, +1 Anguished Unmaking, +1 Negate; -2 reanimation spells, -1 threat.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard graveyard resources (cards with flashback, exile, or reanimation costs). Use discard to protect your graveyard and strip opponent's answers. Don't overcommit mana to reanimation if you can't protect the threat.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal for their interaction or when they're low on resources. Hold back if they have sweepers or spot removal; use discard to find answers or set up a surprise reanimation.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, spot removal (Dismember, Anguished Unmaking), and sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Wrath of God). Note when they tap out for answers. Track their graveyard if they play graveyard hate or combo decks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Reanimate Archon of Cruelty to exile their board, or Emeritus of Ideation to draw into more resources and swing. Combine with discard to strip remaining answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 7+ mana, a guaranteed reanimation spell, and they have no spot removal or sweepers in hand. Also commit when they're at 3-4 life and you can swing for lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (Stax, heavy counters), have no reanimation spells or targets, and they have multiple sweepers or graveyard hate. Also concede if you're down to a dead hand with no mana base to cast your spells.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to strip counterspells. Cast reanimation spells in response to their counterspells if you have priority, or use cheap threats to force interaction. Rely on flashback/graveyard value so countered spells still provide value.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early Thoughtseize to strip mana rocks or key ramp spells. Use spot removal to deal with early threats. Reanimate early to pressure before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep early discard and spot removal. Use Ghostly Prison or similar to slow them down. Reanimate a threat to swing for damage while they're tapped out. Don't overextend into sweepers.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with discard and early threats. Force them to use answers on low-value targets. Reanimate big threats when they're tapped out for counters or sweepers. Use flashback spells to maintain card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize Thoughtseize to strip their reanimation spells or key threats. Use spot removal to clear their board. Reanimate threats when they're out of answers. Control the graveyard with Surgical Extraction if needed.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure combo/reanimator focus to a more interactive control-reanimator hybrid. Bring in more spot removal and discard against control/combo, and more early interaction against aggro. Adjust land counts if needed, but keep the mana base flexible for three colors.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to reanimation without protecting the threat or checking for sweepers. Unique interaction: Emeritus of Ideation's death trigger can be used to draw into more reanimation spells or answers. Always prioritize graveyard protection and discard timing. In Unknown format, adapt to local meta by tracking what answers are common.

philosophy: Through the Breach is a combo-control deck that uses cheap, aggressive threats and pump spells to pressure the board, then casts Through the Breach to exile a massive Eldrazi from your library or graveyard. It wins by leveraging Emrakul’s game-ending enter-the-battlefield triggers or Ulamog’s board-clearing and card advantage to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1–2 lands, Kessig Wolf Run, at least one cheap threat (Goblin Guide, Monastery Swiftspear, Krenko, etc.), and Through the Breach. Keep hands with discard (Thoughtseize, Liliana) if paired with a threat or Wolf Run. Mulligan flood, hands with only lands, or hands lacking cheap threats/Wolf Run unless you have multiple discard spells to disrupt early.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Kessig Wolf Run if available, or play a 1-mana threat. Turn 2: Play a land, cast another cheap threat or pump spell. If you have 5 mana and a threat, cast Through the Breach targeting Emrakul/Ulamog. Prioritize setting up a 3/3 or larger threat by turn 3 to pressure and threaten a TtB finish on turn 3 or 4.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against control: +2 Thoughtseize, +1 Liliana of the Veil, -2 lands, -1 Kessig Wolf Run. Against aggro: +2 Lightning Strike, +1 Krenko, -2 discard, -1 land. Against combo: +2 Surgical Extraction, +1 Liliana, -2 lands, -1 Kessig Wolf Run. Against mirror: +2 Thoughtseize, +1 Liliana, -2 lands, -1 Kessig Wolf Run.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a secondary hand; Through the Breach must be cast from the graveyard, so avoid unnecessary graveyard fillers unless they enable the combo. Preserve mana for TtB (5 mana) and interaction. Use discard to remove key opponent cards rather than hoarding it. Track your library for Emrakul/Ulamog and ensure you have enough mana to cast TtB when needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats early to pressure and force interaction, but hold back a pump spell or discard if the opponent has significant removal or counterspells. If you’ve established a 4/4+ threat, you can often commit fully to casting TtB, as the Eldrazi’s ETB will likely win the game or clear the board. Avoid overextending if you lack a TtB backup.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (especially in control), board wipes (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void). Note when opponents tap out for interaction, as this is your window to cast TtB. Track their hand size; Emrakul’s ETB exiles half, so a full hand is a major advantage.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Through the Breach targeting Emrakul, the Aeons Torn (exiles half opponent’s hand, shuffles library, draws cards) or Ulamog, the Defiler (exiles two permanents, draws cards). Emrakul typically wins immediately; Ulamog sets up an inevitable win by clearing the board and refilling your hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a pumped threat, 5 mana available, and the opponent has no counterspells or instant-speed interaction. Also commit if you’re ahead on board and need to close the game before they can stabilize. If you’ve forced the opponent to use their interaction on your early threats, it’s usually safe to cast TtB.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing heavy graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline) with no way to remove it, or if you’re locked out of your combo by persistent hand disruption. Also concede if you’re behind on board against aggressive decks with no way to stabilize or race, or if you have a dead hand with no lands or combo pieces.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells before casting TtB. Cast TtB in response to their counterspells if you can, or wait until they tap out for interaction. If they have multiple counters, prioritize discarding them or using cheap threats to pressure them into using mana defensively.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race with cheap threats and use discard to disrupt their mana development. If they outpace you, cast TtB to exile their permanents with Ulamog or clear their hand with Emrakul. Don’t overcommit to a race if they have heavy ramp; instead, aim to stabilize and win with the Eldrazi.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use cheap threats to race and discard to disrupt their early plays. Cast TtB to stabilize by exiling their board with Ulamog or drawing into answers with Emrakul. Prioritize survival over aggression if you’re low on life, but don’t delay TtB if it’s your only win condition.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early with threats to force them to use removal or counterspells. Use discard to remove key answers (counterspells, board wipes). Cast TtB when they tap out for interaction or after they’ve used their removal. Emrakul’s ETB bypasses board wipes and hand size limits, making it ideal against control.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Track the opponent’s graveyard to know if they have Through the Breach. Use discard to remove it or their Eldrazi. Race with threats and prioritize killing their pump spells or Wolf Run. Sideboard in more discard and graveyard hate to disrupt their combo. Win by going first and establishing early pressure, or by out-resolving their TtB with better hand disruption.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your land count based on matchup speed (reduce against control/ramp, increase against aggro). Prioritize bringing in discard and graveyard hate against combo and control, while adding more cheap threats and removal against aggro. In the mirror, focus on hand disruption and graveyard hate to deny their combo. Always ensure you have a clear path to casting Through the Breach after sideboarding.
notes: Through the Breach is a sorcery, so it can be countered; always time it carefully. Emrakul’s ETB triggers are mandatory and cannot be responded to, making it a hard win condition. Avoid casting TtB if you don’t have a threat to protect it or if the opponent has significant interaction. Kessig Wolf Run is essential for pumping threats and enabling early TtB. Watch out for Blood Moon and other mana disruption, as your deck is mana-intensive. Finally, practice recognizing when to go for the TtB finish versus when to stabilize, as misplays here are the most common reason for losses.

philosophy: Esper Murktide is a resilient control deck that trades early interaction for late-game inevitability. It aims to survive the early game with efficient disruption, then leverage Psychic Frog to recur Murktide Predator and overwhelm opponents with flying tokens and card advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one interaction spell (Fatal Push, Counterspell, or Thoughtseize), and preferably Psychic Frog or a turn-2 threat. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only removal and no lands, or hands with multiple copies of the same low-impact spell. Ideal opening curve includes a 1-drop interaction, a 2-drop threat/land, and a clear 3-turn plan.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then cast Thoughtseize if possible to disrupt combo/aggro, or hold interaction for key threats. Turn 2: Play a land, then drop Psychic Frog or a cheap threat. If facing aggro, prioritize lethal interaction (Fatal Push on 1-2 mana threats). Land sequencing: Start with duals/shocks if available, otherwise fetch/land drop to ensure WUB mana. Do not tap out on turn 1 unless you have a clear disruption plan.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): +1 Surgical Extraction, +1 Rest in Peace, +1 Dismember, -1 Psychic Frog, -1 Murktide Predator. vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): +1 Counterspell, +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Negate, -1 removal, -1 land. vs Mirror: +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Surgical Extraction, -1 extra interaction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Psychic Frog until you have a Murktide Predator or other high-value threat in the graveyard. Do not overextend into sweepers. Use Fatal Push on 1-2 mana threats to preserve hand for later. Manage your graveyard carefully; avoid dumping Murktide into it if you cannot safely cast it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have sufficient interaction to answer their response. Hold back Murktide if they have sweepers or targeted removal. Use tokens to block or trade into removal efficiently.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Murderous Rider), and combo pieces (Tendrils, Storm count, Amulet targets). Note if they are holding up mana for counterspells or spot removal before committing your own threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Casting Murktide Predator from the graveyard via Psychic Frog, then following up with additional threats or pumping tokens. The combination of flying tokens and Murktide's ETB usually closes the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage, they have no sweepers, and you can safely cast Murktide. Also commit when you are at 3+ life and they are at 5+, or when you can trade tokens for their blockers without risking your life total.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If they have a hard lock (e.g., empty the battlefield plus exile effects), you are at 1 life with no interaction, or you have mulliganed to a dead hand with no way to recur threats. Also concede against Amulet Titan if they have a turn-2 kill and you have no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells. Cast threats in response to their counterspells if you have protection. Use Psychic Frog to recur threats from the graveyard, bypassing the stack entirely.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize Fatal Push on 1-2 mana threats. Use Thoughtseize to grab their ramp spells. Do not let them establish a board; trade efficiently and punish overextensions.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play Fatal Push on every 1-2 mana threat. Use Psychic Frog to draw into answers. Hold back Murktide until you stabilize. Use tokens to block and stabilize your life total.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with Psychic Frog and Murktide. Do not let them stabilize with sweepers by committing multiple threats. Use Thoughtseize to remove their key answers and force them to play suboptimally.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Thoughtseize is key to disrupting their recursion. Use Psychic Frog to recur Murktide efficiently. Trade tokens carefully and wait for them to overcommit or make a sequencing error.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a control shell to a more aggressive or hate-bearing strategy depending on the matchup. Against combo, bring in more disruption and graveyard hate. Against aggro, prioritize early interaction and board wipes. In mirrors, increase Thoughtseize and discard effects. Always track opponent sideboarded cards and adjust your play order accordingly.
notes: Psychic Frog's ability puts the creature onto the battlefield, triggering enters-the-battlefield effects. Murktide Predator's ETB creates tokens based on creatures you control other than itself, so dropping it into an empty board only makes 1 token. Do not sacrifice Psychic Frog to recur a threat if you can cast it normally; Frog is better used to draw a card and recur a Murktide or other high-value threat. Be careful of fetch effects that put Murktide into the graveyard; you can target it with Frog as long as it is in the graveyard. In Modern, verify if Bloodtithe Harvester is legal; if not, adjust win conditions accordingly.

philosophy: Jeskai Prowess aims to overwhelm opponents by casting a high volume of cheap instants and sorceries each turn, triggering Prowess to exile and cast spells for free. It wins by out-resolving control, racing aggro, and deploying Prowess creatures that become unblockable or too large to answer, leveraging tempo and card advantage through free spells.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fixing), at least one Prowess creature (Slickshot Show-Off, Cori-Steel Cutter), and 2+ cheap instants/sorceries (Opt, Lightning Strike, Mishra's Bauble). Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no creatures, or only spells costing 3+ mana. Bauble is a keep with any land.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mishra's Bauble (sacrifice for 1R), cast a cheap instant/sorcery (Opt/Lightning Strike) to trigger Prowess. Turn 2: Play second land, cast another instant/sorcery, potentially cast a Prowess creature for free if you hit two triggers. Sequence to maximize trigger count and maintain mana flexibility.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity/Boros Energy): Bring in artifact hate (Shatterstorm, Relic Breaker), targeted removal (Lightning Strike, Go Blank), and life gain. vs Control/Ramp (Amulet Titan/Jeskai Blink): Bring in counterspells (Spell Pierce, Daze), additional Prowess threats, and graveyard hate. vs Combo (Ruby Storm): Bring in discard (Thoughtseize, Duress), removal, and life gain. vs Mirror: Bring in more Prowess creatures, artifact hate, and discard.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Keep mana flexible for instant-speed responses and Prowess triggers. Use Bauble for fixing and tempo, not just mana. Maintain hand size by cycling Opt/Brainstorm. Treat the exiled top card as a resource for future turns.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough Prowess triggers to make them unblockable or too large to answer. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or hard counters. Use instants to clear blockers or answer threats before committing.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), counterspells (Force of Will, Spell Pierce), and graveyard strategies. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for removal. Track how many triggers they've left open.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cori-Steel Cutter with multiple Prowess triggers (haste, large size), Slickshot Show-Off with flying, or other Prowess creatures cast for free. Win by overwhelming the board with unblockable or unanswerable threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ Prowess triggers in a turn, the opponent has no sweepers/counters, or you can close the game before they can stabilize. Also commit when you can cast a Prowess creature for free and it's too large to block.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards with no Prowess creatures or instants, the opponent has a hard board wipe or lock, or you're facing a combo that will win on their next turn and you have no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Bauble and cheap instants to trigger Prowess before casting threats. Cast Prowess creatures for free via triggers. Use instant-speed threats to bypass sorcery-speed counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Pressure early with Bauble and cheap spells. Use removal to deal with ramp creatures. Don't let them stabilize; race them with Prowess threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use removal and counterspells to stabilize. Prowess creatures can race back. Use Bauble to fix mana for instant-speed interaction.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with Prowess triggers. Cast multiple spells per turn to trigger Prowess and exile their answers. Use discard if available. Don't overcommit to one threat; spread pressure.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better sequencing, more Prowess creatures, and better disruption (discard, artifact hate). Prioritize clearing their Prowess creatures and out-triggering them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your instant/sorcery suite based on matchup. Against control, add more counterspells and discard. Against aggro, add artifact hate and removal. Against mirror, add more Prowess creatures and disruption. Always ensure your mana base can handle the new cards.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to Prowess triggers without considering the opponent's interaction. Always leave up mana for counterspells or removal if needed. Bauble is a tempo tool, not just mana fixing. Prowess triggers are cumulative, so sequence spells to maximize free casts. Don't forget that exiling the top card of your library can be a resource for future turns.

philosophy: A resilient control-ramp hybrid that stabilizes early with counterspells and targeted removal, then pivots to a card-advantage engine centered on Omnath, Locus of Creation. It wins by out-grinding opponents through token generation, protection via Solitude, and tempo control with Teferi, Time Raveler, excelling against combo and control while requiring careful stabilization against hyper-aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one fetch/shock or equivalent fixing), a turn-1 land drop, and either Teferi, Time Raveler, Time Raveler, or a cheap interaction/removal spell. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, unfixable multi-color demands, or only high-cost spells without early answers. Prioritize hands that can play a land and cast a 1-2 mana interaction or draw spell on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Teferi, Time Raveler or Time Raveler if mana allows. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast a counterspell or removal, or play a second land. Against aggro/combo, prioritize interaction over ramp. Against control/ramp, develop mana and draw cards. Sequence lands to ensure W/U/R/G availability by turn 3-4 for Omnath, holding up blue mana for protection when possible.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity: Bring in artifact hate, additional removal, and counterspells. Against Ruby Storm: Bring in graveyard hate, extra counters, and early interaction. Against Amulet Titan: Bring in artifact destruction, more removal, and Teferi, Time Raveler copies. Against Jeskai Blink: Bring in exile protection, counters, and graveyard hate. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counters, and sideboard cards that disrupt opponent's Omnath/Solitude setup.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana for Omnath (typically 5-6 mana) and protection spells. Use card draw to maintain hand size and track opponent resources. Treat the graveyard as a secondary concern unless facing recursion engines; prioritize keeping Solitude and Teferi, Time Raveler in play as they provide both protection and removal. Never overextend mana into a single threat without backup interaction.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction or a board wipe ready. Omnath should be played when you can protect it with Solitude or have counterspells/removal to handle removal. Hold back on overcommitting if the opponent has unanswered removal or a combo finisher. Use Teferi, Time Raveler to lock down key permanents and buy time for your engine to stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana development (ramp vs. control), hand size (combo decks often go all-in), and graveyard activity (recursion engines). Key tells: Opponent tapping for multiple colors early suggests ramp/combo; holding up blue mana suggests counterspells; tapping for black/red suggests targeted removal or discard. Track when they tap out for their win condition to time your protection spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Omnath, Locus of Creation is the primary finisher. Once it resolves and enters, follow up with token generation, card draw, and protection. Solitude can exile opponent's removal or threats to secure the win. Teferi, Time Raveler can lock down the board and enable Omnath. Win by out-valuing the opponent through card advantage and board presence.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have resolved Omnath, have protection (Solitude or counters), and the opponent lacks immediate removal or combo answers. Also commit when you have card advantage and the opponent is low on resources. Push for the win when you can protect your board state and the opponent has no clear path to stabilization.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hyper-aggressive deck that has stabilized past your early interaction, if you're locked out of your colors/mana, or if the opponent has a combo that resolves before you can stabilize. Also concede if you're out of resources and the opponent has a guaranteed win condition, or if you're in a dead draw with no interaction or finisher.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split second (if available), cheap interaction, or resolve threats through layers. Play around counters by having backup plans or using protection. If possible, resolve Omnath when the opponent has no mana up. Force them to use resources on you before committing your win condition.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-grind them with card draw and removal. Use Teferi, Time Raveler to slow their mana development. Prioritize removing their ramp sources and finishers. Don't trade up unnecessarily; wait for them to overextend or make a mistake, then capitalize with your engine.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early counters and removal. Don't overextend. Use Solitude to bounce threats. Once stabilized, pivot to Omnath and out-value them. Prioritize survival over tempo, then transition to a control game plan once the board is clear.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and interaction. Force them to use resources on you. Resolve Omnath when they're tapped out. Use card advantage to outlast them. Don't let them stabilize; keep applying pressure and force them to make difficult choices.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize keeping interaction and counters. Use Solitude to exile opponent's Omnath or Teferi, Time Raveler. Win the card advantage battle by drawing more and protecting your own threats. Track their hand size and mana to time your plays perfectly.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against combo/aggro, bring in more early interaction and protection. Against control/ramp, bring in more removal and card advantage. Always keep a balance of counters, removal, and protection. Prioritize cards that disrupt opponent's game plan while supporting your own Omnath/Solitude engine. Rotate in graveyard hate against storm/recursion decks and artifact hate against affinity/amulet strategies.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Omnath without protection, mismanaging mana colors, ignoring opponent's graveyard, and failing to track opponent's hand size. Unique interactions: Solitude can exile permanents from any zone, making it versatile for protection and removal. Teferi, Time Raveler's loyalty counters can be a resource. Always be mindful of the legendary rule if you control multiple Omnaths. Practice timing your protection spells to counter opponent's removal or combo finishers.

philosophy: Naya Energy aims to rapidly accumulate energy counters through mana-producing creatures and planeswalkers, then deploy a heavy, resilient threat base that scales with energy. It wins by out-scaling aggressive decks with indirect damage and pumps, out-racing combo decks with efficient big threats, and out-grinding control with resilient value engines.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetches/duals if available), at least one energy generator (Ocelot Pride, Ajani, Guide of Souls, or energy planeswalkers), and a curve of 1-3. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only 4+ mana spells with no early energy generation, or multiple unplayable turns.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. Turn 2: Cast a 2-drop or hold for a turn 3 energy generator.
