Turn 3: Deploy Ocelot Pride or Guide of Souls, tap for energy, and either cast a 2-energy spell or pump a creature. Sequence lands to ensure untapped entry or fetch basics to avoid slow starts.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity/Ruby Storm): +1/+1 counters, artifact hate, early removal. vs Control (Jeskai Blink): More resilient threats, card draw, protection spells. vs Combo (Amulet Titan): Hatebears, graveyard hate, fast finishers. vs Mirror (Boros Energy): Additional energy generators, targeted removal for opposing Prides/Pariahs, card advantage.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Track energy counters carefully; prioritize tapping generators for energy over attacking if you have a 2-3 energy spell ready. Manage hand size by cycling cheap spells to find answers, and avoid overcommitting energy when facing potential sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough energy to protect them (pump/haste) or when the board is clear. Hold back if facing sweepers or targeted removal; spread threats across the board or use energy to cast evasion to bypass blockers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (board wipes), targeted removal on your energy generators, and combo win conditions. Key tells: Opponent tapping out on turn 3-4 often signals a sweeper or big spell. Opponent playing artifact mana or fast combo pieces signals Affinity/Ruby Storm.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ocelot Pride's 3 damage, Ajani's +2/+2 haste pump, or energy-fueled finishers like massive creatures or direct damage spells. Win by combining energy pumps with evasion or unblockable effects to close out games quickly.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 4+ energy, a board of 3+ creatures, and no clear answer in hand. Also commit when the opponent is at low life and you can close with pump/haste, or when you've out-resourced them in the mirror.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be answered, if you're swept and have no energy generators left, or if you're dead to specific hate cards (e.g., artifact hate vs Affinity, graveyard hate vs energy). Also concede if you're out of resources and the opponent has a guaranteed win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use energy to cast spells in response to counterspells, or play around them by holding up removal. Use multiple cheap threats to force counter trade-offs and pressure the board.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on their key ramp pieces and out-value them with efficient energy generators that double as threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and board wipes. Use energy to pump creatures for lethal or to block key attackers. Don't overextend into sweepers; prioritize survival until you can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats. Use energy to cast evasion or protection spells. Force them to use resources on your threats while you build toward a win condition, and be ready to pivot if they sweep.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the energy race by playing more generators. Prioritize removal for opposing Prides/Pariahs. Use card draw to out-value them and control the board with efficient pumps.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame prevalence. Against combo/aggro, bring in hate cards and early removal. Against control/midrange, bring in more threats and card advantage. In mirrors, prioritize energy generators and targeted removal. Always keep at least one versatile answer.
notes: Common mistake: Over-tapping generators for energy when you should be attacking. Remember that energy generators are also threats. Another mistake: Hoarding energy without casting spells or pumping creatures. Always track opponent's potential sweepers and combo turns. Unique interaction: Energy counters can be used to cast spells from exile or graveyard if the format allows, but generally, just manage them efficiently.

philosophy: Goblins is a high-speed, aggressive tribal deck that leverages mana acceleration and cheap threats to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize. It aims to win against slower, combo-oriented, or control-heavy archetypes by applying relentless early pressure and disrupting key pieces with its B/G splash.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fetchable or duals), at least one mana acceleration/ritual (Boggart Trawler, Birthing Ritual, or Goblin Lackey), and a turn-1 or turn-2 threat (Goblin Guide, Goblin Lackey, or Conspicuous Snoop). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early threats, or hands with only high-cost spells and no acceleration. Dual lands (Steam Vents, Bloodstained Mire, etc.) are essential for B/R/G fixing.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Goblin Lackey or Goblin Guide. If opening with Birthing Ritual, play it, tap for {R}, then sacrifice it to fetch a Goblin (like Lackey or Guide). Turn 2: Accelerate with Boggart Trawler or Conspicuous Snoop to find more threats, then attack with everything. Prioritize playing fetch lands to find duals for B/R/G color fixing.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity: Bring in Duress/Thoughtseize, Goblin Chainwhirler, Goblin Bushwhacker. Against Ruby Storm/Amulet Titan: Bring in Inquisition of Kozilek, Goblin Bombardment, Goblin Sharpshooter. Against Jeskai Blink: Bring in Duress, Goblin Chainwhirler, and additional discard. Against mirror: Bring in Goblin Rabblemaster, Kuldotha Forgemaster, and hand disruption.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your most valuable resource. Never overextend into sweepers unless you have a clear path to lethal. Use Conspicuous Snoop to dig for specific threats or rituals. Manage graveyard carefully if facing discard/recursion hate, and prioritize keeping Birthing Ritual for late-game value or surprise acceleration.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats early to force interaction. If the opponent has sweepers (like Supreme Verdict or Wrath of God), hold back a threat or two to trade into it. Use Birthing Ritual as a one-time value engine to ensure you don't stall, and always track opponent's removal to know when to swing.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Jeskai Blink), sweepers (Boros Energy/Affinity), and combo win conditions (Ruby Storm, Amulet Titan). Key tells include opponent tapping for multiple colors early, drawing extra cards, or playing artifact/creature synergies. Adjust your aggression based on whether they have board wipes or single-target removal in hand.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lethal is typically achieved through combat damage with a flooded board of 1-2 mana Goblins, Kuldotha Forgemaster triggers, or Goblin Bombardment. Birthing Ritual and Conspicuous Snoop help dig for the final threats when the game extends past turn 4.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ mana, at least two threats, and the opponent has no sweepers or removal in hand. Also commit when you can close the game in 1-2 turns, even if it means leaving yourself vulnerable to a single-target removal spell.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Jeskai Blink with multiple counterspells and no disruption), have no lands/acceleration, or are down to 1 life with no threats and the opponent has a clear win condition. Goblins has very little recovery, so recognizing dead hands early is crucial.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple low-cost threats to force counter trade-offs. Play around single counters with redundant threats. Use Birthing Ritual's one-time effect to bypass counters if needed, and save a threat for when they tap out for counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply pressure immediately. Don't let them stabilize. Use discard to hit their ramp spells or key creatures. If they outpace you, prioritize finding lethal damage over board development.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal if splashed, or out-aggress them. Use Conspicuous Snoop to find more threats. If they're faster, you may need to sideboard in more disruption and play a more controlling version of the deck.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early, then dig for value. Use Birthing Ritual and Snoop to find threats past their removal. Save a threat for when they tap out, and be willing to trade creatures to force interaction.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize discard, track their hand, and use Rabblemaster synergy. Trade efficiently and don't overcommit into their sweepers. The player who manages their graveyard and hand disruption better will win.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure aggression to a mix of disruption and value. Against combo/control, prioritize discard and sweepers. Against aggro/ramp, bring in more early threats and hate bears. Adjust land counts if needed (e.g., more duals for B/R/G fixing). Always track the opponent's sideboard strategy and adapt your aggression accordingly.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting into sweepers without checking for interaction. Unique interaction: Birthing Ritual can be sacrificed to fetch a Goblin even if you're low on mana, enabling surprise turns. Conspicuous Snoop can find lands if you're flooded, but prioritize Goblins. B/R/G splash requires careful land management; fetch lands are essential. Always play around the metagame's top combo decks by keeping early disruption in hand.

philosophy: The deck leverages instant-speed exile and return effects to repeatedly trigger enter-the-battlefield abilities, generating disproportionate card advantage and board presence. It excels against combo and midrange strategies by converting tempo into value, while relying on early interaction to survive against aggressive decks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for four-color), at least one instant-speed blink enabler, and a one- or two-mana creature. Mulligan if you have three or more non-basic lands without fetch/shock support, multiple lands with no spells, or only high-cost threats with no interaction or blink spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a one-mana cantrip or early threat. Turn 2: Cast a blink spell on your creature or a cheap threat to trigger ETB, then follow up with another spell. Prioritize establishing board presence with value-generating creatures while holding up interaction for key threats. Sequence mana to ensure you can blink on the opponent's turn if needed.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy/Affinity: Bring in artifact hate, bounce effects, and early interaction (e.g., Disenchant, Cyclonic Rift, or targeted bounce). Against Ruby Storm/Amulet Titan: Bring in graveyard hate, additional counterspells, and direct damage. Against Jeskai Blink: Bring in card advantage engines, additional blink enablers, and targeted removal to break their value chain.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for instant-speed interaction and blink spells. Avoid overcommitting to the board without blink enablers ready. Use the graveyard strategically if the deck includes recursion, but remain vigilant against hate cards.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have a plan to blink them or protect them. If the opponent has removal, hold back high-value targets until you can blink them into safety or answer their interaction. Use bounce effects to temporarily remove aggressive threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, targeted removal, and board wipes. Note when opponents save removal for specific threats. Track graveyard activity if facing combo or recursion decks, and monitor mana development to anticipate their next play.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Phlage, Titan of Fire's Fury is the primary win condition, often deployed after clearing the board or when the opponent has no interaction. Quantum Riddler and Overlord of the Balemurk provide secondary win conditions through repeated value and board swings.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no interaction, or you can safely blink Phlage into a board-clearing ETB. Commit when you've established card advantage and can out-value their remaining resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked combo that can't be disrupted, if you're down to a single unprotectable threat against a board wipe, or if your mana base is completely broken with no way to cast key spells.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple low-cost spells to force counter windows, employ instant-speed interaction to clear the way, and rely on blink effects that resolve as a single spell or use alternative costs. Chain triggers to out-value their counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development, use bounce/blink to return their ramp artifacts/creatures, and prioritize fast threats that can close the game before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and cheap blink enablers to stall. Use bounce effects to temporarily remove threats, then transition to value generation once stabilized. Don't overextend into combat.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with value-generating creatures. Force them to use resources on your threats while you chain ETB effects. Use blink to protect key pieces and out-value their removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-valuing through better card draw, more efficient blink enablers, and superior sequencing. Prioritize protecting your value engines and disrupting their blink triggers with targeted removal or counters.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your game plan to prioritize matchup-specific hate while maintaining a balance of interaction and value generators. Against control, add more card advantage and protection. Against aggro, add early interaction and clock. Always keep a balance of interaction and value generators.
notes: Blink effects resolve as a single spell, making them hard to counter with single-target counters. Be mindful of 'enter the battlefield' vs 'leaves the battlefield' triggers. Don't blink creatures with negative ETB effects. Coordinate your mana carefully due to the four-color requirement; fetch lands and shock lands are critical. Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without a blink enabler ready, leaving you vulnerable to removal or board wipes.

philosophy: Tron leverages a self-reinforcing land engine to generate massive mana on turn one or two, then deploys resilient, game-ending threats that out-value opponents through card advantage and board presence. It wins by out-scaling slower decks and out-resourcing aggressive strategies with unblockable, multi-angle pressure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with two or more Tron lands (Urza’s Mine, Power Plant, Tower) plus a cantrip or acceleration spell. Keep hands with one Tron land and two non-Tron lands if they include a cantrip and a threat. Mulligan on three or more non-Tron lands, or hands with only one land and no Tron pieces or cantrips.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip (e.g., Thoughtseize or Into the Void) to dig for Tron pieces or strip key interaction. Turn 2: Play a Tron land or cast a cantrip to assemble Tron; if Tron assembles, cast Karn or Ugin. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your turn-2 or turn-3 threat while maintaining mana for protection.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in Dispel, Abrade, Surgical Extraction, and Ghost Quarter. vs Control/Combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm): Bring in Force of Negation, Eye of the Storms, Leyline of the Void, and Surgical Extraction. vs Mirror: Bring in Dispel, Thoughtseize, Surgical Extraction, and additional land destruction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize assembling Tron over playing basic or utility lands. Use cantrips aggressively to dig for Tron pieces and maintain card advantage. Manage your graveyard by exiling opponent threats with Karn or Ugin, and avoid giving free targets to reanimation or storm strategies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Karn early to protect your board, exile threats, and draw cards. Hold Ugin until you have a clear board state or need to wipe a stabilized opponent. Don’t overextend into board wipes; Tron’s value comes from consistent, incremental advantage rather than all-in attacks.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for discard spells, counterspells, and board wipes. Note if opponents are holding up mana for interaction on turn 2 or 3. Track graveyard content for reanimation or storm threats, and monitor if they’re trying to stabilize with sweepers or lock pieces.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Karn (exiles threats, draws cards, protects your board), Ugin (board wipe, card advantage, removes problematic permanents), and Emrakul (game-winning threat). Use Karn to exile opponent win conditions and draw into your own finishers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Tron online, a Karn or Ugin in play, and enough mana to cast a finisher while holding interaction. Commit when the opponent has no clear path to stabilize or when you can safely attack through their remaining resources.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re down to one life, have no Tron pieces, and the opponent has a clear path to victory. Also concede against hard counters if you have no way to break through, or if you’re facing a combo deck that can win on the spot with no interaction in hand.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Force of Negation to protect key spells. Cast threats in response to counterspells to force them to spend resources. Use Karn to exile countered spells or draw into answers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use Ghost Quarter or land destruction to disrupt their mana. Play fast threats before they can stabilize. Use Ugin to wipe their board and regain advantage.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use Dispel or Abrade to remove early threats. Play Karn to stabilize and draw cards. Don’t overextend; trade efficiently and use your late-game threats to close out the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats to force them to use resources. Use Karn to dig through their deck and find answers. Force them to interact with your threats while you assemble Tron and deploy game-winners.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use Thoughtseize to strip their Tron pieces and key threats. Use Karn to exile their threats and draw cards. Prioritize graveyard hate and discard to out-value them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw acceleration to interaction and hate cards. Against combo and control, prioritize disruption, counterspells, and graveyard hate. Against aggro, focus on targeted removal and land destruction. Always carry at least one copy of Force of Negation and Eye of the Storms for specific matchups, and adjust your land count if you’re running too many basics.
notes: Tron is highly dependent on land draws; use cantrips aggressively and consider sideboard land destruction to smooth your draws. Be mindful of mana screw; keep hands with at least two lands. Force of Negation and Eye of the Storms are niche but powerful in specific matchups, so use them to protect key spells or disrupt combo decks. Don’t forget to use Karn’s -3 to exile opponent graveyard threats, and save Ugin’s -3 for when you need a board wipe but can’t afford a sweeper.

philosophy: Mono-Green Midrange accelerates mana to deploy resilient threats ahead of schedule, aiming to out-value opponents through card advantage and efficient removal. It wins by stabilizing the early game, leveraging ramp to close out matches before combo or control decks can execute their win conditions.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Noble Hierarch, Badgermole Cub, or a mix of early threats and acceleration. Mulligan flood, stone, or hands lacking a turn-1/2 play. Prioritize hands that can cast a 1-mana ramp creature or play a land + Hierarch.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, tap Noble Hierarch for G. Turn 2: Play second land, cast Badgermole Cub or another ramp creature. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Green Sun's Zenith or threats without stalling. If opening with multiple ramp creatures, consider holding one or sacrificing early to secure land drops.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity/Boros Energy): +2 Artifact Hate, +2 Cheap Removal, +1 Board Wipe. vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm/Jeskai Blink): +2 Graveyard Hate, +2 Removal, +1 Threat Protection. vs Mirror: +2 Efficient Removal, +1 Card Draw, +1 Upgrade Threat.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; avoid overcommitting to ramp once threats are established. Use Green Sun's Zenith to fetch key threats or instant-speed removal. Manage your graveyard carefully, as it fuels Zenith but can be targeted by hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal for their answers or they lack sweepers. Hold back if they have spot removal or board wipes. Prioritize establishing a resilient board presence over raw acceleration.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo pieces (Amulet Titan, Ruby Storm engines), counterspells, sweepers, and artifact acceleration. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or leave mana open for interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large creatures fetched via Green Sun's Zenith, unblockable threats, or direct damage. Zenith can also fetch instant-speed removal to clear the board for a lethal attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you hold card advantage, have removal for their answers, and they are low on life or board presence. Ensure you can survive their interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are stuck on one land, they have a guaranteed combo turn, or you lack removal for their key threats. Don't overextend into known sweepers or counterspells.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast threats at end of turn, use Zenith for instant-speed answers, overcommit with multiple threats, or rely on uncounterable threats if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-value them with efficient removal, disrupt their mana sources, and race if they are combo-based. Don't let them outpace your threat curve.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play early removal, bring in artifact hate, stabilize with efficient blockers, and avoid over-ramping. Focus on surviving the first few turns.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats, use Zenith to fetch removal or finishers, and avoid letting them stabilize. Force them to interact or lose to board presence.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by playing more efficient threats, using better removal, and out-valuing with card draw. Sideboard in针对性 hate and protection.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure ramp to a balanced midrange/control hybrid. Increase removal and hate cards, adjust land counts based on matchup speed, and prioritize threat protection and graveyard hate against combo decks.
notes: Green Sun's Zenith requires sacrificing a land, so sequence land drops carefully to avoid stalling. Badgermole Cub enters tapped, so don't rely on it for immediate combat tricks. Noble Hierarch sacrifices at end of turn, so use it for tempo or to enable Zenith. Avoid overcommitting to ramp if the board is already favorable, and always track opponent mana to anticipate interaction.

philosophy: Jeskai Steel-Cutter is a fast, artifact-centric combo-aggro deck that leverages Mox Opal acceleration to deploy Cori-Steel Cutter and supporting engines ahead of schedule. It aims to overwhelm midrange and control decks with explosive early turns while using Jeskai interaction to survive against aggro and combo.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any 3+ land hand with Mox Opal, or any 4+ land hand with Opal + a turn-2 play (Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student, or a cheap artifact). Keep 2-land hands only if they include Opal, a cantrip, and a turn-2 threat. Mulligan hands with multiple Opals but no artifacts, or hands lacking Opal and a turn-2 play.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mox Opal if possible, or play a cheap artifact/land. Turn 2: Activate Opal for one mana, cast Tamiyo or Inquisitive Student to dig for Cori-Steel Cutter or additional artifacts. If drawing Opal + two artifacts, play both, activate Opal, and cast a turn-2 threat. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for Tamiyo/Student while maintaining artifact count for Opal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Boros Energy/Affinity: +1 artifact hate/enchantment removal, -1 generic removal. vs Ruby Storm/Amulet Titan: +1 counterspell/spot removal, -1 aggressive threats. vs Jeskai Blink: +1 exile effects/boardsweeper, -1 card draw. vs Control: +1 direct damage/finishers, -1 early interaction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping Opal online; it’s your primary acceleration engine. Use Tamiyo/Student to cycle into answers or finishers rather than hoarding. Don’t overextend into sweepers; maintain a board presence of 1-2 artifacts plus your cutter. Manage colored mana carefully—Jeskai can be mana-screwed without Opal.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction or a backup plan (e.g., Tamiyo loyalty, Opal value). Hold back if the opponent has untapped mana and likely holds a board wipe or hard counter. Use Jeskai spells to trade efficiently rather than trading creatures directly.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent’s mana development (ramp vs. control), graveyard activity (combo decks), and board state (sweepers vs. spot removal). Key tells: opponent tapping for two mana on turn 2 often signals a combo or control plan; untapped blue mana late game usually means a counterspell or removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cori-Steel Cutter is the primary win condition, often closing games through direct damage, exile effects, or artifact synergy. Secondary finishers include Tamiyo’s ultimate or combo chains involving Inquisitive Student.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Opal online, a clear path to damage/exile, and the opponent has no known sweepers or counters. Commit when your hand contains a finisher and you can protect it for one turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing a locked board (e.g., Amulet Titan with infinite mana, Boros Energy with unblockable threats) and lack removal or a way to break it. Concede on dead hands (multiple lands, no Opal, no interaction) against fast decks, or when you’re down to one life and the opponent has a guaranteed win condition in hand.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips and cheap artifacts to dig through counters. Cast Opal and threats in response to counterspells if possible. Rely on Tamiyo’s protection or flashback/graveyard recursion if available. Don’t overcommit to one threat; pressure from multiple angles forces counterspells to break.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use spot removal on key ramp pieces. Opal acceleration lets you race their ramp if you stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap interaction and early board stalls. Tamiyo and Inquisitive Student can draw into answers. Don’t overextend into combat; trade efficiently and stabilize before going for the win.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their life total while digging for your win condition. Use Jeskai spells to trade resources favorably. Force them to use removal on your artifacts to buy time for Cori-Steel Cutter.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize Opal and early artifacts to out-accelerate. Use Tamiyo to out-value their card draw. Sideboard in more interaction and finishers. Trade efficiently and punish overextensions.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure combo/aggro curve to a more resilient midrange-control hybrid. Increase interaction and hate cards against the top metagame decks. Adjust your curve to include more one- and two-mana answers, and ensure you still have enough artifact synergy to keep Opal relevant. Prioritize flexibility over raw power.
notes: Common mistake: playing Opal without enough artifacts to activate it, stalling your game plan. Another: overcommitting to the board without considering sweepers. Unique interaction: Cori-Steel Cutter likely scales with artifact count or has a triggered ability that synergizes with Tamiyo’s card draw. Always track opponent’s graveyard and mana for combo tells. Practice mulligan decisions rigorously—this deck is highly sensitive to opening hands.

philosophy: Mardu Energy aims to rapidly generate energy through artifact and vehicle ETB triggers, then leverage that resource to cast high-impact spells or pump creatures, overwhelming opponents before they can stabilize. It excels against slower control and combo decks that overextend, while struggling against hyper-aggressive or highly disruptive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fixing/shock lands or duals), plus at least one energy-generating creature (Guide of Souls, Ragavan, or Orcish Bowmasters). Keep hands with 2+ energy sources and a way to spend it (e.g., Lightning Strike, Fatal Push, or a pump spell). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no creatures, or single-land hands with no fixing or creatures.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap energy creature (Ragavan or Guide of Souls) if mana allows. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Orcish Bowmasters or a second creature, generating 2-3 energy. Sequence to ensure you have enough energy to cast a removal spell or pump by turn 3. Prioritize playing lands that enter untapped to maintain tempo.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Boros Energy: Bring in targeted removal (Go for the Throat, Dismember) and +1/+1 counters to handle pump threats. vs Affinity: Bring in artifact hate (Shatter, Null Rod) and early interaction. vs Ruby Storm: Bring in graveyard hate (Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace) and early pressure. vs Amulet Titan: Bring in early removal (Fatal Push, Lightning Strike) and artifact destruction. vs Jeskai Blink: Bring in exile protection (Grip of the Roil, Soulherder) and direct damage.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Track energy carefully; hoard 3-4 energy for key spells or pumps. Don't overcommit to ETB triggers if you can't spend the energy. Manage graveyard by sacrificing Guide of Souls strategically for card advantage, but avoid giving opponents free targets for graveyard synergies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough energy to answer interaction or pump past blockers. Hold back if facing sweepers or heavy removal; use energy to cast spot removal instead. Prioritize efficient trades to maintain board presence without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana development (counterspells, sweepers, combo pieces). Note when they tap out for a key spell (potential vulnerability). Track graveyard and exile zones for blink or recursion threats. Pay attention to when they hold up mana (likely holding interaction).

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact spells fueled by accumulated energy (Lightning Strike, Fatal Push, or creature pumps) combined with a board of +1/+1 counters or ETB tokens. Ragavan can close games by attacking for 3 and drawing into finishers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 5+ energy, a board of creatures, and opponent has no clear interaction. Also commit when you can safely cast a game-winning spell or pump a creature to lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if down to one card with no energy generation, facing a locked board or infinite combo. Concede if opponent has established a win condition you can't interact with (e.g., Jeskai Blink with infinite value, or Ruby Storm with untap outlets).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split-second or instant-speed energy generation, cast spells in response to their counters, or overcommit with multiple threats to force counter-sacrifice. Use energy to cast cheap, efficient spells that are hard to counter.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt mana development. Use energy to cast removal on their ramp sources. Out-value them with card draw (Guide of Souls) and efficient spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction (Fatal Push, Lightning Strike) to stabilize. Use energy to pump creatures or generate tokens to block. Don't overextend into sweepers; hold up mana for removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient creatures and ETB triggers. Use energy to cast removal on key threats. Out-grind them with card draw and spot removal. Force them to use resources on your threats while you develop your board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw (Guide of Souls) and efficient removal. Track energy carefully; out-value them in ETB triggers. Use Orcish Bowmasters to swing for value. Sideboard in targeted removal and counters to handle their pump spells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against control-heavy formats, bring in more interaction and card draw. Against combo, prioritize hate cards and early pressure. In mirrors, focus on resource denial and efficient removal. Always keep your core energy engine intact; sideboard cards should complement, not replace, your ETB and energy-spending strategy.
notes: Common mistake: Overgenerating energy without a plan to spend it. Always have a spell or threat ready. Another: Sacrificing Guide of Souls too early without card advantage. Track opponent's mana carefully to time your ETB triggers. Energy is a resource that scales; don't panic if you're behind early—consolidate and out-value midgame. Format-agnostic note: If the format has specific interaction, adapt your sideboard accordingly.

philosophy: Izzet Splinter Twin is a fast combo-control deck that aims to assemble a lethal two-card combo while using disruption and card advantage to out-resource opponents. It excels against slower, resource-heavy archetypes like Amulet Titan and Jeskai Blink, but must race or stabilize against faster threats like Affinity and Boros Energy.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fixing), at least one combo piece (Splinter Twin or protection creature), and disruption or a Tamiyo/Flow State enabler. Mulligan to single-color fixing if it delays turn-2 plays, or hands with only threats and no protection/disruption.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Spell Pierce/Thoughtseize if possible, or set up with Tamiyo if mana allows. Turn 2: Cast Goblin Electromancer/Ironshell Crab or Splinter Twin, using Flow State or Fear of Missing Out to dig for protection or combo pieces. Prioritize establishing the combo threat while holding up counterspells/removal for turn-2 interaction.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity/Boros Energy): Bring in additional counterspells, Lightning Bolt, and artifact hate. vs Control (Jeskai Blink): Bring in more disruption, graveyard hate, and direct damage. vs Combo (Ruby Storm): Bring in additional protection, counterspells, and graveyard hate. vs Mirror: Bring in additional disruption and sideboard counterspells.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve counterspells for critical combo pieces or opponent threats. Use Flow State to dig for protection or combo pieces when running low. Manage Tamiyo loyalty carefully to maintain card advantage and protection. Avoid overcommitting mana on non-essential spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have protection or can trade favorably. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or hard removal. Use disruption to delay their game plan while assembling your own.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Boros Charm), hard removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and combo finishers. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up interaction. Track graveyard contents for reanimation or storm threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Splinter Twin paired with a protection creature (Ironshell Crab/Goblin Electromancer) or a direct damage spell. Use Flow State to dig for the final piece or Fear of Missing Out to set up a lethal turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection, enough mana for a lethal blow, and the opponent has no sweepers or hard removal in hand. Also commit when you can out-value them with Tamiyo's ability or Flow State's library advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control deck with no removal, if your combo pieces are consistently countered, or if you're down to a dead hand against a stabilized aggro board. Also concede if the opponent has a guaranteed win condition you can't interact with.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Fear of Missing Out to dig around counters, cast threats at end step, or use Tamiyo to protect key pieces. Employ cheap, uncounterable threats or split your combo across multiple turns.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race their big spells. Use disruption to steal their mana rocks or counterspell their ramp spells. Prioritize removing their key ramp pieces before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with counterspells, Lightning Bolt, and Tamiyo's loyalty abilities. Use Flow State to dig for removal or protection. Trade efficiently and don't overcommit to the combo until you've stabilized the board.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and disruption. Use Tamiyo to out-value their card draw. Dig through their deck with Flow State and Fear of Missing Out to find combo pieces or direct damage. Force them to use resources on interaction rather than their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by out-drawing with Tamiyo and Flow State. Prioritize disruption to steal their combo pieces or counter their key spells. Control the board with efficient removal and counterspells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction suite based on the metagame. Against control, bring in more graveyard hate and direct damage. Against aggro, prioritize artifact hate and additional counterspells. In mirrors, sideboard in additional disruption and protection. Always keep your core combo pieces and Tamiyo/Flow State enablers, but swap in format-specific hate based on the top archetypes.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the combo without protection. Always hold up interaction for turn-2 threats. Flow State requires careful sequencing—don't cast it without a plan to dig for protection or combo pieces. Tamiyo's loyalty abilities are crucial for card advantage and protection, so don't exhaust them prematurely. Fear of Missing Out is best used to set up your next turn or dig for a specific piece. Watch for opponent's sideboard cards, especially artifact hate against Affinity and sweepers against control.

philosophy: Izzet Phoenix is a fast, graveyard-fueled combo strategy that uses cheap cantrips, discard, and mana acceleration to cast Arclight Phoenix for free or at a steep discount, aiming to close games in 2-4 turns. It excels against slow, resource-heavy decks but struggles against aggressive matchups and dedicated graveyard hate.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or fetchable basics), at least one cheap cantrip (Gitaxian Probe, Opt, Manamorphose), and a discard outlet. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no cheap spells, or only high-cost threats. In a 7-card mulligan, keep 3+ lands if you have multiple cheap spells or discard effects.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Gitaxian Probe on your opponent's turn, or cast Manamorphose for mana/ramp. Turn 2: Fetch a dual land, cast cheap cantrips to dig for Phoenix or discard, or sequence Manamorphose into a cheap spell. Prioritize lands that enter untapped or can be fetched to ensure blue/red mana availability for Probe and Phoenix.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in Lightning Strike, Abrade, Red Elemental Blast, and Surgical Extraction. vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in Thoughtseize, Negate, Spell Pierce, and graveyard hate like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void. vs Mirror: Bring in additional discard, graveyard hate, and spot removal to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize graveyard consistency over board presence. Keep cheap spells in hand to dig for Phoenix or disrupt, and avoid overcommitting to cantrips if you're low on life. Manage mana carefully to ensure you can cast Probe/Phoenix on the correct turn without flooding the board.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back threats until you have a clear path to cast Phoenix or have enough removal to handle interaction. Commit when you have graveyard setup and can answer interaction, but avoid overextending into board wipes or sweepers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline, Surgical Extraction), counterspells, and discard. Note if the opponent is playing fast lands or ramp to gauge if they can race you. Track their hand size to know when they're tapped out for interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Arclight Phoenix (cast for free or with Manamorphose), backed by spot removal and burn. Gut Shot can finish off low-life opponents or trade efficiently in the late game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have 2+ cards in the graveyard, can cast Probe/cheap spells, and the opponent has no graveyard hate or counterspells in play. Go all-in if you can close the game in 1-2 turns or if the opponent is at lethal range.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If the opponent has Rest in Peace/Leyline in play and you have no way to remove it, or if you're facing a fast aggro deck with no removal and low life. Also concede if you're completely dead to counterspells with no backup plan or graveyard access.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Manamorphose for mana/ramp, cast Probe on the opponent's turn, use discard to remove counterspells, or cast Phoenix when they're tapped out. Sideboard Negate/Spell Pierce to protect your key spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use discard to hit their ramp sources, and close games before they stabilize. Gut Shot can finish off low-life ramp players, and Manamorphose helps you race their mana acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize removal (Lightning Strike, Abrade) and life gain if available. Use discard to hit their threats. Don't overextend into board wipes or sweepers, and focus on stabilizing before attempting to combo off.
threat_responses.against_control: Dig deeper with cantrips, use discard to remove key answers, and pressure with Phoenix. Force them to use resources on graveyard hate or let you win by out-graveyarding them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war, control the graveyard, and out-value with better sequencing. Gut Shot can trade efficiently, and prioritizing Probe/cheap spells ensures you reach your win condition first. Track their graveyard to know when to hold up removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure combo to a more interactive midrange/control shell. Bring in hate cards for the metagame, adjust land counts if needed, and prioritize cards that can handle the top archetypes (Boros Energy, Affinity, Ruby Storm, Amulet Titan, Jeskai Blink). Keep the deck flexible to adapt to matchup-specific threats, and consider bringing in additional discard or graveyard hate depending on what you see in the first game.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to cantrips without a win condition, ignoring graveyard hate, and mismanaging mana for Probe/Phoenix sequencing. Manamorphose is crucial for mana fixing and ramp, while Gut Shot serves as niche burn/removal. Demilich is highly atypical for Phoenix; if included, it likely changes the game to a graveyard-focused wincon, but be aware it's vulnerable to exile-based hate and may conflict with your core strategy. Always track opponent's graveyard and hand size, and never assume your win condition will resolve without backup.

philosophy: The deck aims to generate massive mana using Devoted Druid and Delighted Halfling, then use Green Sun's Zenith to tutor for a combo finisher to win through infinite mana, mill, or library exile. It targets slower, combo, and control decks while struggling against fast aggro.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands plus Devoted Druid, Delighted Halfling, or Green Sun's Zenith. Keep any hand with Druid + a land. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only non-creature spells, or hands lacking Druid/Halfling/GSZ unless they contain multiple lands and a clear path to combo.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, pass. Turn 2: Play Devoted Druid, tap for G. Turn 3: Play Delighted Halfling, untap Druid, tap for GG. Turn 4: Cast Green Sun's Zenith for X=2 to tutor for a combo piece or ramp spell. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for GSZ and finisher.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in discard (Thoughtseize, Duress), spot removal (Fatal Push, Go for the Throat), and protection (Abrupt Decay, Natural Order). vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Surgical Extraction), counterspell disruption, and additional combo pieces. vs Mirror: Bring in additional tutors, discard, and hate for combo pieces.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping Devoted Druid and Delighted Halfling on board. Use GSZ efficiently to find the exact finisher needed. Don't overcommit mana if the opponent has interaction. Manage graveyard carefully if playing against discard/hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on casting the finisher until you have protection or can resolve it through interaction. Use discard to clear key threats. Don't attack with Halfling if it exposes you to removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, discard spells, and removal targeting Druid/Halfling. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or have graveyard hate. Track if they're playing around your combo with specific answers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Karn, the Great Creator (exile opponent's library), Bolas's Citadel (mill), or Thassa's Oracle (draw opponent's library). Assemble via Druid/Halfling mana + GSZ tutor.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 5+ mana, a clear path to resolve the finisher, and no immediate interaction from the opponent. If you can tutor for the finisher and have protection, commit.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one land, have no combo pieces, and the opponent is ahead on board. Concede if you're facing a fast aggro deck with no interaction or removal, or if the opponent has graveyard hate and you can't assemble the combo.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use GSZ's flash-in capability if possible, or tutor for a finisher that doesn't rely on the stack. Use discard to remove counterspells. Consider casting the finisher in response to their spell if you have priority.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use discard to remove ramp spells. Use removal on key ramp creatures. Don't let them get ahead on mana; pressure them with early plays or discard.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize discard and spot removal. Use Halfling to generate mana for answers. Don't overextend; stabilize with removal and discard, then combo out.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early plays. Use discard to remove key control spells. Tutor for the finisher and resolve it through interaction. Use graveyard hate if they rely on it.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use discard to remove their tutors. Protect your Druid/Halfling. Out-value them with better sequencing and sideboard cards.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Against combo/control, bring in graveyard hate and counterspell disruption. Against aggro, bring in discard and removal. Against mirror, bring in additional tutors and hate. Always keep at least one copy of your main combo pieces.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to GSZ without a clear finisher. Another: Forgetting that Delighted Halfling's mana generation requires untapping Druid. Watch out for 'Stifle' or 'Surgical Extraction' on your graveyard. Green Sun's Zenith can target creatures in the graveyard, so be mindful of your own graveyard.

philosophy: Azorius Control aims to survive the early game with efficient interaction and conditional board wipes, stabilize with Supreme Verdict and Solitude, and close out games with resilient flying threats like Kaheera, the Orphanguard. It excels against fast, combo-heavy, and aggressive decks that lack answers to flying threats and one-sided wipes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing untapped or dual lands), a turn-1 or turn-2 removal spell, and a board wipe or Kaheera. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no spells, hands with only removal and no land/early interaction, or hands lacking a way to stabilize against early aggression.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap interaction or pass. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a removal spell or counter, or develop mana for a turn-3 play. Prioritize untapped lands for flexibility. If drawing Kaheera, hold for turn 3-4 to ensure it's protected or paired with bounce/removal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy/Affinity): In 2-3 additional removal, 1-2 Solitude, 1-2 Path to Exile. Out 1-2 counters, 1-2 Kaheera. vs Combo (Ruby Storm/Amulet Titan): In 2-3 Solitude, 1-2 Supreme Verdict, 1-2 additional counters. Out 1-2 Kaheera, 1-2 removal. vs Control/Mirror: In 1-2 Kaheera, 1-2 additional removal, 1-2 counters. Out 1-2 Solitude, 1-2 board wipes.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn 4. Avoid overcommitting to the board before you have a clear path to victory. Use the graveyard strategically for bounce effects or as a resource for removal, and track opponent hand size to anticipate combo or control plays.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on committing Kaheera until you have a bounce spell or removal ready. Use Solitude on the opponent's most threatening creature, keeping yours if possible. Supreme Verdict is a hard reset; use it when you're ahead on board or when they commit multiple threats simultaneously.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana development (ramp decks), hand size (combo decks), and whether they're holding up interaction for your threats. Note if they're playing around Solitude by keeping a creature in hand, and track whether they've blown counters on earlier spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Kaheera, the Orphanguard is the primary win condition. Follow up with bounce or removal to protect it, or use it to block flying threats while chipping in damage. Secondary finishers include resilient creatures or direct damage if available.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a resolved Kaheera, sufficient removal to handle their answer, and enough mana to protect it or follow up. Also commit when they've exhausted their interaction on earlier threats or when you can safely resolve a second threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 cards, they have a guaranteed win condition in hand or on board, or you're facing a combo deck that has assembled its win and you lack a hard wipe or lock. Also concede if you're in a dead mirror with no sideboard advantage.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Sequence spells to force counterspells, then follow up with uncounterable threats or bounce/removal that bypasses the stack. Use Solitude on a creature they can't protect, or play around their main counter with a second threat.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early interaction to disrupt their mana development. Use Solitude to remove key ramp creatures or mana dorks. Hold a board wipe for when they commit multiple threats, and avoid overcommitting to the board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Flood the early game with removal and counters. Use Solitude on their most threatening creature. Stabilize with Verdict if they flood the board, then transition to Kaheera once stabilized.
threat_responses.against_control: Trade resources efficiently and avoid overextending. Use Kaheera to pressure while holding up interaction. Force them to use resources on your threats while you preserve yours, and look for value exchanges that swing card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the attrition war through better sequencing and sideboard positioning. Use Solitude to remove their threat while keeping yours, save counters for their key spells, and prioritize card advantage through efficient removal and bounce effects.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a reactive stance to a more proactive one against combo and ramp by bringing in targeted removal and Solitude. Against control and mirrors, increase threat density and counterspells. Always ensure your mana base can handle the new card distribution, and adjust your play pattern to match the new matchup dynamics.
notes: Kaheera is vulnerable to non-flying removal and board wipes; always have a bounce or protection spell ready. Solitude's conditional nature means you must evaluate whether keeping your creature is worth more than removing theirs. Avoid overcommitting to the board before turn 5 unless you have a clear path to victory. Common mistake: playing Kaheera too early without protection, leading to easy removal. Always track whether opponents are playing around Solitude by keeping creatures in hand.

philosophy: Rakdos Midrange seeks to outvalue opponents by leveraging efficient removal, card advantage engines, and resilient threats to control the board while steadily closing out games. It excels against combo and control decks that overextend or lack interaction, while relying on speed and disruption to survive against aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/shocks or basic swamps/stacks for fixing), at least one removal spell (e.g., Fatal Push, Go for the Throat), and a threat or card draw engine. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no plays, hands with only removal and no threats/lands, or hands with 0-1 lands. Keep hands that can play a turn-1 land and a turn-2 threat or interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. If you have a cheap threat or discard spell and the opponent is aggressive, consider playing it to disrupt their curve. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast your 2-drop threat, or use removal on an early aggressive threat. Land sequencing prioritizes fixing to ensure you can cast your 2-drop and 3-drop on curve without flooding. Always prioritize playing a land over casting a spell if you are one mana short of a key play.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in direct damage, cheap removal, and artifact hate. vs Control/Combo (Ruby Storm, Jeskai Blink): Bring in graveyard hate, additional card draw, and threat protection. vs Amulet Titan: Bring in artifact hate, sweepers, and early interaction. vs Mirror: Bring in additional removal, discard, and threat protection. Generally, sideboard in hate cards that target the most common win conditions in the top 5 metagame archetypes.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize card advantage engines and efficient removal. Don't overcommit mana on single-target spells if a board wipe or sweep is available. Manage graveyard resources carefully if the format has graveyard hate, and avoid casting unnecessary spells that could be targeted by discard or removal. Always maintain a hand of 3-4 cards to weather interaction.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal to answer opponent's interaction or when they have no sweepers. Hold back if you suspect a board wipe or heavy removal. Trade efficiently: use removal on threats that are more impactful than your current board state, and avoid overextending into known interaction. Use discard to remove key combo pieces or counterspells from hand.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counters, and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents are holding up mana for interaction vs. developing their board. Track key combo pieces or win conditions, and adjust your aggression accordingly. Pay attention to when they tap out for a single spell, as this often indicates they are vulnerable to a threat or discard.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact threats, card advantage engines that draw into game-ending spells, or direct damage spells. Win by out-resourcing and applying pressure when the opponent has no answers. Use discard to remove their last interaction, then commit your remaining threats to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage, removal for their interaction, and they have no sweepers or hard answers. Go all-in when you can close the game in 1-2 turns, or when you have enough threats to overwhelm their remaining blockers. Commit when you've successfully disrupted their combo or control game plan.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1-2 cards with no threats, facing a combo deck that can combo off, or if you're completely locked out of the game by a dominant board state with no removal. Concede if you have no path to out-value them or disrupt their win condition, and you're running out of resources.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter, or cast spells when they have no mana up. Use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Avoid overcommitting to a single threat; have multiple lines of attack to pressure them into making mistakes.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use efficient removal to kill their ramp spells or early threats. Don't overextend into their late-game finishers; stabilize with removal and discard, then close the game before they can deploy their win conditions.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal and discard. Don't try to out-value them early; focus on surviving until you can stabilize and close the game. Use threat protection and sweepers if available, and prioritize killing their most impactful threats first.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with resilient threats. Use card advantage to out-grind them. Don't overcommit to a single threat; have multiple lines of attack. Use discard to remove their counterspells or sweepers, and pressure them into making mistakes with their limited interaction.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card advantage, efficient removal, and threat protection. Control the board with sweepers if available. Use discard to remove their key threats or interaction, and avoid overcommitting to the board without answers. The player who manages resources better and makes fewer mistakes will win.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to be more interactive against combo/control, or more aggressive against slow decks. Ensure you have enough land for your new spell loadout, and consider adding basic lands if you're bringing in too many non-basic spells. Prioritize hate cards that target the most common win conditions in the metagame, and remove cards that are dead against specific archetypes. Always sideboard in at least 2-3 cards per matchup to maintain deck consistency.
notes: The listed signature cards (Orcish Bowmasters, Moonshadow, Burning Inquiry) do not match the B/R color identity of Rakdos Midrange and likely indicate a decklist error. Verify your card list and ensure all cards are legal in your format. Standard Rakdos Midrange relies on efficient removal, card draw, resilient threats, and discard. Avoid overcommitting to the board without answers, and prioritize card advantage in longer games. Practice mulligan decisions carefully, as Rakdos Midrange is highly sensitive to early mana and card quality.

philosophy: A four-color midrange-control hybrid that leverages artifact synergy, life gain, and recursive threats to outlast fast combo and aggro decks, ultimately closing games with Asmoranomardicadaistinaculdacar or resilient board states powered by Persist.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including duals or fetches), a turn-1 land drop, and either a turn-2 Archon of Cruelty, a mana accelerator, or a Persist enabler. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, hands with only single-color sources and no fixers, or hands lacking any interaction or early threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic or dual land, cast a mana dork or artifact ramp if available. Turn 2: Develop your mana base, drop Archon of Cruelty if ahead, or play a Persist creature to establish board presence. Prioritize fixing over speed; four-color consistency is critical to hitting your key spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Boros Energy, Affinity, Ruby Storm, and Amulet Titan: Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and artifact hate to disrupt their acceleration and combo pieces. Against Jeskai Blink: Bring in card advantage, protection spells, and additional finishers to out-value their blink loops. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and graveyard hate to disrupt Persist recursion.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for casting threats versus holding up interaction. Use Food artifacts for card advantage and life gain to stabilize against aggressive strategies. Preserve Persist creatures by protecting them or maintaining recursion resources in hand and graveyard.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Archon of Cruelty when you can protect it or have removal ready for opposing threats. Hold back Asmo until you have a clear path to victory, sufficient protection, or the opponent is tapped out for a combo win. Trade efficiently; do not overextend into spot removal or blink effects.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo pieces (Energy counters, Storm counters, Amulet activations) and blink triggers. Note when opponents tap out for key spells; this is your window to attack, cast Asmo, or deploy a Persist enabler. Track graveyard contents to anticipate recursion plays.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Asmoranomardicadaistinaculdacar is the primary win condition, often cast for free or reduced cost. Archon of Cruelty combined with Persist recursion creates an unblockable or resilient threat that forces multiple answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, enough mana to protect Asmo, or when the opponent is tapped out for a combo win. Commit to attacking with Archon and Persist creatures when you can trade favorably or force the opponent to use multiple answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to one life, have no interaction, and the opponent has a guaranteed combo win or insurmountable board state. Also concede if your hand is completely dead against the current matchup or if you cannot stabilize against persistent aggro pressure.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips, split casts, or cast Asmo during the opponent's upkeep when they are less likely to have counterspells. Utilize protection spells, free-cast effects, or sacrifice outlets to bypass counters and establish board presence.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use artifact hate and board wipes to slow their acceleration. Prioritize life gain and removal to survive their early game while outvaluing them midgame.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize life gain and early removal. Use Archon of Cruelty to stabilize and gain life. Do not overcommit to expensive spells early; focus on surviving until your late-game finishers can close the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats. Use Persist to recur creatures and force them to use multiple answers. Out-value them with card advantage and late-game finishers, forcing them to make difficult trade decisions.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize graveyard hate to disrupt Persist recursion. Use targeted removal to eliminate key threats. Control the board with wipes and win with Asmo or superior card advantage. Track opponent's hand size and graveyard to anticipate their moves.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the metagame. Against fast decks, increase removal and hate cards. Against control, add card draw and protection. Always ensure your mana base can handle the sideboarded cards, and consider bringing in additional fixers if needed. Adjust your game plan to prioritize survival against aggro and value generation against control.
notes: Asmo's casting cost or commander tax can be mitigated with specific effects; plan your mana accordingly. Persist creatures should be protected or recurred efficiently to maximize value. Watch for format-specific hate cards and adjust your mulligan strategy to avoid dead draws. Practice timing your Asmo cast to avoid counter spells and blink effects.

philosophy: A Rakdos midrange-tempo engine built around Ragavan, Nimble Pilferer to generate card advantage and fuel disruption. It aims to out-value opponents through efficient threats, discard, and flexible removal, capitalizing on metagame matchups against slower or combo-dependent decks while applying enough pressure to punish aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Ragavan, and at least one interaction (Thoughtseize, Fatal Push, or Lightning Axe). Prioritize hands with Ragavan + discard over pure curve-balls. Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no early plays, or hands with zero interaction when facing fast aggro.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize if you have a 2-drop threat or Ragavan in hand. Turn 2: Ragavan into a land, or discard into a threat. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Ragavan on turn 2 and follow up with a cantrip or discard. If facing fast decks, prioritize early interaction over optimal mana.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Boros, Affinity): +1 Bloodtithe Harvester, +1 Fatal Push/Go Blank, -2 discard. vs Control/Combo (Jeskai, Ruby): +1 Duress, +1 Lightning Axe/Infernal Grasp, -2 discard. vs Amulet Titan: +1 Go Blank, +1 Lightning Axe, -2 discard. Mirror: +1 Thoughtseize, +1 Fatal Push, -2 lands or extra threats.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Ragavan's card draw fuels your hand; don't overextend without answers. Use discard to protect your board and disrupt opponent's key pieces. Manage mana to keep removal/discard available for 2-3 mana threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Ragavan early to establish pressure and draw into answers. Hold back if opponent has clear removal or a board wipe ready. Trade efficiently with Super Shredder and Moonshadow, using their ETB/attack triggers to chip in or remove blockers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (Jeskai), artifact acceleration (Affinity/Amulet), and combo win conditions (Ruby). Key tells: opponent tapping out on turn 2/3, holding up mana, or playing non-basic lands that signal specific strategies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ragavan + efficient threats (Moonshadow, Super Shredder, Bloodtithe Harvester). Win by out-valuing through card advantage and applying consistent pressure.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have card advantage, opponent is low on removal, or you've established board control. Go all-in if you can close the game before they can stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're facing a locked combo (Ruby Storm) with no interaction, or if you're completely outclassed in a control mirror with dead hands. Fold if you have no way to interact and opponent is untouchable.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells. Cast Ragavan and threats in response to or after they tap out. Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter (or cast them when they're tapped out).
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early interaction is key. Thoughtseize to find Amulet or ramp spells. Use removal to kill early threats. Super Shredder can clear artifacts.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early removal and discard. Ragavan can trade efficiently. Don't overcommit; stabilize with cheap answers, then close with value.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats. Use discard to find gaps. Ragavan's card draw helps you out-value their control elements. Push damage while they're setting up.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping hands with multiple threats and interaction. Use Ragavan to out-value. Sideboard in more discard and efficient removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure midrange to matchup-specific tuning. Against fast decks, prioritize early interaction and clock. Against control/combo, add more disruption and late-game value. Always carry at least one artifact hate and one combo hate. Adjust land counts if needed, but keep the core Ragavan engine intact.
notes: Common mistake: Overextending with Ragavan without protection. Always have an answer ready. Another: Forgetting that Super Shredder and Moonshadow synergize well with artifact synergies if the deck runs any, but even without, they're efficient. Watch out for board wipes; Ragavan's value is lost if wiped without follow-up. Keep discard flexible to hit different strategies.

philosophy: Green Devotion is a ramp-combo archetype that leverages early mana acceleration and Green Sun’s Zenith to deploy game-winning threats or execute a Fungal Bloom + Frenzied Baloth mana combo, aiming to close out games before interactive decks can stabilize. It dominates slower, control-oriented matchups but struggles against fast aggro and highly disruptive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Noble Hierarch, and a Green Sun's Zenith or early threat. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple Noble Hierarchs without lands, or hands lacking acceleration/early interaction. Prioritize hands that can play a land, cast Hierarch, and have a turn 2-3 plan.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Noble Hierarch. Turn 2: Tap Hierarch for mana, play a second land, cast Green Sun's Zenith targeting Tarmogoyf, Scavenging Ooze, or a key threat. If drawing extra lands, hold Zenith for bigger targets. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your spells on curve.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Affinity, Boros Energy): Bring in Thoughtseize/Duress, Fatal Push, and artifact hate. Against control/combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm): Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal, and sideboard cards that disrupt their game plan. Against mirror: Bring in additional threats and discard to disrupt their Zenith targets.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is critical; always ensure you have enough green for Green Sun's Zenith and your threats. Use the graveyard strategically (Zenith puts creatures directly into play, so manage your graveyard for recursion or protection). Don't overextend into removal; hold back threats if you lack interaction.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a clear path to victory or when the opponent is low on interaction. Hold back if they have sweepers or spot removal. Use discard to remove key threats or answers before they can be cast.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells, sweepers, and removal. Pay attention to when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for interaction. Note if they're playing lands that suggest a specific strategy (e.g., artifact lands for Affinity, snow for Blink).

endgame_tactics.finishers: Green Sun's Zenith into a game-winning threat (Tarmogoyf, Scavenging Ooze, or Frenzied Baloth if comboing), or a direct damage/combo finish. Use discard to remove the last answer.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to victory, the opponent has no interaction, or you're at lethal. Don't commit if they have sweepers or removal in hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life, have no threats, and the opponent has a clear win condition or board presence you can't answer. Also concede if you're dead to the matchup (e.g., no interaction against a locked board).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells. Cast Green Sun's Zenith in response to their counterspells if possible, or use mana dorks to cast threats in response to their spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to prevent them from reaching their big spells. Use discard to remove their acceleration or key threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use discard to remove early threats. Hold spot removal for key creatures. Don't overcommit to the board; stabilize and go for the win with your own threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with early threats. Use discard to remove their answers. Don't let them stabilize; keep the board state favorable.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the discard war. Use Thoughtseize/Duress to remove their Green Sun's Zenith or key threats. Prioritize board presence and don't overextend.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve by adding more interaction or removal based on the metagame. If facing more aggro, add more discard and spot removal. If facing control, add graveyard hate and additional threats. Always keep your core acceleration and Zenith package intact.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board without protection. Always hold up mana for interaction when facing interactive decks. Green Sun's Zenith is a one-of or two-of, so don't cast it on an empty board unless you're going for a specific combo. Fungal Bloom + Frenzied Baloth combo requires careful sequencing; ensure you have enough mana and a way to get the combo off.

philosophy: Boros Midrange leverages Ragavan’s card advantage to find Ocelot Pride, generating a resilient token army that outvalues opponents while setting up Phlage, Titan of Fire’s Fury as an inevitable finisher. It wins by transitioning from early digging and tempo plays to overwhelming board presence and late-game inevitability.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2–3 lands (ensuring at least one white and one red source), Ragavan, or early interaction. Mulligan pure land, no Ragavan/Ocelot/Phlage, or unplayable mana bases. In a 3+ land mulligan, prioritize hands with Ragavan or a turn-2 Ocelot Pride setup.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, hold mana for potential disruption or Ragavan. Turn 2: Cast Ragavan (1R), tap for a card draw/spark targeting Ocelot Pride or a cantrip. Turn 3: Cast Ocelot Pride (1W) if available, begin generating 1/1 Cat Warrior tokens. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Ragavan, Ocelot Pride, and ramp to Phlage’s 4RR (or 3RR with a Dragon).
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Boros Energy, Affinity): Bring in board wipes (Cataclysm, Anger of the Gods), spot removal (Lightning Strike, Path to Exile), and life gain. Against Control/Combo (Jeskai Blink, Ruby Storm): Bring in graveyard hate, counterspell protection, and faster threats. Against Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, card advantage engines, and Dragon Fodder for alternate cost setup.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Ragavan as a value engine, not a finisher; avoid over-sparking when ahead. Use Ocelot Pride’s ability to generate blockers for stabilization or attackers for pressure. Manage mana to keep Phlage’s alternate cost open if you control a Dragon, otherwise save for full cost. Track life total carefully due to Ragavan’s life loss.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or blockers ready to handle sweepers. Hold back if facing targeted removal or sweepers, using tokens to trade efficiently. Prioritize board presence over raw card advantage when the opponent is low on answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Amulet Titan’s Thragtusk/Trinisphere, Jeskai Blink’s counters/wipes). Track graveyard for Storm/Ruby combos. Note if the opponent is trying to blink or counter your key pieces, and adjust your attack timing accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Phlage, Titan of Fire’s Fury is the primary win condition, entering with +1/+1 counters and threatening lethal. Ocelot Pride’s token swarm can close games if left unblocked or if you can protect it with removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board dominance, the opponent has no sweepers or spot removal, or you can protect Phlage with counterspells or evasion. Commit to attacking with tokens when the opponent is forced to block, leaving Phlage open.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you face a hard combo that resolves before you can stabilize, or if you’re dead to a specific hate card with no answers. Concede if you’re sweepered with no recovery tools and the opponent has a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Ragavan’s multiple casting steps to dig through counters. Pair cheap threats with protection or discard to disrupt the opponent’s hand. Force counterspells on non-critical spells to open up for Phlage.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to force trades before they stabilize. Use removal on key ramp pieces (e.g., Thran Dynamo, Arcane Signet). Stabilize with Ocelot Pride tokens and sweepers once they reach their power level.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Utilize early interaction and token blockers to survive the initial onslaught. Gain life to offset aggressive damage. Deploy sweepers at the right time to reset the board and transition into your midgame plan.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats to force difficult decisions. Use value engines like Ragavan and Ocelot Pride to out-value control’s removal. Protect Phlage with counterspells, evasion, or timing tricks.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through superior card advantage and sequencing. Use targeted removal to eliminate key threats like Ocelot Pride or Phlage. Out-value with better mana management and Ragavan sparks. Control the board with sweepers when the opponent overcommits.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw value to matchup-specific hate. Against combo, prioritize disruption and faster wins. Against control, add more value and protection. Maintain a balance of removal and sweepers, and adjust your Dragon count if relying on Phlage’s alternate cost.
notes: Phlage’s alternate cost requires controlling a Dragon; include a cheap one like Dragon Fodder if you plan to rely on it. Ocelot Pride makes Cat Warriors, not Dragons, so don’t confuse the two. Avoid overcommitting to Ragavan sparks when ahead, and watch your life total. In multiplayer formats, consider targeting players with the most resources to prevent snowballing.
